name: British Canada
officialName: Provinces of Lower Canada and Upper Canada (British North America)
description: The Constitutional Act of 1791 partitioned the Province of Quebec into Lower Canada and Upper Canada, giving each an appointed governor or lieutenant-governor, an appointed legislative and executive council, and an elected legislative assembly. Lower Canada retained French civil law, seigneurial tenure, and the privileges of the Roman Catholic Church, while Upper Canada was organized under English common law to accommodate the Loyalists who had fled the American Revolution. Effective executive power rested with the British-appointed governor general at Quebec, and the elected assemblies had limited authority over the colonial administration. Persistent friction between the assemblies and the appointed councils fed constitutional grievances that culminated in the Rebellions of 1837–1838 in both provinces. Lord Durham's ensuing report led to the Act of Union of 1840, which merged the two into the single Province of Canada in 1841, ending this constitutional arrangement.
yearDesc: In 1800, Lower Canada and Upper Canada were governed under the framework of the Constitutional Act 1791, each with its own elected assembly and appointed councils. The governor general and administration of British North America sat at Quebec City, while York (present-day Toronto) had served as the capital of Upper Canada since the seat moved there from Newark in 1796. Lower Canada was overwhelmingly French-speaking and Catholic, whereas Upper Canada was a smaller, rapidly growing settler society of Loyalists and later American and British immigrants. Robert Shore Milnes served as lieutenant-governor administering Lower Canada, while Peter Hunter was lieutenant-governor of Upper Canada. Tensions between the elected assemblies and the appointed executive were already emerging, foreshadowing the constitutional conflicts of the following decades.
issues: No corrections needed; verdict confirmed. Every claim independently verified. Existence: Constitutional Act 1791 received royal assent June 1791, effective 26 December 1791, splitting the Province of Quebec into Lower and Upper Canada (Canadian Encyclopedia, Wikipedia) — existedIn1800 "yes" correct. Span 1791–1841 correct: Act of Union passed July 1840, proclaimed/effective 10 February 1841 creating the single Province of Canada (Wikipedia, Canadian Encyclopedia). Capital Quebec City verified at 46.81N, -71.21W (actual ~46.8123, -71.2145) — the record correctly points at Quebec (seat of the governor-in-chief over both provinces) rather than Upper Canada's own capital York; period name "Quebec" correct. yearDesc facts all confirmed: Robert Shore Milnes was lieutenant-governor/administrator of Lower Canada from 1799 and Peter Hunter lieutenant-governor of Upper Canada from 10 April 1799 (Dictionary of Canadian Biography); York became Upper Canada's capital on 1 February 1796, moved from Newark (Wikipedia). Population kept: Bouchette's 1806 figures give Lower Canada ~250,000 and Upper Canada 70,718 (Statistics Canada historical census), matching the honestly-flagged ~300,000 c.1806 anchor — close enough to 1800, retained. Area and GDP correctly dashed: no reliable single 1800 figure exists for this two-province composite. description is exactly 5 sentences with no geography clause and no pop/area/GDP number; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". One minor nuance (not an error): in 1800 the governor-in-chief Robert Prescott had sailed home (July 1799) while retaining the office until 1807, so Milnes administered Lower Canada in practice — the record's general framing of the governor general/administration seated at Quebec remains accurate for the period.

name: Anguilla
yearDesc: In 1800, Anguilla was a small British colony in the Leeward Islands, first settled by English planters from St Kitts in 1650. In 1800, it was governed locally by a council of freeholders while nominal oversight came through Antigua, decades before the unpopular 1825 union that placed it under St Kitts. In 1800, its economy rested on struggling sugar, cotton and provision estates worked by enslaved Africans, hampered by thin soil, low rainfall and frequent drought. In 1800, enslaved people vastly outnumbered the free population, as reflected in the 1819 count of 360 Europeans, 320 free people of colour and 2,451 slaves. In 1800, the scattered settlements around The Valley formed the island's administrative and social centre, the plantation-era Wallblake House of 1787 standing among its earliest surviving structures.

Administration via a local council overseen from Antigua, with the unpopular 1825 legislative union placing it under St Kitts — confirmed by Wikipedia History of Anguilla and Britannica. officialName kept as a descriptive framing ("Anguilla (British Leeward Islands)") since Anguilla had no separate formal colony charter in 1800 — accurate, not fabricated. span 1650–Present confirmed. Capital The Valley confirmed as the island's administrative/social centre; supplied coords (18.22, -63.06) match actual (~18.217, -63.057) within ±0.5°, no change. yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences, all beginning "In 1800,", all facts corroborated: struggling sugar/cotton/provision estates, thin soil/low rainfall/drought, enslaved majority, 1819 census 360 Europeans / 320 free people of colour / 2,451 slaves = 3,131, Wallblake House built 1787 (oldest surviving structure, in The Valley). pop kept: 1819 census total 3,131 verified exactly; ~3,000 is a transparently labeled estimate for 1800. area 91 km² (35 sq mi) confirmed (island extent unchanged). gdp dashed — no reliable ~1800 figure exists for so minor a colony. Sources: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Anguilla, en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anguilla, en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wallblake_House, Britannica Anguilla/History.

name: Bambara Empire
description: The Bambara Empire of Ségou emerged as a centralized state around 1712 when Mamari (Biton) Coulibaly transformed the communal ton age-group associations into a standing military monarchy on the upper Niger. Power rested on the faama (ruler) commanding the tonjon, a warrior caste drawn largely from enslaved and captive soldiers, giving the state an unusually militarized and predatory character sustained by raiding and the slave trade. After Biton's death in 1755 the Coulibaly dynasty collapsed into instability until Ngolo Diarra seized the throne in 1766, founding the Ngolosi (Diarra) dynasty and restoring firm control over Djenné and Timbuktu. His son Mansong (Monzon) Diarra ruled from 1790 to 1808, marking the empire's zenith through relentless campaigns against Kaarta, Massina, the Dogon, and the Mossi. The empire declined after Mansong's death, suffered defeat by Fula jihadists at Noukouma in 1818, and was finally conquered in 1861 by El Hadj Umar Tall's Toucouleur forces.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Bambara Empire of Ségou stood at the height of its power under the faama Mansong (Monzon) Diarra, who had ruled since 1790. Its capital, Ségou, was a prosperous city of roughly 30,000 people straddling both banks of the Niger, whose bustling canoes and cultivated surroundings had impressed the Scottish explorer Mungo Park on his 1796–1797 visit. Mansong was waging successful campaigns to extend Ségou's dominance over neighbouring polities, reportedly founding hundreds of new settlements and welcoming immigrants of varied origins. The state's strength continued to rest on the tonjon warrior caste and on wealth drawn from trade and the capture of slaves, with cowrie shells serving as currency. The empire faced no existential threat at this date, and the Fula jihad and internal decline that would erode it lay years in the future.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified. existedIn1800=yes confirmed (empire at zenith in 1800 under Mansong Diarra, centralized 1712, ended 1861). Span 1712–1861 confirmed (Wikipedia Ségou Empire, New World Encyclopedia); an earlier ~1640 kingdom predates it, but the centralized empire's standard dates are 1712–1861. officialName acceptable (also called Ségou Empire). Capital Ségou confirmed as period-correct name; coordinates -6.27/13.44 fall within multiple sourced values (~13.43–13.45 N, -6.22 to -6.27 W), inside ±0.5°. Rulers/dates verified: Biton Coulibaly founder 1712, d.1755; Ngolo Diarra seized throne 1766 founding Ngolosi/Diarra dynasty; Mansong (Monzon) Diarra r. ~1790–1808 (some sources 1788–1808 / Ngolo d.1787, but 1790 is the most commonly cited and within accepted range). Battle of Noukouma 1818 vs Fula jihadists (Cheikou Amadu) confirmed. Mungo Park 1796–1797 visit confirmed (reached Ségou July 1796). tonjon caste, cowrie currency, ~30,000 Ségou city population all confirmed — note the 30,000 is a city figure (Mungo Park), not a state total, so pop tile correctly dashed. pop/area/gdp all dashed: no reliable 1800 state-level figures exist. description = exactly 5 sentences, political history, no standalone geography clause, no pop/area/GDP numbers. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,".

name: Bahamas
yearDesc: In 1800, the Bahamas were a British colony administered from Nassau on New Providence, having been confirmed as a British possession by the 1783 Peace of Paris. The colony had been transformed by the arrival of roughly two thousand American Loyalists and the people they enslaved between 1783 and about 1789, which nearly tripled the population and shifted its economy toward cotton and sea-island plantation agriculture. These plantations largely failed within a generation as thin island soils were exhausted and pests destroyed the cotton, leaving an enslaved African-descended majority alongside a smaller white settler class and a growing free black community. Lord Dunmore, the former royal governor of Virginia and New York, had served as governor from 1787 until 1796, overseeing fortification of Nassau and much land granting to the newcomers. The islands remained economically marginal within the British Empire, their scattered "Out Islands" thinly settled and their trade dependent on shipping, salt, wrecking, and the produce of struggling estates.
issues: Corrected one factual error and dashed one unsupported figure. (1) yearDesc: Lord Dunmore served as Governor of the Bahamas from 26 Oct 1787 to April 1796, NOT "until 1797" — corrected "until 1797" to "from 1787 until 1796" (confirmed by Wikipedia, Britannica, Encyclopedia Virginia, Statista, and worldstatesmen.org). (2) pop: the asserted colony-wide range of "c. 14,000–17,000" for 1800 is an interpolation with no reliable census behind it, so per the dash-if-shaky rule I dashed the headline value and kept only the two corroborable anchors — the firm 1789 census (~11,300) and Nassau in 1803 (3,861 enslaved + 1,599 white + 752 free black ≈ 6,200). name: Awadh
officialName: Nawab Wazir of Awadh (Nawabate of Awadh)
description: Awadh was founded in 1722 when the Persian-born Shia adventurer Saadat Ali Khan I, styled Burhan-ul-Mulk, was appointed subahdar and turned the office into a hereditary principality under nominal Mughal suzerainty. His successors Safdar Jung and Shuja-ud-Daula expanded the state and secured the Mughal office of wazir, until defeat by the East India Company at Buxar in 1764 forced Awadh into an increasingly subordinate alliance. Asaf-ud-Daula moved the capital from Faizabad to Lucknow in 1775, presiding over a celebrated court culture even as British subsidies and territorial demands mounted. In 1798 the Company deposed Wazir Ali Khan and installed Saadat Ali Khan II, who by the 1801 Treaty of Lucknow ceded roughly half the state's territory to the British in exchange for military protection. The much-reduced state endured as a British-dependent kingdom after its rulers assumed the royal title in 1819, until the Company annexed it in 1856 on the pretext of misgovernment.
yearDesc: In 1800, Awadh was ruled by Nawab Saadat Ali Khan II, who had been placed on the throne in January 1798 by the British East India Company after they deposed his nephew Wazir Ali Khan. His authority rested heavily on Company backing, and he pursued fiscal retrenchment, curbing the extravagant court spending inherited from his predecessors while promoting trade and building projects in and around Lucknow. The state remained one of the wealthiest in India, yet it was already burdened by large annual subsidies owed to the British for the upkeep of a subsidiary garrison. Negotiations were under way that would culminate in the Treaty of Lucknow of November 1801, by which Awadh surrendered about half its territory, including the Doab, Rohilkhand, and Allahabad, to the Company. Thus in 1800 Awadh stood as a rich but shrinking client state, its capital at Lucknow flourishing culturally even as its political independence steadily eroded.
issues: Verdict corrected. POPULATION DASHED: the original "c. 3 million (early 18th-c. estimate)" is not corroborable for ~1800. Per Oudh State (Wikipedia), the "3 million" figure is explicitly an EARLY-18th-century estimate for the original Mughal core subah, BEFORE Awadh's large mid-1700s expansion under Safdar Jung and Shuja-ud-Daula; by 1800 the polity was substantially larger (pre-1801-cession), so applying the ~3M figure to a 1800 info box materially understates the state. A single AI-generated summary suggested ~10 million for the late 18th century, but I could not corroborate that in a reliable source, so per the dash-when-shaky rule the population is set to "—". All other fields CONFIRMED: existedIn1800 "yes" (state 1722–1856; Saadat Ali Khan II reigning since 21 Jan 1798 — no anachronism); span 1722–1856 (founded by Saadat Khan/Burhan-ul-Mulk, annexed by the EIC in 1856); officialName acceptable (the ruler's formal title c. 1800 was Nawab Wazir al-Mamalik, of which "Nawab Wazir of Awadh" is a fair rendering); capital Lucknow (seat since Asaf-ud-Daula's 1775 move, period-correct for 1800) with coordinates 80.9231 / 26.8393 matching modern Lucknow exactly (26.83928 N, 80.92313 E). Description and yearDesc are each exactly 5 sentences with no geography clause or number-grid figure; yearDesc begins "In 1800,". Verified in-prose facts: Saadat Ali Khan II crowned 21 Jan 1798 after the EIC deposed his (half-)nephew Wazir Ali Khan; Treaty of Lucknow signed 10 November 1801 ceding roughly half the territory (Rohilkhand, Lower Doab, Allahabad, Gorakhpur, etc.); royal/King title first assumed by Ghazi-ud-Din Haidar — dated 1819 in the record, which is defensible (sources split between Oct 1818 proclamation and 1819 recognition), so left unchanged. area and gdp already dashed and left dashed (no corroborable ~1800 figures).

name: Batavian Republic
description: The Batavian Republic was proclaimed on 19 January 1795 when French revolutionary armies and Dutch Patriot exiles toppled the stadtholderate of William V, who fled to England, ending the old Republic of the Seven United Netherlands. Established as a French sister republic, it abolished the federal structure of the provinces and, under the unitary constitution of 1798, created a centralized state modelled on the French Directory, with executive power held by a five-member Uitvoerend Bewind. Its politics were turbulent, marked by successive coups d'état between radical unitarist and moderate federalist factions and by financial ruin from war indemnities and the loss of overseas trade. A revised constitution in October 1801 recast it as the Batavian Commonwealth under a twelve-member Staatsbewind, and in 1805 Napoleon imposed rule by a single grand pensionary, Rutger Jan Schimmelpenninck. The republic was dissolved on 5 June 1806 when Napoleon replaced it with the Kingdom of Holland under his brother Louis Bonaparte.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Batavian Republic was governed under the unitary 1798 constitution, with executive authority vested in the five-man Uitvoerend Bewind seated at The Hague and departments run by national agents such as Isaac Gogel at Finance. It remained a client of France, bound by the 1795 Treaty of The Hague to maintain a French garrison, cede territory, and pay a heavy indemnity, and it was drawn into France's war against Britain. The previous year's Anglo-Russian invasion of North Holland had been repelled by Franco-Batavian forces at Bergen and Castricum, and its aftermath included the loss of much of the Dutch fleet at Vlieter. The state was financially strained, its Asian and Atlantic commerce crippled by the British blockade and the mounting crisis of the Dutch East India Company. Napoleon's rise after the coup of 18 Brumaire in France was reshaping relations, foreshadowing the constitutional overhaul that would create the Batavian Commonwealth the following year.
issues: No corrections needed; verdict confirmed. Every claim independently re-verified against Wikipedia (Batavian Republic, Vlieter incident, Treaty of The Hague 1795) and Britannica. Confirmed: proclamation 19 Jan 1795 and dissolution 5 June 1806 (span 1795–1806); "Batavian Republic" is period-correct for 1800 (renamed Batavian Commonwealth/Bataafs Gemenebest only from Oct 1801); 1800 government = unitary 1798 constitution with a five-man Uitvoerend Bewind at The Hague; Isaac Gogel as Agent of Finance; twelve-member Staatsbewind under the 1801 constitution; Schimmelpenninck as grand pensionary 1805; Kingdom of Holland under Louis Bonaparte 1806; 1799 Anglo-Russian invasion repelled at Bergen and Castricum with the Vlieter fleet surrender (30 Aug 1799); Treaty of The Hague 1795 (indemnity of 100M guilders, 25,000-man French garrison, cession of Maastricht, Venlo, Zeelandic/Dutch Flanders); coup of 18 Brumaire (Nov 1799) correctly placed as prior to 1800. Capital The Hague at 4.31E/52.08N is correct (within tolerance). Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or numeric figure; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". Population c. 2,000,000 retained — a reasonable interpolation between sourced endpoints (1,883,009 in 1795 and 2,178,000 in 1806 per Wikipedia). Area dashed (no clean 1800 km² figure sourceable given 1795 cessions) and GDP dashed (only Holland-only Maddison per-capita available, not a whole-republic total) — both left as "—" rather than fabricated.

name: Bahawalpur
description: Bahawalpur was founded in 1748 by Nawab Bahawal Khan I, whose Daudpotra family, claiming Abbasid descent, had migrated from Shikarpur in Sindh and carved out a principality along the Sutlej. The Abbasi nawabs ruled continuously, holding their territory as tributaries of the Durrani Empire until that empire's collapse allowed Muhammad Bahawal Khan II to assert effective independence in the early nineteenth century. The dynasty consolidated power through a network of desert forts, most notably Derawar, which sheltered the ruling house during an Afghan-backed assault on the capital in 1785. In 1833 Bahawalpur entered a subsidiary alliance with the British East India Company, becoming a princely state that supported the British in the First Anglo-Afghan War and the 1848 campaign against Multan. It survived under the Abbasi nawabs until 1947, when it acceded to Pakistan as the first princely state to do so, and was finally merged into West Pakistan in 1955.
yearDesc: In 1800, Bahawalpur was governed by Nawab Muhammad Bahawal Khan II, the third Abbasi ruler, who had reigned since 1772. The state was still formally a tributary of the fading Durrani Empire, and one of the nawab's sons had earlier been held as a hostage by Timur Shah Durrani. Bahawal Khan II was actively strengthening the realm, constructing irrigation canals and adding frontier forts such as Marot, Anupgarh, Walhar, and Wingrot. He was also expanding eastward across the Sutlej, incorporating tracts around Mailsi, Dunyapur, and Pakpattan. Within a few years the disintegration of Durrani authority would let him consolidate Bahawalpur as an effectively autonomous state, though he died in 1809.
issues: No changes; all claims corroborated. Verified against Wikipedia (Bahawalpur princely state / Bahawalpur city), Britannica, Royal Ark / Royal Archives succession, and regional histories. CONFIRMED: existedIn1800 "yes" (founded 1748, ruled in 1800 by Muhammad Bahawal Khan II) — the Durrani-tributary caveat does not negate the polity's existence, so "yes" is correct rather than "partial"; officialName "State of Bahawalpur"; span 1748–1955 (sovereign 1748–1833, British princely state 1833–1947, acceded Pakistan 1947, merged into West Pakistan 14 Oct 1955); capital Bahawalpur city at 71.68E/29.40N (actual ~71.675E/29.398N, well within tolerance). Founder Bahawal Khan I (Daudpotra/Abbasi, ex-Shikarpur) confirmed; Durrani tributary status and ~1802 effective independence confirmed; 1785 Afghan/Durrani assault on the capital with refuge at Derawar confirmed; 1833 British subsidiary alliance, First Anglo-Afghan War + 1847–48 Multan support, and first-to-accede-1947 all confirmed. yearDesc verified: Bahawal Khan II was the THIRD Abbasi ruler reigning 1772–1809 (succession Bahawal Khan I 1746/48–1750 → Mubarak Khan II 1750–1772 → Bahawal Khan II 1772–1809); his son (Nawabzada Mubarak Khan) was held hostage by Timur Shah Durrani; canals + Forts Marot/Anupgarh/Walhar/Wingrot and the eastward Sutlej expansion to Mailsi/Dunyapur/Pakpattan are attributed to his reign; died 1809 — all confirmed. NOTE: one source (travel-culture.com) uses a divergent regnal numbering that labels the 1750–1809 ruler "Mubarak Khan II" and dates "Bahawal Khan II" to 1809–1826, and one govt page (Bahawalnagar) attributed the forts/canals to a "Mubarak Khan"; both reflect garbled/alternate numbering of the SAME person on the throne in 1800 (the ruler who died 1809), and the dominant scholarly convention used by the record (Bahawal Khan II, r. 1772–1809, 3rd Abbasi) is correct — no change needed. description = 5 sentences (political history, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP figures); yearDesc = 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly DASHED: the common 1,341,209 population is a 1941 figure and the 45,911 km2 area is the later fixed princely-state extent, not the still-expanding 1800 territory; no reliable ~1800 figures exist, none fabricated.

name: Anhalt principalities
officialName: The Ascanian Principalities of Anhalt (Anhalt-Dessau, Anhalt-Bernburg and Anhalt-Köthen)
description: The Anhalt lands were held by the House of Ascania, whose Henry I was made a Prince of the Holy Roman Empire in 1218, making Anhalt one of the oldest continuously ruled German princely territories. After Joachim Ernest briefly reunited the lands, his sons partitioned Anhalt in 1603 into the branch principalities of Dessau, Bernburg, Köthen, Zerbst, and Plötzkau, each a sovereign Imperial Estate under its own Ascanian prince. The lines repeatedly extinguished and re-merged, most notably when Anhalt-Zerbst died out in 1793 and was divided among Dessau, Bernburg, and Köthen. In 1806 Napoleon elevated the three surviving principalities to duchies, and they were dissolved as separate states only when the Köthen line failed in 1847/1853 and the Bernburg line in 1863, at which point all Anhalt was reunited under Dessau as the single Duchy of Anhalt. Throughout their history the principalities remained small, closely intermarried Ascanian states, punching above their weight through Enlightenment reform, Protestant patronage, and a tradition of famous soldiers.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Anhalt territory consisted of three independent Ascanian principalities — Anhalt-Dessau, Anhalt-Bernburg, and Anhalt-Köthen — each an Estate of the Holy Roman Empire, the Zerbst line having recently died out and been absorbed. Anhalt-Dessau, the leading and most prosperous branch, was ruled by Leopold III Friedrich Franz ("Prince Franz"), a celebrated enlightened reformer who transformed Dessau into a model Garden Kingdom of parks, schools, and improved agriculture. Anhalt-Bernburg was under Prince Alexius Frederick Christian, and Anhalt-Köthen under Prince August Christian Friedrich, both continuing the dynasty's tradition of small but well-ordered courts. All three shared close family ties, a common Ascanian heritage, and a settled Protestant character, and none had yet been touched by the coming Napoleonic reorganization. Within a few years the collapse of the Holy Roman Empire would sweep them into the Confederation of the Rhine and raise each prince to the rank of duke.

(5-sentence count preserved, still purely political). Rulers all confirmed: Anhalt-Dessau Leopold III Friedrich Franz "Prince Franz" (r. 1751–1807 as prince, Garden Kingdom); Anhalt-Bernburg Alexius Frederick Christian (r. 1796–1834, prince to 1807); Anhalt-Köthen August/Augustus Christian Friedrich/Frederick (r. 1789–1812) — the record's German-spelling "August Christian Friedrich" is a valid variant of English Wikipedia's "Augustus Christian Frederick," kept as-is. name: Bornu Empire
officialName: Bornu Empire (Sultanate of Bornu under the Sayfawa dynasty)
description: The Bornu Empire was the west-of-Lake-Chad successor to the earlier Kanem state, established around 1380 when Mai Umar I and the Sayfawa dynasty relocated their imperial center to Bornu after being driven from Kanem by the Bilala. Ruled by the mais of the long-lived Sayfawa line, it reached its zenith under Mai Idris Alooma in the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries, whose military and administrative reforms, firearms, and consolidation of Islam made Bornu a dominant regional power. From the mid-seventeenth century onward the empire entered a slow decline, weakened by dynastic troubles, drought, defeats such as the 1781 Mandara reverse, and the erosion of its trans-Saharan trade dominance. In the early nineteenth century Fulani forces of Usman dan Fodio's jihad overran the empire and sacked Ngazargamu in 1808, after which the scholar-warrior Muhammad al-Kanemi rose to become the true power in the state while the Sayfawa mais became figureheads. The Sayfawa dynasty finally ended in 1846 when the last mai was killed, and the al-Kanemi shehus supplanted them as rulers of Bornu.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Bornu Empire was ruled by Mai Ahmad Alimi of the Sayfawa dynasty from its fortified capital at Ngazargamu west of Lake Chad. In 1800, the state was a diminished but still-functioning Muslim empire, its authority reaching westward toward the Hausa lands even as its former reach had contracted. In 1800, Bornu was already strained by decades of decline, environmental stress, and the loss of its old commercial primacy on the trans-Saharan routes. In 1800, the rising militancy of the Fulani reform movement to the west posed a growing threat that would erupt into open jihad within a few years. In 1800, the empire retained its Sayfawa monarchy and Islamic character but stood on the eve of the crisis that would cost it Ngazargamu in 1808 and hollow out the dynasty.

name: British Guiana
officialName: Colonies of Essequibo, Demerara and Berbice (under British occupation)
yearDesc: In 1800, the coastal Guiana territory comprised the three separate Dutch colonies of Essequibo, Demerara, and Berbice, held under British military occupation since 1796 during the wars of the French Revolution. Each colony kept its Dutch-derived administration and Court of Policy, with Essequibo and Demerara jointly governed and Berbice run separately, so no unified "British Guiana" yet existed. The administrative and commercial center was Stabroek at the mouth of the Demerara River, a town laid out on the Dutch grid plan that would be renamed Georgetown only in 1812. The economy rested on plantation agriculture worked by enslaved Africans, with Essequibo and Demerara together holding roughly 380 estates producing sugar, cotton, and coffee. Legal sovereignty remained Dutch and disputed, and the colonies would briefly revert to the Batavian Republic under the 1802 Peace of Amiens before permanent British reoccupation in 1803.
issues: No changes; all fields independently corroborated. (1) existedIn1800="partial" verified: the name "British Guiana" and the merger of the three colonies date only to 1831 (Demerara-Essequibo formed 1812, joined Berbice 21 July 1831); in 1800 they were three separate Dutch colonies under British occupation — the anchor label "British Guiana" is anachronistic for the map year. (2) span "1796–1802" correct: British occupation from 1796 until the Peace of Amiens returned the colonies to the Batavian Republic (1802–1803) before British reoccupation in 1803; formal cession 1814–1815. (3) Capital Stabroek is the correct 1800 name (renamed Georgetown 5 May 1812); coords -58.16/6.8 match Georgetown (6.804, -58.155), well within ±0.5°. (4) yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences, opens "In 1800,"; administrative structure (Essequibo+Demerara jointly governed, Berbice separate) confirmed via History of Guyana; ~380 estates for Demerara+Essequibo by 1800 corroborated (Grokipedia/Demerara-Essequibo sources). (5) description="" per reuseExisting=true. (6) pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable single ~1800 total for the three combined colonies. Verdict: confirmed.

name: Cape Colony
officialName: Colony of the Cape of Good Hope (under British military occupation)
yearDesc: In 1800, the Cape of Good Hope was a British-occupied colony, seized from the Dutch East India Company in 1795 to secure the sea route to India during the French Revolutionary Wars. It was administered as a military possession, with Sir George Yonge serving as governor over a population of former Company officials, Boer farmers, slaves, and Khoikhoi under British authority. Cape Town remained the administrative and commercial hub, its garrison and harbour guarding the strategic passage around Africa. Frontier tensions persisted to the east, where colonists, the Xhosa, and the Khoikhoi contested land and cattle along the unstable eastern districts. The occupation proved temporary, for under the 1802 Treaty of Amiens Britain agreed to restore the Cape to the Batavian Republic, which took control in 1803.
issues:
CORRECTED the governor in yearDesc: the record named "Francis Dundas serving as acting governor" in 1800, but during the year 1800 the governor was Sir George Yonge, who arrived 9 Dec 1799 and served until his recall; Francis Dundas's acting terms were 21 Nov 1798–9 Dec 1799 and 20 Apr 1801–20 Feb 1803, so he was NOT governor in 1800 (source: Wikipedia, Francis Dundas). Replaced the Dundas clause with "Sir George Yonge serving as governor"; kept the remaining four sentences (still exactly 5, begins "In 1800,").

CONFIRMED (unchanged): existedIn1800 "yes" — first British occupation 1795–1803 covers 1800 (Dutch surrender Sept 1795; handover to Batavian Republic Jan–Feb 1803 under Treaty of Amiens). span "1795 – 1803" accurate. officialName acceptable (administered as a British military possession). capital Cape Town correct city and 1800 name; lon/lat 18.4241/-33.9249 on Cape Town. pop "~62,000 (1797 census)" corroborated — Wikipedia's Dutch Cape Colony 1797 census table sums to 61,947, and the confidence-note breakdown (21,746 free Christians, 25,754 slaves, 14,447 Khoikhoi) matches the table's column sums exactly. area "~145,000 km²" confirmed as the colony's greatest extent c.1795 (~56,000 sq mi). gdp "—" correctly dashed (no reliable Maddison figure for this sub-national colony). description "" per reuseExisting=true.

name: British Ceylon
description: British forces took the Dutch-held maritime provinces of Ceylon in a campaign of 1795–96, receiving the surrender of Colombo on 15 February 1796. The captured coastal belt was at first governed from Madras by the East India Company, whose fiscal exactions provoked a serious revolt in 1797. From 1798 the territory passed to a dual arrangement in which Frederick North served as the first British governor while the Company retained control of commercial affairs, and in 1802 the Treaty of Amiens confirmed British possession and made Ceylon a distinct crown colony. British rule was long confined to the maritime lowlands, as the highland Kingdom of Kandy preserved its independence until its annexation in 1815 unified the whole island under the Crown. British Ceylon endured as a colony until the island gained independence as the Dominion of Ceylon in 1948.
yearDesc: In 1800, British Ceylon consisted only of the former Dutch maritime provinces along the coast, with the interior Kingdom of Kandy remaining an independent native state. The colony was administered under the dual East India Company and Crown system established in 1798, with Frederick North governing from Colombo. North was pursuing early civil reforms, reorganising revenue collection and curbing the abuses that had sparked the 1797 rising against Company rule. The strategic value of the harbour at Trincomalee during the wars with France had convinced Britain to make its hold permanent rather than temporary. Full crown-colony status and the confirmation of British title by the Treaty of Amiens still lay two years ahead.
issues: CORRECTED two items. (1) Description date error: original said the Dutch surrender at Colombo was "5 March 1796"; multiple sources (morethannelson, eyesrilanka, Wikipedia Invasion of Ceylon) confirm Colombo capitulated on 15 February 1796 (British occupied 16 Feb). "5 March 1796" is the Wikipedia infobox date for the conclusion of the whole Dutch-Ceylon campaign, not the Colombo surrender — changed to "15 February 1796". Minor wording tweak in sentence 3: "commercial interests" → "control of commercial affairs" to match the confirmed dual-control arrangement (governor held law/order; EIC controlled finance/commerce, 1798–1802). (2) officialName corrected: the record's "British Settlements on the Island of Ceylon (Maritime Provinces of Ceylon)" is not a documented 1800 legal title — Wikipedia lists the 1796–1802 formal name simply as "Ceylon" (an EIC chartered territory), with "British Settlements and Territories in the Island of Ceylon with its Dependencies" only from 1802. Replaced with the defensible descriptive rendering "Ceylon (British-held Maritime Provinces)". CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800=yes (Britain held maritime provinces from Feb 1796; EIC/Crown dual control in 1800; crown colony from 1802 — Kandy independent until 1815); span 1796–1948 (independence as Dominion of Ceylon, 4 Feb 1948); capital Colombo at 79.85E/6.93N (accurate, within tolerance); Frederick North first governor 1798–1805; 1797 revolt real; Treaty of Amiens 1802; Kandy annexed 1815 (Kandyan Convention, 2 Mar 1815). pop c.800,000 kept as an indicative scholarly estimate (corroborated as the island/maritime-province population near conquest) with "(est.)" retained; area and gdp remain dashed — not reliably sourceable for a sub-island territory in 1800. Description remains exactly 5 sentences (no geography clause, no numeric grid figures); yearDesc exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," (Trincomalee mention is a strategic/political point, not a terrain clause).

name: British West Indies
description: The British West Indies grew from seventeenth-century settlement and conquest, as England seized islands such as Barbados, the Leewards, and Jamaica (taken from Spain in 1655) and built them into wealthy sugar-and-slave plantation colonies. Each island was administered as a distinct Crown colony or proprietary colony, typically with its own governor, appointed council, and elected assembly of white planters, rather than as a single federated territory. Their fortunes were shaped by the transatlantic slave trade and by repeated Anglo-French and Anglo-Spanish wars, which passed islands like Grenada, Dominica, and St. Vincent back and forth before Britain secured them by the treaties of 1763 and 1783. The abolition of the British slave trade in 1807 and the emancipation of the enslaved in 1833–1838 undercut the plantation economy and reshaped colonial society. The colonies persisted under varied administrative unions into the twentieth century, briefly forming the West Indies Federation in 1958 before it dissolved and the territories moved toward independence in the 1960s.
yearDesc: In 1800, the British West Indies were among the most valuable possessions of the British Empire, their economies driven by sugar, coffee, and enslaved African labour. Jamaica was by far the largest and richest colony, governed from Spanish Town, where the governor, assembly, and courts sat. Britain was at war with Revolutionary France and Spain, and had recently occupied Trinidad in 1797, though it was not yet formally British. Planter assemblies dominated island politics, while a rising abolitionist movement in Britain increasingly challenged the slave system that underpinned the colonies' wealth. The enslaved population vastly outnumbered the white minority, and fear of revolt had intensified after the ongoing revolution in neighbouring French Saint-Domingue.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified. EXISTENCE: Confirmed — in 1800 Britain held Jamaica, Barbados, the Leewards, the ceded Windwards (Grenada, St. Vincent, Dominica, ceded by France under the Treaty of Paris 1763), and the Bahamas as a grouping of separately administered colonies; existedNote's Trinidad (captured 1797, ceded by Spain under Treaty of Amiens 1802) and Tobago/St. Lucia (permanently British under Treaty of Paris 1814) caveats are accurate. CAPITAL: Spanish Town verified as Jamaica's colonial capital and seat of government (King's House, Assembly, courts) 1655–1872; actual coords ~17.991°N, -76.957°W vs. record's 17.9959, -76.9551 — offset ~0.005° in lat and ~0.002° in lon, well within ±0.5°, left unchanged. DESCRIPTION: Exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no numeric figures; Jamaica taken from Spain 1655, treaties 1763/1783, slave trade abolition 1807, emancipation 1833–1838, Federation 1958 all confirmed. YEARDESC: Exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,"; war with France (from 1793) and Spain (from 1796), Trinidad occupied 1797, ongoing Saint-Domingue revolution all confirmed. POP: kept as a dashed range — corroborated (enslaved British Caribbean ~591,000 in 1800, rising to ~700,000 by 1807/1808, plus whites and free people of colour). AREA/GDP: correctly dashed (no reliable single figure for the multi-colony grouping; no Maddison series for "British West Indies" as a unit).

name: Cisalpine Republic
description: The Cisalpine Republic was proclaimed by Napoleon Bonaparte on 29 June 1797, formed by merging the Transpadane and Cispadane republics he had established in northern Italy, and it was governed under a French-modelled constitution with a five-member Directory and legislative councils. It served as a French sister-republic, its politics dominated by Paris even as local figures such as Giovanni Galeazzo Serbelloni and Francesco Melzi d'Eril held office. After the French defeat at Cassano in April 1799 the republic was overrun and occupied by Austrian and Russian forces under an imperial commission. As French forces reoccupied Lombardy in the Marengo campaign, Napoleon restored the republic on 4 June 1800, reorganising its government first under an extraordinary commission and Consulta and then a narrow Committee of Government. In January 1802 an Extraordinary Consulta meeting at Lyon renamed the state the Italian Republic with Napoleon as its president, ending the Cisalpine Republic.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Cisalpine Republic was re-established by Napoleon on 4 June, two days after his entry into Milan and ten days before his decisive victory over Austria at the Battle of Marengo on 14 June. In 1800, the Austro-Russian occupation that had crushed the republic in 1799 collapsed as allied forces withdrew and French control over Lombardy and Emilia was reasserted. In 1800, Napoleon reconstituted its administration through an extraordinary Commission of Government of nine members and a legislative Consulta established on 17 June. In 1800, executive power was later concentrated in a restricted Committee of Government of three members, Giovan Battista Sommariva, Sigismondo Ruga, and Francesco Visconti, with Milan again its capital. In 1800, it functioned as a firmly dependent French client state, its restored institutions setting the stage for its transformation into the Italian Republic under Napoleon's presidency in early 1802.
issues: CORRECTED the Marengo date chain, which was factually wrong in three fields. The record repeatedly gave the Battle of Marengo as 2 June 1800 and framed the 4 June restoration as coming "two days after" / "following" Marengo. Verified against Wikipedia, Britannica, napoleon.org and multiple sources: (a) the Battle of Marengo was fought 14 June 1800, not 2 June; (b) 2 June 1800 was the date Napoleon ENTERED MILAN; (c) the republic was restored 4 June 1800 (15 Prairial year VIII), which was two days after the Milan entry and TEN DAYS BEFORE Marengo — so the restoration PRECEDED the battle rather than following it. Fixed in the anchor/description (was "Following his victory at Marengo, Napoleon restored the republic on 4 June 1800", now framed as during the Marengo campaign as French forces reoccupied Lombardy) and in yearDesc sentence 1 (was "re-established by Napoleon on 4 June, only two days after his decisive victory over Austria at the Battle of Marengo"). Description kept at exactly 5 sentences; yearDesc kept at exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,". CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800=yes; officialName; span 1797–1802 (founded 29 June 1797, renamed Italian Republic Jan 1802); capital Milan at 9.19E/45.46N (city center); population 3,240,000 (Wikipedia; 3,239,572 recorded Nov 1797); area 42,500 km² (Wikipedia infobox, 1797); GDP dashed (no reliable series for this short-lived polity); the 1800 government bodies — nine-member extraordinary Commission, legislative Consulta of 17 June, three-member Committee of Government (Sommariva, Ruga, Visconti) — all verified. Note: pop/area are 1797 figures used as best-available proxy for ~1800; territory shifted slightly at the 1800 restoration, so treat as approximate.

name: Denmark–Norway
officialName: The Realms and Lands of the King of Denmark, Norway, the Wends and the Goths (the Oldenburg Monarchy)
description: Denmark–Norway was a multinational, absolutist dual monarchy ruled by the House of Oldenburg, formed as a personal union of the two kingdoms after the collapse of the Kalmar Union in the 1520s and consolidated as an integrated hereditary state from 1537. Political and economic power was centralized in Copenhagen, where the crown governed Denmark, Norway, the duchies of Schleswig and Holstein, the North Atlantic dependencies of Iceland, Greenland and the Faroe Islands, and overseas trading colonies in the Caribbean, West Africa and India. The monarchy became an absolute one under Frederick III in 1660–1661, a system codified in the Kongelov, and it repeatedly contested Sweden for supremacy in the Baltic across the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. From 1784 Crown Prince Frederick governed as regent for his mentally incapacitated father Christian VII, presiding over a period of enlightened reform including the abolition of adscription serfdom for Danish peasants. The union was dissolved after the Napoleonic Wars, when a defeated Frederick VI ceded Norway to the Swedish crown by the Treaty of Kiel in January 1814, though Denmark retained Norway's former Atlantic dependencies.
yearDesc: In 1800, Denmark–Norway was nominally ruled by the incapacitated King Christian VII while Crown Prince Frederick exercised effective royal authority as regent from Copenhagen. That year the monarchy joined Russia, Sweden and Prussia in the Second League of Armed Neutrality, asserting the right of neutral shipping to trade freely during the French Revolutionary Wars. Britain regarded the League as a hostile pro-French alignment, setting the stage for direct conflict with the Royal Navy. The state remained an absolutist composite realm binding Denmark, Norway, Schleswig-Holstein and its Atlantic and tropical colonies under the Oldenburg crown. Within months this diplomatic stance would lead to the British attack on the Danish fleet at the First Battle of Copenhagen in April 1801.
issues: All claims independently re-checked and confirmed; no corrections needed. existedIn1800 "yes" verified (dual monarchy intact until 1814 Treaty of Kiel). officialName corroborated by Wikipedia's royal style "King of Denmark and Norway, the Wends and the Goths" and the "Oldenburg Monarchy" alternate name. Capital Copenhagen and coordinates (55.68°N, 12.57°E) correct within tolerance. Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no numeric figures (sentence 2's territory list is political composition, not a physical-geography clause). yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," and every claim verified (Christian VII incapacitated; Frederick regent from 1784; Second League of Armed Neutrality joined Dec 1800 with Russia/Sweden/Prussia; British hostility; First Battle of Copenhagen April 1801). pop "c. 1.9 million" corroborated by Wikipedia (~1,859,000: ~929k Denmark + ~883k Norway + ~47k Iceland) and 1801 censuses (~926k + ~879k); kept. area and gdp correctly dashed — not reliably sourceable for the 1800 composite state. JUDGMENT CALL retained, not corrected: span start year 1524 (union re-established after Kalmar Union) vs. the 1537 integrated-state date used in Wikipedia's infobox — both are standard in the literature and the existedNote already flags the discrepancy, so 1524–1814 is left as a defensible choice.

name: Bundelkhand
officialName: Bundelkhand (Bundela Rajput states under Maratha suzerainty)
description: Bundelkhand took its name from the Bundela Rajputs, who rose to prominence in the 16th century when Rudra Pratap Singh established Orchha as the parent Bundela kingdom, from which Datia and Panna later split off. The Bundelas repeatedly resisted Mughal authority, most famously under Chhatrasal of Panna, who after 1729 invited Peshwa Baji Rao I to aid him against Muhammad Khan Bangash and rewarded the Marathas with lands and diamond mines, opening the region to Maratha power. Through the 18th century the Bundela states fragmented into numerous small chieftaincies while the Marathas exacted tribute and eventually placed the tract under a subahdar, Ali Bahadur, grandson of Baji Rao I, who consolidated much of it as Nawab of Banda. The region thus became a mosaic of Bundela Rajput principalities and Maratha-administered districts, chronically divided and heavily burdened by tribute demands. Maratha control unravelled after the Treaty of Bassein (1802) and the Anglo-Maratha wars, and following the Third Anglo-Maratha War of 1818 the British absorbed the whole tract, reorganising it as the Bundelkhand Agency.
yearDesc: In 1800, Bundelkhand was not a single state but a fragmented patchwork of Bundela Rajput chiefs and Maratha-held territory in central India. In 1800, the Maratha administration of the region rested with Ali Bahadur, Nawab of Banda and the Peshwa's subahdar, who had extended his authority over large parts of Bundelkhand. In 1800, numerous Bundela principalities — including Orchha, Datia, Panna, Ajaigarh, Bijawar, Charkhari and Chhatarpur — persisted as tributary or semi-autonomous chieftaincies amid this Maratha overlordship. In 1800, Ali Bahadur was campaigning to complete his control of the region and was besieging the great fortress of Kalinjar, before which he would die in August 1802. In 1800, British power had not yet entered Bundelkhand, but the Maratha cession under the 1802 Treaty of Bassein and the subsequent Anglo-Maratha wars would soon bring the region under East India Company control.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently confirmed against sources. existedIn1800 "partial" is correct — Bundelkhand in 1800 was a region/patchwork of Bundela Rajput chieftaincies (Orchha, Datia, Panna and offshoots) plus Maratha-held territory under Ali Bahadur, not a unified state. Span 1531–1818 verified: Orchha State founded 1531 by Rudra Pratap Singh (Bundela parent kingdom); British absorbed the whole tract after the Third Anglo-Maratha War of 1818. Political history all confirmed: Chhatrasal of Panna, the 1729 Battle of Jaitpur / alliance with Peshwa Baji Rao I against Muhammad Khan Bangash, the reward of lands and diamond mines, Ali Bahadur (grandson of Baji Rao I) as Nawab of Banda / Maratha subahdar, his death besieging Kalinjar in August 1802, Treaty of Bassein (31 Dec 1802), and the Bundelkhand Agency. Capital Banda is a sound 1800 administrative proxy (Ali Bahadur's Maratha seat; later the Agency HQ) since no single Bundelkhand capital existed; coords 25.48N/80.33E match Banda's real location (25.483, 80.333) essentially exactly. Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause and no pop/area/GDP figures; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable 1800 figures exist for this fragmented multi-state region. Minor non-blocking nuance: the Bundelkhand Agency was first organised c. 1811 (HQ Banda), so "reorganising it as the Bundelkhand Agency" after 1818 slightly compresses the timeline, but the description's phrasing about the post-1818 absorption is broadly accurate and not worth changing.

name: Barotseland
description: The Lozi kingdom, known to its people as Bulozi and later as Barotseland, was founded in the seventeenth century by the Aluyi (Luyana) after a migration associated with the Lunda world, with Mboo Mwanasilundu Muyunda remembered as the first Litunga (king). It developed into a highly centralized, aristocratic monarchy in which the Litunga ruled through a bureaucracy of appointed councillors, headed by the Ngambela (chief minister), and a national council or kuta, with a distinctive dual-capital system dividing power between a senior northern seat and a junior southern one at Nalolo. Under successive rulers such as Ngalama, Ngombala and Yeta the kingdom expanded across the floodplain, subordinating and integrating neighbouring peoples, and it reached its classical form under the tenth Litunga, Mulambwa Santulu, celebrated as a great lawgiver who codified property, marriage and military custom and opposed the slave trade. Mulambwa's death around 1830 triggered a civil war between his sons Silumelume and Mubukwanu, which fatally weakened the state and left it vulnerable to invasion. Within a few years the Kololo under Sebitwane conquered Barotseland and ruled it until 1864, when a Lozi rebellion restored the indigenous dynasty, whose later kings culminated in Lewanika and eventual absorption into the British sphere.
yearDesc: In 1800, Barotseland was a strong, expanding kingdom on the upper Zambezi under the tenth Litunga, Mulambwa Santulu, widely remembered as its greatest ruler. In 1800, royal authority rested on control of the annual flood, cattle and cultivation, exercised through a hierarchy of indunas and the kuta council rather than the king alone. In 1800, Mulambwa was consolidating power and issuing the legal reforms that earned him the epithet "creator of laws," while enforcing a firm hostility to slave-raiding that kept slave traders out of his territory. In 1800, the kingdom was absorbing incoming groups, most notably Mbunda migrants fleeing conflict and the slave trade to the west, who were welcomed as allies and settlers. In 1800, no European power had yet reached the floodplain, and the succession dispute and Kololo conquest that would later shatter the kingdom still lay decades in the future.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently corroborated against Wikipedia (Lozi kingdom, Mulambwa Santulu) and supporting sources. Verified: (1) existedIn1800 "yes" — Lozi/Aluyi kingdom well established by 1800, Mulambwa Santulu 10th Litunga reigning c. 1780–1830. (2) Founding in 17th c. by Aluyi (Luyana), first Litunga Mboo Mwanasilundu Muyunda, Lunda-associated migration — confirmed. (3) Centralized monarchy with Ngambela + kuta + dual capital (northern Namuso / southern Nalolo/Lwambi) — confirmed. (4) Expansion under Ngalama (4th), Ngombala (6th, established southern subordinate centre + tribute system), Yeta — confirmed. (5) Mulambwa as lawgiver ("Mutomi Wa Mulao"/creator of laws), anti-slave-trade stance, welcome of Mbunda refugees fleeing Angola slave trade — confirmed. (6) Death c. 1830 at Lilundu, civil war between sons Silumelume and Mubukwanu — confirmed. (7) Kololo conquest under Sebitwane (~1840), restoration 1864, later Lewanika — confirmed. Capital: coordinates -15.22/23.02 confirmed as Lealui (-15.225, 23.023) on the northern floodplain royal-capital site; the record's own confidence note correctly flags that the NAME "Lealui" is anachronistic for 1800 (permanent Lealui dates to Sipopa/Lewanika 1860s–1878; in 1800 the northern royal seat was "Namuso" with shifting per-king capitals) — coords are period-appropriate, name-flag retained rather than corrected. span end 1830 = end of the pre-Kololo indigenous iteration (Mulambwa's death → civil war → conquest), a defensible boundary. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist (the ~390,000 km² figure is late-19th-c. peak extent, not 1800). description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or numeric figures; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,". Verdict: confirmed.

name: Cyrenaica
officialName: Cyrenaica (eastern territory of the Regency of Tripoli / Eyalet of Tripolitania under the Karamanli dynasty)
description: Cyrenaica formed the eastern portion of the Ottoman Regency of Tripoli, which from 1711 was governed as a hereditary de facto monarchy by the Karamanli dynasty while still paying nominal tribute to the Ottoman sultan. Real authority over the region was exercised from Tripoli rather than locally, and the Bedouin tribes of the interior retained wide autonomy under only light central control. Under Yusuf Karamanli, who ruled the regency from 1795, the dynasty tightened its grip, and by 1807 he had forced the tribes of Cyrenaica and the Fezzan to submit to his authority. In 1835 the Ottoman government of Sultan Mahmud II exploited local disorder to depose the Karamanlis and reassert direct imperial rule, ending the dynasty's control of the region. Later in the nineteenth century Cyrenaica became a separate Ottoman administrative unit centered on Benghazi and the seat of the influential Sanusi religious order.
yearDesc: In 1800, Cyrenaica was the eastern territory of the Karamanli-ruled Regency of Tripoli, administered from Tripoli rather than as a self-governing province. Its principal town and port was Benghazi, a small and impoverished coastal settlement whose silted harbor limited its commercial importance. The vast interior steppe and desert were the domain of largely autonomous Bedouin tribes, over whom Ottoman-Karamanli authority remained nominal and loosely enforced. The reigning pasha, Yusuf Karamanli, had held power since 1795 and would over the following years compel the region's tribes to acknowledge his overlordship. The Sanusi order that would later dominate Cyrenaican political and religious life had not yet been founded, so the region in 1800 remained a peripheral, thinly governed frontier of the Tripoli regency.
issues: No corrections needed; verdict confirmed. All claims independently verified against Wikipedia (Ottoman Tripolitania, Karamanli dynasty, History of Benghazi, Senusiyya) and search corroboration: (1) Karamanli dynasty ruled the Regency of Tripoli 1711–1835 as a de facto hereditary monarchy under nominal Ottoman suzerainty, with Cyrenaica its eastern part governed from Tripoli — confirmed. (2) Yusuf Karamanli reigned from 1795 and by 1807 forced the tribes of Cyrenaica and Fezzan to submit — confirmed. (3) 1835 Sultan Mahmud II deposed the Karamanlis and reasserted direct Ottoman rule — confirmed. (4) Cyrenaica became a separate sanjak centered on Benghazi later (1863) with the Sanusi order — confirmed; Sanusi order founded 1837 (Mecca), so correctly "not yet founded" in 1800. (5) Benghazi coordinates 32.12N/20.07E accurate (actual ~32.115N/20.067E, within tolerance); "Benghazi" was the period name; impoverished silted-harbor town of ~5,000 corroborated. existedIn1800 "partial" is the honest call — Cyrenaica existed as a region but not a distinct self-governing polity in 1800 (became a distinct Ottoman unit only after 1835/1863). description = exactly 5 sentences of political history, no geography clause, no numeric figures; yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable region-specific ~1800 figures exist for Cyrenaica as a unit (it was not a distinct polity; Maddison has no separate estimate); dashing is the correct adversarial call rather than fabricating a total.

name: Carnatic
description: The Nawabate of the Carnatic emerged as a Mughal governorship of the Deccan, becoming a distinct hereditary Muslim principality under Saadatullah Khan I from about 1710 while nominally subordinate to the Nizam of Hyderabad. It rose to prominence under Muhammad Ali Khan Wallajah, who reigned from 1749 to 1795 and made the Nawabate a close ally of the British East India Company, drawing on Company troops to prevail over French-backed rivals in the Carnatic Wars. Wallajah's growing debts to the Company and its private creditors progressively mortgaged the state's revenues and eroded its independence, leaving successive Nawabs financially and militarily dependent on the British. His son Umdat ul-Umara, who succeeded in 1795, resisted Company demands and was accused of secret dealings with Tipu Sultan, giving the British a pretext to seize control after Tipu's defeat in 1799. Following Umdat ul-Umara's death in 1801, his nephew Azim-ud-Daula signed the Carnatic Treaty ceding civil and revenue administration to the Company, reducing the Nawabs to titular pensioners until the line was abolished under the Doctrine of Lapse in 1855.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Carnatic was ruled by Nawab Umdat ul-Umara, who had succeeded his father Muhammad Ali Khan Wallajah in 1795 and held court at Chepauk Palace in Madras. The Nawabate was by then only nominally independent, its finances crippled by enormous debts to the East India Company and its private creditors. Fresh from destroying Tipu Sultan of Mysore in 1799, the Company accused Umdat ul-Umara of having secretly aided Tipu and demanded that he surrender the administration of his territories as indemnity. Umdat ul-Umara resisted these pressures throughout 1800, but the Company was already moving to absorb the state's revenues and reduce the Nawab to a figurehead. His death in July 1801 opened the way for the Carnatic Treaty of that year, which formally transferred civil government to the British.
issues: No changes; all claims independently verified and confirmed. Rulers/dates checked against Wikipedia (Carnatic Sultanate, Umdat ul-Umara, Muhammad Ali Khan Wallajah, Azim-ud-Daula, Carnatic Treaty): Umdat ul-Umara reigned 1795–1801 (died 15 July 1801, possibly poisoned by the Company); nephew Azim-ud-Daula succeeded and signed the Carnatic Treaty on 26 July 1801 at Chepauk Palace with Arthur Wellesley — so existedIn1800 "yes" and the "not yet annexed in 1800" anachronism caveat are both correct (treaty postdates 1 January 1800 and postdates Umdat ul-Umara's death). Foundation ~1710 (Saadatullah Khan I moved court from Gingee/Arcot) confirmed; capital moved Arcot→Chepauk (Wikipedia: Chepauk 1768–1855) confirmed, so "Chepauk (Madras)" period-correct for 1800. Coordinates 80.28E/13.06N verified against Chepauk/Triplicane, Chennai (~13.064N, 80.284E) — within tolerance, no fix. Doctrine of Lapse abolition of the line in 1855 (13th Nawab Ghulam Muhammad Ghouse Khan) confirmed. description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or pop/area/GDP figure; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures sourceable for this specific polity.

name: Dominica
yearDesc: In 1800, Dominica was a British colony that France had ceded in the 1763 Treaty of Paris, though its population and planter class remained heavily Francophone. In 1800, the island was administered as a separate Crown colony governed from Roseau, with a British-appointed administration and a legislative assembly that represented only the white settler minority. In 1800, its economy rested on plantation agriculture, especially coffee and sugar, worked by an enslaved African majority who vastly outnumbered the European inhabitants. In 1800, enslaved Africans continued to be landed on the island as part of a heavy wave of arrivals in the years before Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807. In 1800, Dominica still faced the threat of French attack, and Roseau would be raided and largely burned by a French naval force in 1805.
issues: CORRECTED the existedIn1800 note: the original claimed Dominica in 1800 "was administered within the Leeward Islands group at this date... not yet transferred to the Windward Islands administration (that reclassification came in the 19th century)" — factually wrong on two counts. (1) Dominica was made a SEPARATE Crown colony in 1771 (Britannica / Wikipedia History of Dominica), so in 1800 it was a standalone colony, NOT part of the Leewards (only rejoined to the Leewards in 1871/1883). (2) The transfer to the Windward Islands administration came in 1940, NOT the 19th century. The corrected framing: in 1800 Dominica was a separate British Crown colony (separated 1771) with a British-appointed administration, its own legislative assembly, and capital at Roseau; the anchor's "Windward Islands" label reflects the modern geographic grouping, not an 1800 administrative unit. name: Duchy of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel
description: The principality emerged in 1269 from a partition of the lands of the House of Welf and was ruled continuously by cadet branches of that dynasty. Its seat lay at Wolfenbüttel from 1432 until 1753, when Duke Charles I moved the court back to the larger city of Brunswick. As a princely state of the Holy Roman Empire within the Lower Saxon Circle, it retained sovereignty while its rulers cultivated ties to Prussia and to Hanover. Charles William Ferdinand, duke from 1780, was a celebrated Prussian field marshal whose defeat and mortal wounding at Jena-Auerstedt in 1806 exposed the small state to French power. Napoleon abolished it in 1807, folding its territory into the Kingdom of Westphalia until it was restored in 1815 as the Duchy of Brunswick.
yearDesc: In 1800, the principality was ruled by Duke Charles William Ferdinand, a cultured "enlightened despot" and renowned soldier married to a sister of Britain's George III. Its capital and court were at Brunswick, where the ruling family resided in the ducal palace. The state remained a sovereign member of the Holy Roman Empire, maintaining close dynastic and military links to Prussia. It stood at peace after the earlier campaigns of the French Revolutionary Wars, in which the duke had commanded allied armies. Within a few years this stability would collapse, as the duke's death and Napoleon's conquest ended the principality's independent existence.

Span 1269–1807 accepted: founding by the 1269 partition of Brunswick-Lüneburg is correct, and 1807 correctly marks Napoleon's abolition (annexed into the Kingdom of Westphalia) ending the principality's independent existence before the separate 1815 Duchy of Brunswick; note the Wikipedia principality article gives 1269–1815 spanning to the restoration. Capital Brunswick (Braunschweig) is period-correct for 1800 — the residence returned from Wolfenbüttel to the newly built Brunswick Palace in 1753 during Charles I's reign (Charles I reigned 1735–1780; his father Ferdinand Albert II died in 1735, so the "Duke Charles I moved the court" attribution is correct, contra one stray source that credited his father). name: Duchy of Holstein
description: The Duchy of Holstein was created on 14 February 1474 when Emperor Frederick III elevated Christian I of Denmark's County of Holstein-Rendsburg to ducal rank, and it was ruled throughout its existence by the Danish House of Oldenburg jointly with the neighbouring Duchy of Schleswig. Uniquely, it lay within the Holy Roman Empire while its dukes were also Kings of Denmark, making it a bridge between the German and Danish worlds. From 1544 the duchy was repeatedly partitioned between the royal (Glückstadt) line and the rival Holstein-Gottorp line, a fragmentation that fuelled conflict with Denmark for nearly two centuries. This division ended in 1773 when the Gottorp claimant, Grand Duke Paul of Russia, ceded his Holstein lands to the Danish crown in exchange for the County of Oldenburg, reuniting Holstein under sole Danish royal authority. After the Holy Roman Empire dissolved in 1806 the duchy briefly became sovereign, then joined the German Confederation in 1815, and it was ultimately severed from Denmark and absorbed into Prussia following the wars of 1864 and 1866.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Duchy of Holstein was a Holy Roman Empire fief governed by King Christian VII of Denmark as its duke, with the crown prince and future Frederick VI acting as effective regent. It had been a unified single state since the 1773 reunification, its administration and highest court seated at the capital, Glückstadt, while Kiel remained an important town and university seat. The duchy formed the southern, German-speaking half of the Danish composite monarchy, sharing its ruler and much of its administration with the Duchy of Schleswig to the north. Denmark's policy of armed neutrality during the Napoleonic-era wars shaped Holstein's affairs, and the Second League of Armed Neutrality that year drew Danish territories toward confrontation with Britain. Its status as an Imperial fief would end only a few years later, in 1806, when the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire left the duchy sovereign under the Danish king.

Verified: (1) existedIn1800 = yes — Holstein was a Danish-held HRE fief in 1800, reunited since 1773, retaining Imperial status until 1806. (2) officialName correct. (6) yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning 'In 1800,'; Christian VII as duke with Frederick VI as crown-prince regent (regent since 1784) confirmed; the Second League of Armed Neutrality's founding convention was signed 16 December 1800 (Denmark acceding that year, formalized into 1801), so 'that year' is accurate. name: Duchy of Massa and Carrara
officialName: Duchy of Massa and Principality of Carrara
description: The Duchy of Massa and Principality of Carrara was a small Italian state formed in 1473 when the lord of Massa acquired the lordship of Carrara, uniting the two towns under one rule. It was governed first by the Malaspina family and, after 1520, by the Cybo-Malaspina dynasty, which elevated the domain to a duchy and principality and enriched it through the region's marble quarries. The line passed to Maria Teresa Cybo-Malaspina and then, in 1790, to her daughter Maria Beatrice d'Este, the last hereditary sovereign, whose marriage tied the state to the House of Austria-Este. French Revolutionary armies deprived the dynasty of the territory in 1796, folding it into the Cispadane and then the Cisalpine Republic and ending its independence. Restored to Maria Beatrice at the Congress of Vienna in 1815, the duchy was inherited on her death in 1829 by her son Francis IV, who absorbed it into the Este states of Modena, and it later joined the Kingdom of Italy.
yearDesc: In 1800, Massa and Carrara was not a functioning sovereign duchy but occupied territory contested between France and the Second Coalition. Its rightful ruler, Maria Beatrice d'Este, had been dispossessed in 1796 and lived in exile, chiefly at the Austrian imperial court in Vienna. A Provisional Regency set up under Austro-Russian occupation on 28 July 1799 administered the towns through the first half of the year while awaiting a possible restoration. Napoleon's victory at the Battle of Marengo on 13 June reversed the Coalition's gains, and on 20 July 1800 the Regency was dissolved. The territory was thereupon reincorporated into the French-sponsored Cisalpine Republic, remaining part of the Napoleonic system until 1815.

name: Duchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz
description: The Duchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz was created by the 1701 Treaty of Hamburg, which resolved a succession dispute by permanently partitioning the old Duchy of Mecklenburg between the senior Schwerin line and the junior Strelitz line under Adolphus Frederick II. Ruled throughout by the Strelitz branch of the House of Mecklenburg, it remained a small state of the Holy Roman Empire whose sovereigns held a shared vote with Mecklenburg-Schwerin in the Imperial Diet. Its capital moved to the newly built residence town of Neustrelitz after the old seat of Strelitz was destroyed by fire in 1712. Under Duke Charles II, whose sister Charlotte was queen consort of George III of the United Kingdom, the duchy pursued cautious neutrality during the Napoleonic upheavals before joining the French-sponsored Confederation of the Rhine in 1808. At the Congress of Vienna its ruler was elevated to grand duke in 1815, transforming the state into the Grand Duchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz.
yearDesc: In 1800, Mecklenburg-Strelitz was a small north German duchy of the Holy Roman Empire ruled by Duke Charles II, who had succeeded his childless brother Adolf Friedrich IV in 1794. Its court and administration were seated at Neustrelitz, the planned Baroque residence town rebuilt in the 1730s. The duke pursued domestic reforms, encouraging new agricultural methods, founding a police force, and introducing compulsory schooling. Dynastic ties bound the ruling house to Britain, since the duke's sister Charlotte was the reigning queen consort of George III. The duchy stood on the eve of the Napoleonic turmoil that would soon force it, like its neighbours, to navigate between France, Prussia, and the collapsing Empire.

name: Duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwerin
description: The Duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwerin was created in 1701 by the Treaty of Hamburg, which partitioned the older Duchy of Mecklenburg between the Schwerin and Strelitz lines of the ruling Nikloting (Obotrite-descended) dynasty. It was governed as a constitutional estate-based monarchy in which the dukes shared power uneasily with an entrenched Estates assembly of nobility and towns, a relationship formalized in the 1755 Landesgrundgesetzlicher Erbvergleich. Duke Friedrich Franz I, who succeeded his uncle Friedrich II in 1785, ruled a small but internationally recognized German principality within the Holy Roman Empire. During the Napoleonic era the duchy was occupied by French troops and coerced into joining the Confederation of the Rhine in 1808 before defecting to the anti-French coalition in 1813. In recognition of this stance, Friedrich Franz I was raised to Grand Duke at the Congress of Vienna in 1815, ending the duchy's existence as a duchy and beginning the Grand Duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwerin.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwerin was ruled by Duke Friedrich Franz I, who had reigned since 1785. In 1800, the duke governed from Ludwigslust, which had served as the ducal residence and administrative seat since 1765, while Schwerin remained the traditional and nominal capital. In 1800, the duchy was a sovereign member state of the Holy Roman Empire, its politics still shaped by the powerful noble Estates and the persistence of serfdom in the countryside. In 1800, it stood at peace on the eve of the Napoleonic upheavals that would bring French occupation to the region within a few years. In 1800, the duchy's leading Baltic port was the Hanseatic city of Rostock, while the neighbouring town of Wismar was still administered by Sweden and only passed to Mecklenburg under a pledge treaty in 1803.

In 1800, Wismar was still under direct Swedish administration and was NOT part of/administered by the duchy. Rewrote the sentence to keep the verified fact (Rostock as the duchy's leading Baltic port possession, corroborated) and correctly state that Wismar was still Swedish-administered in 1800 and only passed to Mecklenburg under the 1803 pledge. All other fields CONFIRMED against Wikipedia and Deutsche Biographie: existence/dates (duchy 1701–1815, Treaty of Hamburg 1701, elevated to Grand Duchy at Congress of Vienna 1815), ruler Friedrich Franz I (r. 1785 succeeding uncle Friedrich II, raised to Grand Duke 17 June 1815), officialName, capital Ludwigslust (working capital/ducal residence 1765–1837, Schwerin the traditional/nominal capital — period-correct for 1800; coordinates 11.497/53.324 match Ludwigslust at ~11.499/53.330, within tolerance), Confederation of the Rhine 1808 and defection to the coalition in 1813 (first member to abandon Napoleon), Nikloting/Obotrite dynasty, and the 1755 Landesgrundgesetzlicher Erbvergleich. name: Duchy of Württemberg
description: The Duchy of Württemberg was created on 21 July 1495 when Emperor Maximilian I raised the County of Württemberg to ducal rank, with Eberhard I "the Bearded" as its first duke. Its early history was turbulent under Duke Ulrich, whose heavy debts, misrule, and expulsion led to a Habsburg occupation from 1520 to 1534 before he was restored and introduced the Protestant Reformation. Governed thereafter as a Lutheran state within the Holy Roman Empire, the duchy developed a strong constitutional tradition in which the territorial estates checked the ruler's power, anchored by the Treaty of Tübingen of 1514. Its strategic position exposed it to repeated French invasions during the wars of the 17th and 18th centuries, and rulers such as Duke Charles Eugene pursued absolutist and lavish courtly ambitions that strained relations with the estates. The ducal iteration ended in 1803, when Duke Frederick was elevated to Elector under the imperial reorganization, a prelude to the Kingdom of Württemberg proclaimed in 1806.
yearDesc: In 1800, Württemberg was still a duchy of the Holy Roman Empire ruled by Duke Frederick, who had succeeded his father Frederick II Eugene in December 1797. That year French armies of the Revolutionary Wars occupied the duchy, and the duke and duchess fled to Vienna as the fighting swept across southwestern Germany. The war left Württemberg exposed and pressured, foreshadowing the territorial bargains Frederick would soon strike, including ceding the enclave of Montbéliard to France in 1801. Stuttgart remained the capital and seat of the ducal court and the powerful territorial estates. Within three years the duchy would be transformed by the Reichsdeputationshauptschluss into an enlarged Electorate, ending its centuries-long existence as a duchy.

name: Duchy of Parma
officialName: Duchy of Parma, Piacenza and Guastalla
description: The Duchy of Parma and Piacenza was created in 1545 by Pope Paul III for his illegitimate son Pier Luigi Farnese, and the Farnese dynasty ruled it until its male line died out in 1731. After a brief Habsburg interlude, the 1748 Peace of Aix-la-Chapelle installed the Spanish Bourbon Infante Philip, founding the House of Bourbon-Parma and adding Guastalla to the duchy's title. Philip's son Ferdinand I succeeded on his father's death in 1765 and presided over a devout, conservative court through the upheavals of the revolutionary era. French armies under Bonaparte overran the duchy from 1796, and by the Treaty of Aranjuez in 1801 it was ceded to France, with Ferdinand's son Louis compensated with the newly created Kingdom of Etruria. Ferdinand refused to abandon Parma and clung to nominal rule until his death in 1802, whereupon France assumed direct administration, ending this Bourbon iteration of the state.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Duchy of Parma was still ruled in name by the Bourbon Duke Ferdinand I, but its independence was largely a fiction under continuous French military presence. The French victory at Marengo that June tightened Napoleon's grip on the Po plain and left the neutral duchy subjected to constant troop movements and requisitions. Diplomatic bargaining between France and Spain was already underway to trade Parma to France in exchange for Tuscany for Ferdinand's heir. Parma remained the ducal capital and the seat of Ferdinand's pious, old-regime court, which had long patronized the arts and administration. Within months these negotiations would be formalized by the treaties of Lunéville and Aranjuez, sealing the duchy's absorption into the French orbit.

name: Dutch East Indies
officialName: Dutch East Indies (possessions of the Batavian Republic in the East Indies)
description: The Dutch East Indies originated when the bankrupt Dutch East India Company was dissolved on 31 December 1799 and its Asian territories, debts, and administration were nationalized by the Batavian Republic. The colonial High Government of Governor-General and Council of the Indies, inherited from the Company, continued to rule from Batavia under state rather than corporate authority. The colony passed briefly under Franco-Dutch and then British administration during the Napoleonic Wars before being restored to the Netherlands in 1816, after which Dutch power was steadily expanded across the archipelago through the nineteenth century. Its character shifted from a mercantile trading network into a directly governed territorial state, marked by systems such as the Cultivation System and by prolonged campaigns of conquest against local polities. The colony endured until the Japanese invasion of 1942 and the subsequent Indonesian struggle for independence, formally ending with the transfer of sovereignty in 1949.
yearDesc: In 1800, the former Company possessions had just passed into the hands of the Batavian Republic, with Pieter Gerardus van Overstraten serving as Governor-General at Batavia. He was both the last Company Governor-General and the first appointed under direct state rule, presiding over a colony still administered by the inherited Council of the Indies. Effective Dutch authority remained concentrated on Java and a network of coastal forts and enclaves rather than the wider archipelago. The colony was drawn into the wider Revolutionary and Napoleonic conflicts, its shipping and outposts vulnerable to British naval power following the collapse of the old Dutch Republic. Administration in this period was strained by war, corruption inherited from the Company, and the disruption of trade with a besieged homeland.
