name: Duchy of Oldenburg
description: The Duchy of Oldenburg arose in the 1770s when the counties of Oldenburg and Delmenhorst, ceded by the Danish crown to the junior Holstein-Gottorp line under the 1773 Treaty of Tsarskoye Selo, were elevated to a duchy within the Holy Roman Empire. Its first duke was Frederick August I, an aged prince-bishop of Lübeck, who was succeeded in 1785 by his son William, whose mental incapacity meant the state was governed throughout its ducal period by the regent Peter Frederick Louis. The regency oversaw a cautious, well-administered Enlightenment-era principality and gained the secularized Prince-Bishopric of Lübeck around Eutin in 1803. In 1810 Napoleon annexed the duchy to the French Empire, and its seizure became a notable grievance feeding the diplomatic rupture between France and Russia. Restored after Napoleon's fall, it was elevated by the Congress of Vienna to the Grand Duchy of Oldenburg, the form in which it continued into the 19th century.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Duchy of Oldenburg was a small north German state of the Holy Roman Empire under the House of Holstein-Gottorp. Its nominal ruler was Duke William, but because of his mental incapacity the government was directed by his cousin and regent, Peter Frederick Louis, who had held that role since 1785. Peter Frederick Louis also then reigned as the Lutheran Prince-Bishop of Lübeck, a neighboring territory whose secularization and union with Oldenburg would follow in 1803. The duchy maintained a policy of neutrality and orderly administration amid the upheavals of the Revolutionary and Napoleonic wars sweeping the German lands. Its capital and princely seat was the city of Oldenburg, and the dynasty retained close kinship ties to both the Danish and Russian imperial houses.

Standard sources give the duchy period as 1774/1777–1815. name: Durrani Empire
officialName: Durrani Empire (Sadozai Kingdom; colloquially the Afghan Empire or Kingdom of Afghanistan)
description: The Durrani Empire was founded in 1747 when Ahmad Shah Durrani of the Sadozai clan of the Abdali (Durrani) Pashtuns was elected ruler by a tribal council following the assassination of Nader Shah, forging a confederal, tribally-based monarchy. Ahmad Shah expanded the realm through repeated campaigns, decisively defeating the Marathas at the Third Battle of Panipat in 1761 and building one of the largest Muslim empires of its era. After his death in 1772 the throne passed to his son Timur Shah, who quelled revolts and moved the primary capital from Kandahar to Kabul, keeping Peshawar as the winter seat. Following Timur Shah's death in 1793 his sons fought for the succession, and the empire slid into chronic civil war among rival Sadozai princes and their Barakzai backers, hastening the loss of Indian and Persian territories. Weakened by these dynastic feuds, the state disintegrated by the 1820s as the Barakzai dynasty displaced the Sadozai rulers, paving the way for the later Emirate of Afghanistan.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Durrani Empire was ruled by Zaman Shah Durrani, grandson of the founder, who had held the throne since 1793 from his capital at Kabul. In 1800, his authority was crumbling as his brother Mahmud Shah rebelled repeatedly from Herat and powerful Barakzai chiefs conspired against him after he executed their leader, Painda Khan. In 1800, Zaman Shah's ambitions to campaign into the Punjab and reassert power in India were repeatedly frustrated by unrest at home and by the mounting Qajar Persian threat under Fath-Ali Shah in the west. In 1800, the empire still nominally spanned much of present-day Afghanistan, Pakistan, and adjacent regions, but real control was fracturing amid competing factions and provincial governors. In 1800, the polity stood on the brink of collapse, and within a year Zaman Shah would be overthrown, captured, and blinded, plunging the realm into deeper civil strife.
issues: No corrections needed — every claim independently verified against Wikipedia (Durrani Empire, Zaman Shah Durrani, Timur Shah Durrani) and search corroboration. Confirmed: existedIn1800=yes (Zaman Shah r. 20 May 1793 – 25 July 1801, so fully extant in 1800, fragmenting into dynastic civil war). officialName matches Wikipedia's Sadozai Kingdom / Afghan Empire / Kingdom of Afghanistan colloquial names. span 1747–1823 correct: founded July 1747 by Ahmad Shah Durrani after Nader Shah's assassination; main empire dissolved March 1823 as the Barakzai dynasty (Dost Mohammad Khan) displaced the Sadozai Durranis. capital Kabul correct and period-correct: primary/summer capital since 1776 under Timur Shah (Peshawar the winter seat, Kandahar the old seat); Zaman Shah ruled from Kabul. Kabul coords verified 34.528N / 69.172E — record's 34.53/69.17 within ±0.5°. Description verified: 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP figures; Third Battle of Panipat 1761 vs Marathas, Timur Shah succeeded 1772, moved capital to Kabul, all confirmed. yearDesc verified: exactly 5 sentences each beginning 'In 1800,'; Mahmud Shah's Herat rebellion, the Painda (Payandah) Khan execution triggering the Barakzai conspiracy, the frustrated Punjab campaigns, the Qajar/Fath-Ali Shah threat, and the 1801 overthrow-capture-blinding all corroborated. pop/area/gdp correctly left as dashes — commonly cited ~14M pop / ~2,000,000 km² are peak estimates not 1800-specific (empire was actively losing territory by 1800), and no reliable 1800 GDP figure exists; dashing rather than fabricating is correct.

name: Electorate of Bavaria
description: The Wittelsbach Duchy of Bavaria was raised to an electorate on 25 February 1623 when Emperor Ferdinand II transferred the electoral dignity from the banned Frederick V of the Palatinate to Duke Maximilian I as a reward for his Catholic loyalty in the Thirty Years' War. Maximilian secured the electoral vote permanently at the Peace of Westphalia in 1648, and the Bavarian Wittelsbachs governed as one of the leading Catholic powers of the Empire for the next century and a half. When the Bavarian line died out with Maximilian III Joseph in 1777, the succession passed to Charles Theodore of the Palatinate, reuniting Bavaria and the Electoral Palatinate under a single ruler after centuries of division. On Charles Theodore's death in 1799 the inheritance devolved on Maximilian IV Joseph of the Palatinate-Zweibrücken branch, whose minister Maximilian von Montgelas launched a sweeping program of enlightened, centralizing reform. The electorate was dissolved in 1806 when Maximilian assumed the title King of Bavaria and joined the Napoleonic Confederation of the Rhine, ending Bavaria's membership in the Holy Roman Empire.
yearDesc: In 1800, Bavaria was ruled by the new elector Maximilian IV Joseph, who had inherited the united Wittelsbach lands only the year before and governed from Munich. His powerful chief minister, Count Maximilian von Montgelas, dominated policy and pushed the state toward administrative modernization and a French-leaning foreign orientation. Despite these sympathies, weak finances and a scattered, disorganized army left Bavaria dependent on Austria, and its troops shared in the Austrian defeat at Hohenlinden on 3 December 1800, after which Moreau's French forces again occupied Munich. Montgelas concluded that Bavaria's interests lay with the French Republic, steering the reluctant elector toward a separate peace and alliance with France. Meanwhile Montgelas advanced early reforms, fostering agriculture and commerce, equalizing taxes, drafting a new criminal code, and suppressing religious houses to fund education.
issues: Corrected one factual error: yearDesc dated the Battle of Hohenlinden to "2 December 1800," but the battle was fought on 3 December 1800 (Wikipedia and all military-history sources agree; the "2 December" in one source refers to the Bavarian Army's involvement / Moreau's occupation-of-Munich context, not the battle date). Changed to "3 December 1800." All other claims independently verified and confirmed: 1623 elevation of Maximilian I by Ferdinand II (25 Feb 1623, transferring Frederick V's electoral dignity); 1648 Westphalia confirmation; 1777 extinction of the Bavarian line → Charles Theodore; 1799 succession of Maximilian IV Joseph of Palatinate-Zweibrücken; Montgelas as dominant minister with French-leaning, reformist program (agriculture/commerce fostered, taxes equalized, new criminal code, religious houses suppressed to fund education — all sourced verbatim to Britannica/Wikipedia); Moreau's post-Hohenlinden occupation of Munich; move toward a separate peace/alliance with France; 1806 assumption of the royal title and Confederation of the Rhine, ending HRE membership. existedIn1800 (yes), span (1623–1806), and capital Munich (11.58E, 48.14N, within tolerance) all correct. Description is 5 sentences of pure political history with no geography/number clause; yearDesc is 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". officialName "Electorate of Palatinate-Bavaria (Kurfürstentum Pfalz-Bayern)" is a defensible scholarly designation for the united Wittelsbach electorate; the Bavarian electoral vote and precedence carried the combined state, so "Electorate of Bavaria" remains the standard map label (no single canonical German compound name is attested; "Kurpfalz-Bayern"/"Pfalz-Baiern" also seen — kept as reasonable). pop/area/gdp correctly dashed: no reliably sourceable clean per-state 1800 figure (Pfalz-Bayern territory was in flux pre-1803 secularization; no Maddison entry for this sub-state polity).

name: Emirate of Diriyah
description: The Emirate of Diriyah, known as the First Saudi State, was founded in 1744 when Muhammad bin Saud, emir of the Najdi town of Diriyah, allied with the religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab to spread a puritanical revivalist doctrine under the political leadership of the House of Saud. Muhammad bin Saud's successor Abdulaziz bin Muhammad, who ruled from 1765 to 1803, dramatically expanded the state across Najd and eastern Arabia and was assassinated in the mosque at Diriyah in 1803. Under Saud bin Abdulaziz, who reigned from 1803 to 1814, the state reached its zenith, seizing the holy cities of Mecca and Medina and directly challenging Ottoman authority over the Hejaz. This defiance provoked the Ottoman sultan to task Muhammad Ali of Egypt with crushing the emirate, and his son Ibrahim Pasha campaigned into the Arabian interior against the final ruler Abdullah bin Saud. After a months-long siege, Diriyah fell in 1818 and was razed, Abdullah was executed in Constantinople, and the First Saudi State came to an end.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Emirate of Diriyah was a rising Wahhabi power ruled by Emir Abdulaziz bin Muhammad, who had already brought most of Najd and the eastern coastal regions of Qatif and al-Hasa under Saudi control. The state functioned as a theocratic alliance between the House of Saud and the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, enforcing a strict interpretation of Islam across the territories it absorbed. Its capital Diriyah had grown into a thriving political, religious, and cultural center, drawing scholars, tribute, and trade from across the peninsula. Saudi raiding forces, often led by the emir's energetic son Saud bin Abdulaziz, pressed against the frontiers of the Hejaz and southern Iraq, setting the stage for the sack of Karbala in 1802 and the capture of Mecca in 1803. The emirate's aggressive expansion was steadily alarming the Ottomans and the sharifs of Mecca, foreshadowing the confrontation that would ultimately bring about its destruction.
issues: No corrections needed; verdict confirmed. Every claim independently corroborated against Wikipedia (First Saudi state, At-Turaif District, Wahhabi sack of Karbala) and Saudipedia. existedIn1800 "yes" correct — state ran 1744–1818, ruled by Abdulaziz bin Muhammad in 1800. officialName correct (Wikipedia formal name "Emirate of Diriyah"). span 1744–1818 correct as the standard historical span (note: Saudi govt now designates 1727 as "Founding Day," but 1744 Diriyah Pact / 1818 fall is the conventional and internally-cited span — kept). Capital coords (46.5739 E, 24.7339 N) match At-Turaif District of Diriyah (~46.575 E, 24.733 N) essentially exactly, well within tolerance — the note about a divergent 47.6 E/22.3 N map centroid is informational only and does not affect the capital pin. Ruler reign dates verified: Abdulaziz 1765–1803, assassinated in the al-Turaif mosque at Diriyah 1803; Saud 1803–1814; Abdullah bin Saud executed in Constantinople after the 1818 siege. Qatif/al-Hasa confirmed under Saudi control by 1795–1796 (so held in 1800). Karbala sack dated 1802 confirmed as the widely-accepted date (21 April 1802); Mecca captured 1803 confirmed. description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause and no pop/area/GDP figures; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for the First Saudi State and Maddison does not break out Arabia separately.

name: Electorate of Hanover
description: The Electorate was created in 1692 when Emperor Leopold I elevated Duke Ernest Augustus of the House of Brunswick-Lüneburg to the rank of prince-elector as a reward for support in the Nine Years' War, a promotion formally ratified by the Imperial Diet in 1708. In 1714 the succession of Elector George Louis to the British throne bound the electorate into a personal union with Great Britain and Ireland that would last until 1837, so that its ruler was simultaneously a German prince and the British sovereign. Because successive electors after George II resided in Britain, Hanover was governed on their behalf by a Privy Council in the city of Hanover coordinating with a German Chancery in London. Though styled Elector of Brunswick-Lüneburg, the rulers were commonly known as Electors of Hanover after their principal residence, and the state remained a mid-sized Protestant power within the Holy Roman Empire. The electorate was extinguished in 1806 upon the dissolution of the Empire, its lands passing through French and Westphalian control before being restored and raised to the Kingdom of Hanover in 1814.
yearDesc: In 1800, the electorate was ruled by George III, who had been Elector since 1760 while residing permanently in Britain and governing his German lands through his ministers in Hanover. Its formal status remained that of an Imperial electorate within the Holy Roman Empire, with the city of Hanover as its administrative seat. The state was caught between the great powers during the French Revolutionary Wars, having narrowly avoided involvement and maintaining an uneasy neutrality in these years. This precarious position soon collapsed, as a brief Prussian occupation in 1801 was followed by a French invasion on 26 May 1803. The Convention of Artlenburg on 5 July 1803 forced the disbandment of the Hanoverian army and delivered the electorate into French hands, foreshadowing its dissolution in 1806.
issues: No corrections required; all claims independently corroborated against Wikipedia (Electorate of Hanover, Invasion of Hanover (1803), Convention of Artlenburg) and reference sources. Verified: existedIn1800=yes (extant electorate, ended 1806 with HRE dissolution); officialName "Electorate of Brunswick-Lüneburg" (Kurfürstentum Braunschweig-Lüneburg) correct; span 1692–1806 correct (created by Leopold I 1692, Diet-ratified 1708, electoral title ended 1806, Kingdom from 1814); capital Hanover confirmed at 52.3705N/9.7332E (record's 52.37/9.73 within tolerance); area 26,455 km² confirmed as the post-1752 Bentheim maximum extent applicable to 1800; governance (Privy Council in Hanover + German Chancery in London), 1801 Prussian occupation, 26 May 1803 French invasion, and 5 July 1803 Convention of Artlenburg all confirmed. Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause and no numeric pop/area/GDP; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". pop and gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures. Minor note: one secondary source dates the 1803 invasion to 18 May (war declaration) / 4 June (fall of the city); the record's 26 May follows the Electorate of Hanover article's stated invasion date and is left as-is.

name: Emirate of Sindh
officialName: Emirate of Sindh (Talpur Mirs of Sindh)
description: The Emirate of Sindh was founded in 1783 when the Baloch Talpur clan, led by Mir Fateh Ali Khan, overthrew the ruling Kalhora dynasty at the Battle of Halani and Fateh Ali proclaimed himself Rais of Sindh. Fateh Ali governed jointly with his three brothers in an arrangement known as the Chauyari, or "rule of four," and the dynasty split into semi-autonomous branches centred on Hyderabad, Khairpur, and Mirpur Khas. The Talpurs adopted Shia Islam, held nominal suzerainty under the Durrani Afghans and the Khanate of Kalat, and paid tribute that lapsed as Kabul's power waned. Under the Talpur amirs Sindh remained a decentralised, tribal confederation of Baloch chiefs rather than a centralised state, and it repelled an invasion by the deposed Afghan king Shah Shuja in 1832. The emirate was conquered by the British East India Company under Sir Charles Napier, whose victory at the Battle of Miani on 17 February 1843 ended Talpur rule over most of Sindh, though the Khairpur branch survived as a princely state.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Emirate of Sindh was ruled by its founding amir, Mir Fateh Ali Khan, from the capital at Hyderabad, in the final year before his death in 1801. Power was still shared under the Chauyari system among Fateh Ali and his surviving brothers, with cadet branches at Khairpur and Mirpur Khas holding their own territories. The emirate remained nominally subordinate to the Durrani Empire under Zaman Shah, but Afghan internal weakness left the Talpurs effectively independent in practice. Fateh Ali had by this time consolidated Talpur authority across the lower Indus and extended influence over neighbouring Baloch and frontier regions. His death the following year would pass leadership to his brother Mir Ghulam Ali Talpur, continuing the dynasty's joint-rule tradition.
issues:
CORRECTED one factual error: yearDesc sentence 5 said Mir Ghulam Ali Talpur was Fateh Ali's "son" — he was in fact his BROTHER. All authoritative sources (English Wikipedia Talpur dynasty + Mir Fateh Ali Khan articles; talpur.org tomb-architecture entry) confirm the Chauyari "rule of four" were four brothers (Fateh Ali, Ghulam Ali, Karam Ali, Murad Ali) who succeeded one another fraternally: Fateh Ali → brother Ghulam Ali (d. 1811) → third brother Karam Ali. Changed "his son" to "his brother." A few careless secondary summaries mislabel him "son" but contradict themselves in the same breath ("one of the four brothers").

CONFIRMED (unchanged): existedIn1800=yes (founded 1783 at the Battle of Halani, Mir Fateh Ali Khan ruling in 1800); span 1783–1843; capital Hyderabad with coordinates 68.377E/25.396N (matches present-day Hyderabad, Sindh ~25.392N/68.374E, well within tolerance); end via Sir Charles Napier's victory at the Battle of Miani, 17 Feb 1843; Khairpur branch survived as a princely state; Shia Islam; nominal Durrani suzerainty with Zaman Shah (r. 1793–1801) the Afghan overlord in 1800; Shah Shuja's 1832 invasion repelled (matches the exact wording of the Talpur dynasty Wikipedia article — kept per source, though the broader expedition ran 1833–34 and some accounts have Shuja defeating the Talpurs near Sukkur before treatying; not a clean fabrication). Description = exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP figures — clean. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," — clean after the son→brother fix.

FLAGGED (kept, noted): Death year 1801 is DISPUTED — majority of historical sources and most secondary summaries say 1801, but the grave inscription (1217 AH) corresponds to 1802 and the current English Wikipedia infobox gives 11 May 1802. Kept 1801 per majority usage; the "final year before his death in 1801" / "his death the following year" framing therefore rests on the more common but not unanimous date.

area ~100,000 km²: corroborated by the Talpur dynasty Wikipedia article ("over 100,000 square kilometers, ... population of approximately 4 million") but that figure is pinned to c.1832 and covers the full multi-branch domain, not a verified 1800 measure; retained as an explicit approximation (label already reads "approx."). pop and gdp correctly DASHED — only a rough dynasty-wide ~4 million circulates and no reliable 1800 census or Maddison GDP figure exists; not fabricated.

name: Funj Sultanate
description: The Funj Sultanate was founded in 1504 by Amara Dunqas, whose people rose to power in the Nile valley after the collapse of the Christian Nubian kingdom of Alwa, and it soon adopted Islam while retaining African sacral-kingship traditions. Ruled from Sennar by the Funj dynasty in alliance with the Abdallab and a council of elders, it reached its territorial and commercial zenith in the 17th and early 18th centuries under sultans such as Badi II, who conquered Fazughli and its goldfields, and Badi III. Royal authority eroded through the 18th century as merchants and Islamic scholars gained influence and the unpopular centralizing sultan Badi IV was deposed in 1760–1762 by the Hamaj regent Muhammad Abu Likayik. This coup established the Hamaj Regency, under which the actual rulers governed while the Funj sultans were reduced to nominal figureheads, and outlying provinces such as Kordofan and Dongola were lost. The sultanate finally collapsed when Badi VII submitted to the invading Turco-Egyptian army of Ismail Pasha in 1821, ending more than three centuries of Funj rule.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Funj Sultanate was a fading power ruled in name by its Funj sultan at Sennar but governed in fact by the Hamaj regents, who held real authority. In 1800, the state was riven by chronic civil war among its urbanized factions, and effective control was collapsing back toward the Gezira plain between the Blue and White Niles. In 1800, its once-extensive outer provinces had already slipped away, with Kordofan lost to the Fur Sultanate and Dongola to the Shaykiyya in preceding decades. In 1800, the sultanate's decay left a fragmented realm that the regent Muhammad Adlan would only briefly stabilize after taking power in 1808. In 1800, the state still stood as an independent Islamic monarchy, roughly two decades before its conquest by the forces of Muhammad Ali of Egypt in 1821.
issues: All claims independently verified against Wikipedia (Funj Sultanate, Hamaj Regency) and corroborating sources; no corrections needed. Confirmed: founding 1504 by Amara Dunqas after the fall of Christian Nubian Alwa; capital Sennar with coordinates 33.6°E/13.55°N (multiple GPS sources give ~33.56–33.61°E, 13.54–13.57°N — within ±0.5°); Badi II conquered Fazughli/goldfields (1665); Badi IV deposed 1760–1762 by Hamaj regent Muhammad Abu Likayik (entered Sennar 27 Mar 1762), establishing the Hamaj Regency; Kordofan lost to Fur Sultanate (1785/86), Dongola to Shaykiyya (~1782); regent Muhammad Adlan rose 1808; end 1821 with Badi VII submitting to Ismail Pasha (Muhammad Ali's Turco-Egyptian army). existedIn1800 = yes (state persisted until 1821, in decline under Hamaj Regency). officialName "Sultanate of Sennar" is the standard formal name. Description is exactly 5 sentences, no geography clause, no numeric figures. yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures are sourceable. Note: the confidence field references a stale supplied centroid (31.8, 17.8), but the actual capital lon/lat in the record (33.6, 13.55) are correct and were retained.

name: Free City of Bremen
officialName: Free Imperial City of Bremen (Freie Reichsstadt Bremen)
description: Bremen won broad municipal autonomy through Emperor Frederick Barbarossa's Gelnhausen Privilege of 1186 and became a leading member of the Hanseatic League, joining formally in 1358 and thriving on Weser and North Sea trade. Full imperial immediacy was confirmed by Emperor Ferdinand III's Linz Diploma of 1646, establishing it as a Free Imperial City answerable only to the Emperor, a status it defended against Swedish and Hanoverian encroachment over the following decades. It was governed as an aristocratic merchant republic in which a self-perpetuating Senate led by several Bürgermeister held executive power alongside an assembly of the citizenry. Hanover, having acquired the neighbouring Duchy of Bremen-Verden, formally acknowledged the city's free status in 1720, ending a long rivalry over its independence. The city retained this republican constitution until Napoleon's France annexed it in 1811, after which its independence was restored in 1813 and confirmed at the Congress of Vienna in 1815.
yearDesc: In 1800, Bremen was a self-governing Free Imperial City and one of the three surviving Hanseatic ports, its Senate steering a neutral, commerce-focused policy amid the wars of the French Revolution. Its wealth rested on Weser shipping and overseas trade, and neutrality allowed its merchants to profit as belligerent powers' commerce was disrupted. The republic was ruled by its patrician Senate and Bürgermeister, with civic affairs conducted from the Rathaus on the market square beneath the cathedral. Its formal sovereignty still derived from the Holy Roman Empire, whose institutions were by now under severe strain from Napoleon's advance. Within a few years this order would collapse, as the Empire dissolved in 1806 and France annexed the city in 1811, though its independence was restored after 1813.
issues: Verdict corrected. Population changed from "~36,000 (city, 1811 census)" to "~35,800 (city, 1810)": the specific "1811 census" / ~36,630 figure could not be corroborated in any source; Wikipedia's History of Bremen population table gives 35,800 for 1810 (the nearest corroborable data point), with independent figures of 35,392 for 1812 and 37,029 for 1818. The stated magnitude (~36k) was right but the census attribution was unverifiable, so it was replaced with the sourced 1810 figure. All other fields confirmed: existedIn1800 "yes" (Bremen was a sovereign Free Imperial City with imperial immediacy in 1800); officialName correct for 1800 (Freie Reichsstadt Bremen — the "Freie Hansestadt" style postdates the 1806 dissolution); span 1646–1811 defensible (Linz Diploma confirming imperial immediacy → French annexation); capital Bremen with coords 8.8072 E / 53.0758 N matching Wikipedia's 8.80722 E / 53.07583 N; 5-sentence political-history description verified (1186 Gelnhausen Privilege, 1358 Hanseatic membership, 1646 Linz Diploma, 1720 Hanoverian recognition, 1811 annexation, 1813 restoration, 1815 Congress of Vienna all corroborated by Wikipedia/State Dept Office of the Historian); 5-sentence yearDesc begins "In 1800," and its claims (three surviving Hanseatic ports, Senate/Bürgermeister governance, 1806 dissolution, 1811 annexation) are corroborated; area and GDP correctly dashed (no clean single-city-state figures for ~1800).

name: Free City of Hamburg
officialName: Free Imperial City of Hamburg (Freie Reichsstadt Hamburg)
description: Hamburg's career as a free town began with a charter attributed to Emperor Frederick I in 1189, and it grew into a leading member of the Hanseatic League that dominated Baltic and North Sea trade. It was ruled not by a prince but as a republic, governed by a Senate or Rat of co-opted patrician merchant families alongside a citizens' assembly, an oligarchic arrangement fixed by the constitutional recess of 1712. For centuries its imperial immediacy was contested by the kings of Denmark, whose theoretical claims were finally renounced in the 1768 Treaty of Gottorp, leading to Hamburg's recognition as an immediate imperial city around 1770. When the Holy Roman Empire was dissolved in 1806 the city continued as a sovereign state styled the Free and Hanseatic City of Hamburg, but it was soon occupied and then annexed by Napoleonic France, becoming capital of the Bouches-de-l'Elbe department from 1810 to 1814. Restored after Napoleon's fall, it joined the German Confederation in 1815 as a fully sovereign member, preserving its merchant-republican identity into the modern era.
yearDesc: In 1800, Hamburg was a prosperous and independent Free Imperial City, the largest of the surviving imperial cities and one of Europe's busiest ports. Its government remained the traditional merchant Senate and citizenry, resisting the dynastic and territorial powers that surrounded it. The wars of the French Revolution had actually enriched the city, as neutral Hamburg captured much of the trade disrupted elsewhere and served as a hub for goods, finance, and émigrés. It still nominally belonged to the Holy Roman Empire, whose collapse and Napoleon's rise would soon threaten its autonomy and its commerce. Within a few years the dissolution of the Empire in 1806 and later French annexation would end this comfortable neutrality and bring economic ruin under the Continental System.
issues: No changes. Every claim independently corroborated. existedIn1800 "yes": in 1800 Hamburg was a Free Imperial City of the HRE (Britannica, Wikipedia). officialName correct for 1800 — the "Free and Hanseatic City" title postdates the 1806 dissolution, so "Free Imperial City of Hamburg / Freie Reichsstadt Hamburg" is the right pre-1806 formal name. span 1189–1806: 1189 Barbarossa free-city charter to 1806 HRE dissolution (end of imperial-city status). Capital Hamburg with lon/lat 9.99/53.55 sits on the city (correct, within tolerance). Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history, no geography clause, no numeric figure; all facts verified — 1712 Hauptrezess recess consolidating patrician-Senate rule, 1768 Treaty of Gottorp renouncing Danish claims → ~1770 recognition as an immediate imperial city (Britannica corroborates exact 1768/1770 dates), 1806 dissolution → Free and Hanseatic City, French annexation and Bouches-de-l'Elbe department 1810–1814, restoration and German Confederation membership 1815. yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," — the French Revolutionary Wars trade boom is corroborated (British exports to Hamburg rose ~600% 1792–1800; Hamburg/Bremen were the principal neutral American entrepôt of the 1790s). pop c. 130,000 (1800) is well-attested (sources ~130,000, some to ~150,000); kept. area and gdp left as dashes — no reliable 1800 city-state figures; no individual free-city GDP entry. Verdict: confirmed.

name: Ethiopian Empire
officialName: Ethiopian Empire (Mängəśtä Ityop'p'ya), the Solomonic realm ruled from Gondar
description: The Ethiopian Empire was founded in 1270 when Yekuno Amlak overthrew the Zagwe dynasty and established the Solomonic dynasty, whose rulers claimed descent from the biblical King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba to legitimize their reign. Under emperors such as Amda Seyon I and Zara Yaqob the medieval realm expanded and consolidated Christian rule across the highlands, before the devastating war with Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi and the subsequent Oromo migrations reshaped the state in the sixteenth century. In the seventeenth century Emperor Fasilides fixed the capital at Gondar, inaugurating a cultural and architectural flowering that lasted until royal authority disintegrated. From 1769 the empire entered the Zemene Mesafint, an era of princes in which the crowned emperor became a ceremonial figurehead while rival regional lords such as the Yejju rases of Begemder governed and warred in his name. Central power was finally restored by Emperor Tewodros II in 1855, and the Solomonic empire endured until the 1974 revolution deposed Haile Selassie and abolished the monarchy.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Ethiopian Empire was at the heart of the Zemene Mesafint, its emperor a captive figurehead in the walled palaces of Gondar. In that single year the throne changed hands between the puppet emperors Demetros and Tekle Giyorgis I, who were enthroned and deposed at the whim of rival warlords. Real power lay with the great regional lords, the Yejju rases who dominated Begemder and the imperial court and Ras Wolde Selassie, who ruled Tigray as a near-independent prince and championed Tekle Giyorgis I against the Yejju-backed Demetros. The realm was fragmented into rival provinces whose lords fought incessant civil wars, and imperial decrees carried little weight beyond the court. This paralysis at the centre defined Ethiopian politics until the reunification begun by Tewodros II half a century later.
issues: CORRECTED. Central attribution error fixed in yearDesc (and the original existedNote): the record claimed both puppet emperors — Demetros and Tekle Giyorgis I — were "controlled by Ras Gugsa of Yejju." Sources show the 1800 throne was CONTESTED between two rival factions: the Yejju rases installed Demetros (reigned to 24 March 1800, then again from June 1800), while Ras Wolde Selassie of Tigray brought and backed Tekle Giyorgis I (his 24 March–June 1800 reign). Also corrected the Yejju regent identity: the formal Enderase (regent) at Gondar in 1800 was Ras ALIGAZ of Yejju (regent 1788 until his death in 1803), not Gugsa; Gugsa was Ras of Begemder from c.1798 (issued proclamations "in the name of the Emperor" in 1800) and rose to the regency after Aligaz died in 1803 — though some sources loosely frame "Ras Gugsa's reign" as 1800–1825, so his 1800 dominance is contested, not settled. yearDesc rewritten to name "the Yejju rases" rather than specifically Gugsa and to state the Yejju-vs-Wolde-Selassie contest; kept at exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800 = yes; span 1270–1974 (Yekuno Amlak overthrew Zagwe 1270 → Haile Selassie deposed 1974); capital Gondar, period-correct name, coordinates 37.47E/12.6N (verified Gondar = 12.60N, 37.4667E, within ±0.01°); officialName; the 5-sentence description (Fasilides founded Gondar 1636, Zemene Mesafint standard start 1769, Tewodros II restored central power 1855 — all verified, no geography clause or numbers). POPULATION: stripped the original "modern back-estimates put Ethiopia near 3 million c.1800" parenthetical to a clean dash — that figure is only a modern reconstruction with wide uncertainty and no period census, so no unverified number should stand. AREA and GDP remain dashed (the fragmented empire had no fixed agreed extent in 1800; no reliable c.1800 GDP series exists for Ethiopia).

name: Free City of Lübeck
officialName: Free and Hanseatic City of Lübeck (Freie und Hansestadt Lübeck)
description: Emperor Frederick II granted Lübeck the status of a Free Imperial City in 1226, making it an autonomous republic answerable only to the Emperor. Rising to lead the Hanseatic League, it became the "Queen of the Hanse" and dominated Baltic commerce as the trading network's foremost member, its Lübeck law adopted by scores of Baltic towns. Power lay with a merchant patriciate that governed through a self-perpetuating Senate presided over by Bürgermeister, a constitution that endured with little change into the nineteenth century. After the Hanseatic League's effective dissolution in 1669 the city's wider influence waned, yet it retained its independence and self-government as a small sovereign city-republic. It survived the mediatization of 1803 and the empire's collapse in 1806 before Napoleon annexed it to France in 1811, after which it was restored in 1813 and confirmed as a sovereign state by the Congress of Vienna in 1815.
yearDesc: In 1800, Lübeck was a sovereign Imperial Free City on the Baltic, governed by its patrician Senate and Bürgermeister and still nominally a member of the Holy Roman Empire. Its economy rested on Baltic maritime trade, which had shown modest revival toward the end of the eighteenth century after generations of relative decline. The city remained politically neutral amid the wars of the French Revolution, profiting for a time as a trading intermediary. In 1801 Danish troops briefly occupied the town, but Lübeck preserved its freedom and self-governing status. Its independence would persist through the empire's dissolution in 1806 until the French annexation of 1811.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified. existedIn1800 "yes" confirmed: Lübeck was a sovereign Imperial Free City in 1800, survived the 1803 mediatization and 1806 HRE dissolution as one of the six free cities that kept independence, was annexed by Napoleonic France in 1811 (Bouches-de-l'Elbe department), and restored in 1813 (Wikipedia: Free City of Lübeck; German mediatisation). officialName "Free and Hanseatic City of Lübeck / Freie und Hansestadt Lübeck" confirmed. span "1226–1811": 1226 Free-Imperial-City grant by Frederick II confirmed; 1811 correctly marks the end of its independent free-city continuity via the Napoleonic annexation (the entity nominally persisted to 1937, but the sovereign span framing is defensible). Capital Lübeck with lon 10.685 / lat 53.866 confirmed — Wikipedia gives 53.870°N, 10.686°E for the old-town center, within ~0.005° (well inside ±0.5°). Description = exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no number; facts cross-checked (1226 grant; 1669 last Hanseatic Diet / effective dissolution in Lübeck; 1803/1806/1811/1813; 1815 Congress of Vienna). yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,"; the 1801 Danish occupation confirmed (April 1801, ~11,000 Danish troops under Carl of Hessen occupied Hamburg and Lübeck after the British attack on Copenhagen). pop "~31,000 (city, near 1807)" confirmed against Wikipedia's population table (1807 = 31,450), appropriately hedged as a proxy for c. 1800. area and gdp correctly dashed — no reliable 1800 territory-wide area or city-state GDP series exists.

name: Grand Duchy of Tuscany
officialName: Grand Duchy of Tuscany (Granducato di Toscana; Latin: Magnus Ducatus Etruriae)
description: The Habsburg-Lorraine Grand Duchy of Tuscany was created in 1737 when Francis Stephen of Lorraine succeeded the last Medici grand duke, transferring the state from the extinct Medici line to the House of Lorraine. Its most celebrated ruler, Grand Duke Leopold (Pietro Leopoldo, r. 1765–1790), made Tuscany a byword for Enlightened absolutism, overhauling taxation and abolishing capital punishment in 1786 before leaving to become Holy Roman Emperor. His son Ferdinand III succeeded in 1790 and continued a cautiously reformist, largely peaceful course, declaring Tuscan neutrality in the Revolutionary Wars in 1795. That neutrality collapsed when French armies invaded in 1799, expelling Ferdinand and provoking the royalist "Viva Maria" uprising centred on Arezzo. The Habsburg-Lorraine grand duchy was extinguished by the Treaty of Aranjuez in 1801, which handed Tuscany to the Bourbon-Parma as the Kingdom of Etruria, though the dynasty would be restored to a revived grand duchy in 1814.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Grand Duchy of Tuscany was a state under military occupation, its rightful sovereign Ferdinand III still in exile in Vienna after fleeing the French invasion of 1799. In the previous year French troops had held Florence only from March to July 1799 before being driven out by the popular "Viva Maria" revolt and the Austro-Russian counter-offensive, leaving Austrian and allied forces in de facto control at the year's start. Napoleon's victory at Marengo on 14 June 1800 reversed the tide in Italy, and French forces again pushed south to occupy Tuscany over the following months. The grand duchy thus spent 1800 as a contested prize between the Habsburg and French war efforts rather than a functioning independent monarchy. Its fate was effectively sealed by the campaign, and within months the Treaty of Aranjuez (March 1801) would abolish it in favour of the French-sponsored Kingdom of Etruria.
issues: Verdict corrected — one factual/temporal fix in yearDesc; everything else confirmed. FIX: yearDesc sentence 2 opened "Earlier that year French troops had held Florence only from March to July 1799," which is a temporal contradiction ("earlier that year" refers to 1800, but the occupation dates are 1799). Changed "Earlier that year" → "In the previous year" so the phrasing matches the 1799 dates. All substantive facts in yearDesc verified: French held Florence Mar–Jul 1799, expelled by Viva Maria revolt + Austro-Russian advance, Austrian de facto control at the start of 1800, Marengo 14 June 1800, French re-occupied Tuscany (Oct 1800, cited on grounds of British activity at Livorno) — "over the following months" is accurate; Treaty of Aranjuez 21 March 1801. CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800 "yes" (de jure Habsburg-Lorraine grand duchy existed in 1800, militarily contested). officialName (Italian Granducato di Toscana, Latin Magnus Ducatus Etruriae — both per Wikipedia). span 1737–1801 correct for the Habsburg-Lorraine iteration (Lorraine rule began 1737 on Gian Gastone's death; dissolved 1801, restored 1814). capital Florence, coords 11.2558E/43.7696N on the city centre (within ±0.5°). description exactly 5 sentences, political-only, no figures — Lorraine succession 1737, Leopold's Enlightened reforms + capital-punishment abolition 30 Nov 1786, Ferdinand III succeeded 1790, neutrality declared 1 March 1795, Viva Maria centred on Arezzo, extinguished by Treaty of Aranjuez 1801, restored 1814 — all verified. pop 1,096,641 (1801) confirmed by Wikipedia. area c. 22,000 km² corroborated (Wikipedia gives 21,710 km² for 1852; rounded up is acceptable and flagged as approximate). gdp dashed — no reliable per-state 1800 figure (Maddison does not break Tuscany out of Italy); correctly left as "—".

name: Electorate of Saxony
description: The Electorate of Saxony took its enduring form in 1547 when, after the Schmalkaldic War, the Albertine duke Maurice supplanted the Ernestine line and was invested with the Saxon electoral dignity, shifting the seat of power to Dresden. As one of the leading princes of the Holy Roman Empire and a prime mover of the Reformation, the electorate ranked among the foremost German states, second in weight only to Habsburg Austria. Its rulers of the House of Wettin culminated in Augustus the Strong and his son, who held the Polish crown in personal union through much of the eighteenth century, entangling Saxony in the wars of the age. The dynasty's conversion to Catholicism in 1697 to secure that throne left a Catholic sovereign governing an overwhelmingly Lutheran land, a defining tension of the state. The electorate was devastated by the Seven Years' War, pursued cautious neutrality thereafter, and finally ended in December 1806 when Napoleon elevated its last elector to King Frederick Augustus I of Saxony.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Electorate of Saxony was ruled by Frederick Augustus III, who had governed since 1763 and was widely esteemed for his frugal, orderly administration. Governed from Dresden, it remained a senior member of the Holy Roman Empire, its Catholic elector still presiding over a Lutheran population. Having been dragged into the War of the First Coalition against revolutionary France by the Empire's declaration of Reichskrieg in 1793, Saxony had withdrawn from the coalition in August 1796 and by 1800 held to a policy of prudent neutrality. This long peace allowed its economy, mining, textiles, and the great Leipzig trade fairs to prosper while much of Germany suffered war. The electorate would maintain this quiet course until 1806, when alliance with Prussia, French defeat of both, and the Treaty of Poznań transformed it into the Kingdom of Saxony.
issues: No corrections needed — all claims independently verified against Wikipedia and corroborating sources. VERIFIED: (1) existedIn1800 = yes; it was an Imperial Electorate under the Albertine Wettin Frederick Augustus III (r. 1763–1806) and was raised to the Kingdom of Saxony only on 20 Dec 1806 via the Treaty of Poznań (11 Dec 1806), so "Electorate" is period-correct for 1800. (2) span 1547–1806 is a defensible framing for the Albertine electorate specifically: the electoral dignity passed to Maurice of Saxony's Albertine line after the Battle of Mühlberg / Capitulation of Wittenberg in 1547 (enfeoffed 4 June 1547), with the seat moving to Dresden — matching the anchor and the description's "took its enduring form in 1547." (The Electorate as an institution predates this to the 1356 Golden Bull, but that is the Ascanian/Ernestine lineage, not this Albertine polity.) (3) capital Dresden confirmed; actual coordinates 51.051 N, 13.738 E — the record's 51.05/13.74 are within ±0.01°. (4) 1697 conversion of Augustus the Strong to Catholicism confirmed (formal conversion 1697, coronation as King of Poland 15 Sep 1697). (5) Reichskrieg declaration 1793 and Saxon withdrawal from the First Coalition Aug 1796 both confirmed. (6) description is exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no numeric pop/area/GDP; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". POP: kept at "c. 2,000,000 (est. c. 1800)" as an explicitly-flagged rounded estimate. Rationale confirmed: at the 1815 Congress of Vienna the Kingdom ceded ~850,000 inhabitants to Prussia and retained ~1,500,000 (total ~2.35M in 1815), so c.1800 was plausibly ~2M and certainly well above the Wikidata "1,100,000 (1800)" figure, which conflicts with the partition arithmetic; the flagged ~2M estimate is retained rather than the questionable 1.1M. AREA: 39,425 km² (1805) confirmed by Wikipedia (pre-1815 borders). GDP: correctly dashed — Maddison/standard sources do not break out the Electorate separately.

name: Great Lakes kingdoms
officialName: Great Lakes kingdoms (Buganda, Bunyoro-Kitara, Rwanda, Burundi and Nkore)
description: The interlacustrine region was home to several independent Bantu monarchies whose ruling dynasties traced their legitimacy to the earlier Empire of Kitara, from which Bunyoro-Kitara emerged as the senior power under the Babiito dynasty founded by Rukidi-Mpuga in the late sixteenth century. Buganda, ruled by its Kabaka, expanded steadily at Bunyoro's expense from the seventeenth century onward, seizing counties such as Bulemeezi and Kyaggwe and building a centralized state administered through appointed chiefs headed by the Katikkiro, eventually eclipsing Bunyoro as the region's dominant kingdom. To the south, the Nyiginya dynasty consolidated the Kingdom of Rwanda under its Mwami, while the Ganwa aristocracy governed the Kingdom of Burundi and the Bahinda dynasty ruled the cattle-keeping kingdom of Nkore. These states shared broadly similar institutions of sacred kingship, clientage, and cattle-based hierarchy, yet remained politically separate and frequently warred, raided, and negotiated with one another. Their independence endured until the late nineteenth century, when British and German colonial expansion absorbed them into the Uganda Protectorate and German East Africa.
yearDesc: In 1800, Buganda was the ascendant power of the region, ruled by Kabaka Semakookiro from his royal capital at Kasangati and pressing its expansion against a declining Bunyoro. In 1800, Bunyoro-Kitara under Omukama Kyebambe III Nyamutukura remained large but was losing ground and territory to Buganda and to internal fragmentation. In 1800, the Kingdom of Rwanda under the Nyiginya dynasty was ruled by Mwami Mibambwe III Mutabazi II Sentabyo, whose death the following year would bring the boy-king Yuhi IV Gahindiro to the throne under a regency. In 1800, Burundi was entering its greatest phase of expansion under Mwami Ntare IV Rutaganzwa Rugamba, who roughly doubled the kingdom's territory through conquest and diplomacy. In 1800, Nkore was a smaller pastoral kingdom under its Bahinda Omugabe, one of several interlacustrine states whose sovereignty would last until the colonial partition at the century's end.
issues: CORRECTED the Rwanda sentence of yearDesc (sentence 3), which was anachronistic. The original claimed Rwanda in 1800 was "governed by a regency for the young Mwami Yuhi IV Gahindiro." This is wrong for the year 1800: Gahindiro acceded only in 1801. In 1800, Rwanda was still ruled by his predecessor Mwami Mibambwe III Mutabazi II Sentabyo (r. 1796–1801, Wikipedia), who died c. 1801 (of smallpox), after which the boy-king Gahindiro succeeded under the regency of his mother Nyiratunga and her brother Rugaaju/Rugagi. Rewrote the sentence to name Sentabyo as the 1800 ruler and note the imminent 1801 succession and regency; still exactly 5 sentences all beginning "In 1800,". Everything else CONFIRMED: existedIn1800 "yes" — all five kingdoms were functioning monarchies in 1800. Semakookiro of Buganda r. 1797–1814 with capital at Kasangati (Wikipedia). Bunyoro's Kyebambe III Nyamutukura r. 1786–1835, so reigning in 1800 (the original prose gave no wrong date). Ntare IV Rutaganzwa Rugamba of Burundi r. c. 1796–1850, credited with roughly doubling the kingdom — confirmed. Bunyoro-Kitara Babiito dynasty founded by Rukidi-Mpuga in the late 16th c. after the Empire of Kitara — confirmed. Ganwa aristocracy governing Burundi and Bahinda dynasty ruling Nkore — confirmed. officialName format, span "—", capital blank/null (composite grouping with no single capital), and pop/area/gdp all dashed (no reliable 1800 figures exist) — all appropriate and left as-is. Description is a general 5-sentence political history with no geography clause and no numeric figures — left unchanged.

name: Habsburg Monarchy
officialName: The Habsburg Monarchy (the personal union of the Austrian hereditary lands, the Kingdom of Bohemia, and the Kingdom of Hungary under the House of Habsburg-Lorraine)
description: The Habsburg Monarchy took shape in 1526 when Ferdinand I united the Austrian hereditary lands with the crowns of Bohemia and Hungary, creating a composite state held together only by shared dynastic rule rather than a single constitution. For centuries its rulers also monopolized the elective title of Holy Roman Emperor, so the monarchy blended dynastic hereditary lands with leadership of the wider German realm. Under Maria Theresa and Joseph II in the eighteenth century the dynasty pursued centralizing and enlightened reforms that strengthened the bureaucracy and army while provoking resistance in Hungary and the Austrian Netherlands. The dynastic line continued through the House of Habsburg-Lorraine after 1740, and the monarchy became a leading opponent of Revolutionary France in the 1790s. This iteration ended in 1804 when Francis II proclaimed the hereditary Austrian Empire, formally consolidating the separate realms into a single imperial state.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Habsburg Monarchy was ruled by Francis II, who was simultaneously Holy Roman Emperor, King of Bohemia, and King of Hungary, governing from Vienna. In that year it was still waging the War of the Second Coalition against Napoleonic France largely alongside Britain, Russia having abandoned the alliance early in 1800 as Tsar Paul I turned toward Napoleon. Its armies suffered decisive defeats at Marengo in June 1800 and at Hohenlinden in December 1800, which shattered the coalition's position in Italy and Germany. These reverses forced Francis to the negotiating table, leading to the Treaty of Lunéville in February 1801, which confirmed French dominance west of the Rhine and in northern Italy. The monarchy remained a sprawling multiethnic composite of Austrian, Bohemian, Hungarian, and other lands, still four years away from being reorganized as the Austrian Empire.
issues: Corrected yearDesc sentence 2: the original stated Austria was "having joined Britain, Russia, and other powers" against France, which misleadingly implied Russia was still a coalition partner in 1800. In fact Tsar Paul I had disengaged Russia in October 1799 and formally ended the Russian alliance with Britain and Austria on 8 January 1800, then turned pro-Napoleon. Revised to state Austria fought on largely alongside Britain in 1800, with Russia having already left. All other fields VERIFIED and unchanged: existedIn1800 "yes" (no anachronism — "Austrian Empire" title proclaimed only 11 Aug 1804, so "Habsburg Monarchy" is period-correct for 1800); span 1526–1804 (standard scholarly dating of the composite state: Ferdinand I's 1526 union of the Austrian, Bohemian, and Hungarian crowns → 1804 Austrian Empire; note Wikipedia's article uses 1282–1918 for the broader dynastic realm, but 1526–1804 for the composite monarchy is a well-established convention consistent with the anchor); capital Vienna correct in city, 1800 name, and coordinates (16.37E/48.21N accurate); rulers/titles of Francis II confirmed (HRE 1792–1806, King of Hungary and Bohemia); 1800 events all confirmed (Marengo 14 Jun 1800, Hohenlinden 3 Dec 1800, Treaty of Lunéville 9 Feb 1801, French dominance left bank of Rhine + northern Italy); Habsburg-Lorraine line dated to after 1740 (senior male line extinct on Charles VI's death 1740) confirmed; description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or numeric figures; yearDesc remains exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". Population ~24 million circa 1800 is corroborated (Habsburg sources cite 24 million by 1800); the hedged range "c. 21–24 million" is kept given genuine uncertainty over shifting Napoleonic-era territorial extent. Area and GDP dashed — no reliable 1800-specific figure sourced.

name: Helvetic Republic
description: The Helvetic Republic was proclaimed "One and Indivisible" on 12 April 1798 after French troops invaded and dissolved the Old Swiss Confederacy, replacing cantonal sovereignty with a centralized unitary state modeled on the French constitution of 1795 and largely drafted by the Basel magistrate Peter Ochs. Executive power rested with a five-member Directory over a bicameral legislature, but the regime was a French client dependent on Paris for its survival and never commanded broad Swiss loyalty. Its brief life was consumed by factional strife between centralists and federalists, producing more than six constitutions and a cascade of coups d'état, including the deposition of Peter Ochs in 1799 and the ousting of Frédéric-César de La Harpe on 7 January 1800. Armed uprisings in the Catholic cantons, mounting debt, and the 1802 Stecklikrieg civil war exposed the state's chronic instability and its inability to govern without French bayonets. Napoleon Bonaparte ended the experiment with the Act of Mediation of 19 February 1803, restoring a confederal structure with substantial cantonal autonomy.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Helvetic Republic was in deep political crisis, governed from Bern as its seat since the capital had shifted from Aarau and Lucerne. On 7 January 1800 a coup d'état overthrew the Directory and deposed Frédéric-César de La Harpe, replacing directorial rule with an executive commission amid bitter feuding between unitarist and federalist factions. The centralized regime remained wholly dependent on French military backing and continued to lack legitimacy among a population resentful of conscription, requisitions, and the loss of cantonal self-rule. Switzerland in this year still bore the strains of the 1799 campaigns fought across its territory between French and Austro-Russian armies, which had left devastation and financial ruin. The unstable government lurched through further constitutional revisions, foreshadowing the collapse that would culminate in the 1802 civil war and Napoleon's 1803 Act of Mediation.
issues: CORRECTED pop "~1.6 million" → "~1.5 million". The specific sourced 1800 figure for the Helvetic Republic's own territory is 1,493,726 (German Wikipedia infobox, year 1800), i.e. ~1.5M. Higher ~1.7–1.8M figures (BFS/swissinfo) count present-day Switzerland INCLUDING territories not in the Republic (Geneva, Neuchâtel, Valais, Bishopric of Basel), so they overstate the Republic; ~1.6M was between the two and slightly high. All other fields CONFIRMED: existedIn1800=yes (1798–1803, mid-existence). officialName confirmed — 121 deputies proclaimed the Helvetic Republic "One and Indivisible" on 12 April 1798 (EN Wikipedia). span 1798–1803 confirmed (12 Apr 1798 – 10 Mar 1803). Capital Bern confirmed: seat moved Aarau (1798) → Lucerne (Sept 1798–1799) → Bern from May 1799 (fled during War of Second Coalition), Bern 1799–1803 per EN Wikipedia infobox; coords 7.4474/46.948 = city of Bern, correct. 7 Jan 1800 coup verified: both chambers deposed Directors La Harpe/Oberlin/Secrétan, abolished the Directory, and installed a provisional executive committee (Vollziehungsausschuss) — matches description + yearDesc exactly. Peter Ochs (Basel magistrate, drafter), five-member Directory, 1802 Stecklikrieg, Act of Mediation 19 Feb 1803 all confirmed. description = exactly 5 sentences, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP number. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences, begins "In 1800,". area dashed appropriately (1800 borders ≠ modern Switzerland, no clean period figure). gdp dashed appropriately (Maddison Switzerland series begins 1820, not 1800).

name: Hyderabad State
description: Hyderabad State arose in 1724 when Asaf Jah I (Nizam-ul-Mulk), a Mughal viceroy of the Deccan, asserted de facto independence and founded the Asaf Jahi dynasty. His successors, most notably Nizam Ali Khan (Asaf Jah II), consolidated the state amid the rivalries of the Marathas, Mysore, and rival European powers, permanently establishing the seat of government at Hyderabad city. Pressed between these powers, the Nizam allied with the British East India Company, becoming in 1798 the first Indian ruler to accept a subsidiary alliance, which surrendered his foreign policy and defence to the Company in exchange for protection. Thereafter Hyderabad endured as the largest and most senior of the princely states within the British paramountcy, ruled by successive Nizams under a British Resident. The dynasty ended in 1948 when the independent Indian state annexed Hyderabad through the military action known as Operation Polo.
yearDesc: In 1800, Hyderabad State was ruled by Nizam Ali Khan, Asaf Jah II, who had reigned since 1762 and would rule until 1803. In 1800, the state was firmly within the British orbit following the subsidiary alliance signed on 1 September 1798, having earlier dismissed its French-officered corps at British insistence. In 1800, a supplementary treaty concluded on 12 October formalised the cession of the Ceded Districts to the Company as payment for the subsidiary British force stationed in the Nizam's territory. In 1800, the Nizam nonetheless retained internal sovereignty over a vast Deccan realm governed from Hyderabad, with the British Resident based at the court. In 1800, the state remained the foremost of the emerging princely states, its external and military autonomy curtailed but its dynasty and administration intact.
issues: Two corrections. (1) officialName: removed the anachronistic "His Highness" prefix — the Nizam's British honorific style "His Exalted Highness" was conferred only in 1918, not in use as the polity's formal designation in 1800; the well-attested formal name is "The Nizam's Dominions (Hyderabad State)." (2) area: DASHED. The prior value ~82,698 sq mi (~214,190 km²) is the 1901/1941 administrative extent of Hyderabad State proper and EXCLUDES Berar (per en.wikipedia Hyderabad State, which gives 82,698 sq mi for 1941 and states no ~1800 figure exists). In 1800 the Nizam still fully held Berar (~17,728 sq mi; not "assigned" to the British until 1853), so the 1800 state was materially larger than that figure — attributing the later figure to 1800 boundaries understates the state and is unsupportable, so it is dashed rather than left standing. CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800 (founded 1724 by Asaf Jah I, extant in 1800 — no anachronism); span 1724–1948 (dissolution via Operation Polo, Sept 1948); capital Hyderabad, correct for 1800 (capital moved Aurangabad→Hyderabad in 1763) with coords on Hyderabad city (17.36N/78.47E vs actual ~17.385N/78.487E, within tolerance); ruler Nizam Ali Khan Asaf Jah II r.1762–1803; 1798 subsidiary alliance (1 Sept 1798, first Indian ruler); dismissal of Raymond's French-officered corps; 12 Oct 1800 Treaty of Cession of the Ceded Districts (Cuddapah, Kurnool, Bellary, Anantapur); British Resident at court. description = exactly 5 sentences, political history, no geography/number clauses. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences all beginning "In 1800," (minor wording tweak "ratified"→"concluded" for the 12 Oct treaty to match sources; date confirmed correct). pop and gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures. Sources: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyderabad_State; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nizam_Ali_Khan,_Asaf_Jah_II; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ceded_Districts; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berar_Province; 1911 Encyclopaedia Britannica (Wikisource); en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad.

name: Hohenzollern principalities
description: The two Swabian Hohenzollern principalities descend from the County of Hohenzollern, which was partitioned in 1576 among the sons of Count Karl I into the lines of Hechingen, Sigmaringen, and the soon-extinct Haigerloch. Both surviving lines, which remained Roman Catholic and formed the senior branch of the dynasty that would later rule Prussia and Germany, were elevated from counts to Imperial Princes in 1623. As immediate fiefs of the Holy Roman Empire they governed small, largely independent territories from their seats at Hechingen and Sigmaringen, sharing dynastic origins but ruling as distinct sovereign states. After the Empire's dissolution they joined the Confederation of the Rhine in 1806 and then the German Confederation in 1815, retaining their sovereignty into the 19th century. Following the Revolutions of 1848 both princes abdicated, and in 1849–1850 the territories were ceded to the Kingdom of Prussia, which combined them into the Province of Hohenzollern.
yearDesc: In 1800, the two principalities were separate immediate members of the Holy Roman Empire, each ruled by a prince of the Catholic Swabian Hohenzollern branch. Hohenzollern-Hechingen was governed by Prince Hermann, who had succeeded his uncle in 1798 and ruled as a strict, businesslike patriarch enjoying a final high point of princely absolutism. Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen was ruled by Prince Anton Aloys, who had succeeded his father in 1785 and had fled to Vienna during the French Revolutionary Wars before returning in 1796. Both princes now faced the upheavals of the Napoleonic era, and Anton Aloys would soon lose his rich Dutch estates to France, receiving territory such as Glatt and secularized monasteries as compensation in 1802–1803. The Empire that guaranteed their status still stood in 1800, but its dissolution in 1806 and their absorption into the Confederation of the Rhine lay only a few years ahead.
issues: No corrections needed — all claims independently verified against Wikipedia. CONFIRMED: (1) 1576 partition of the County of Hohenzollern among Count Karl I's three sons into Hechingen (Eitel Friedrich IV), Sigmaringen (Charles II), and the soon-extinct Haigerloch (Christoph, line extinct 1592/1634). (2) Both surviving Catholic lines elevated from counts to Imperial Princes in 1623 (Ferdinand II, 23 March 1623). (3) Both were immediate HRE fiefs seated at Hechingen and Sigmaringen. (4) Joined Confederation of the Rhine 1806, German Confederation 1815–1850. (5) Both princes abdicated after the 1848 Revolutions (7 Dec 1849); territories ceded to Prussia and incorporated 12 March 1850 as the Province of Hohenzollern. RULERS CONFIRMED: Hechingen — Prince Hermann, succeeded his uncle Josef Friedrich Wilhelm on 9 Apr 1798, r. 1798–1810, described in sources verbatim as "a businesslike, vigilant patriarch" managing "one last high point of princely absolutism." Sigmaringen — Prince Anton Aloys, succeeded his father 1785, r. 1785–1831, fled to Vienna during the French Revolutionary Wars and returned 1796, lost his Dutch estates (via his mother's county of Bergh) to France in 1802, compensated with Glatt and the secularized monasteries of Inzigkofen/Beuron/Holzen (1802–03, per the Reichsdeputationshauptschluss). existedIn1800 set to "partial" rather than "yes": in 1800 this was NOT a single unified polity but two separate immediate principalities grouped by the map as a convenience — the note and description correctly flag this. Capital Sigmaringen coordinates verified: actual 48.088 N, 9.230 E; recorded 48.088 / 9.217 (longitude off by ~0.013°, well within ±0.5° tolerance, left as-is). pop/area/gdp kept as dashes — no reliable ~1800 figures for these two tiny states are sourceable; not fabricated. Description = exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no numeric figures. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". Span 1623–1850 correctly reflects the shared principality-status era of both lines. Verdict: confirmed.

name: Hausa Kingdoms
description: By tradition the Hausa states traced their origin to the Bayajidda legend, in which a wandering hero married the queen of Daura and fathered the founders of the seven "true" states known as the Hausa Bakwai—Daura, Kano, Katsina, Zazzau, Gobir, Rano, and Biram. They never formed a single kingdom but functioned as a loose set of rival city-states, each ruled by its own sarki and lacking any central authority or shared overlord. From the fourteenth century onward Islam spread among their courts through Wangara and other merchants, and reformers such as Muhammad Rumfa of Kano and Muhammad al-Korau of Katsina strengthened Islamic institutions while older ritual practices persisted alongside them. Their history was one of shifting hegemonies and constant warfare, with Kano and Katsina dominating trade in earlier centuries and militarized Gobir rising to prominence in the eighteenth. The states' era of independence ended when Usman dan Fodio launched his jihad in 1804, defeating their rulers and incorporating them one by one as emirates of the Sokoto Caliphate by around 1808.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Hausa Kingdoms were still independent city-states, each governed by its own sarki and electoral council rather than any unified crown. In 1800, Gobir stood as the most aggressive military power under the aging Sarki Nafata, who had begun imposing restrictions on the reformist preaching of the Fulani scholar Usman dan Fodio. In 1800, Kano and Katsina remained the great commercial centers, though Kano's earlier dominance had been eroded by cowrie-shell inflation and heavy taxation that shifted trade advantage toward Katsina. In 1800, the states were nominally Muslim but were denounced by dan Fodio's followers for syncretic practices, unjust taxation, and enslavement of fellow Muslims, tensions that were rapidly sharpening. In 1800, none of the rulers foresaw that within four years dan Fodio would proclaim a jihad that would sweep away their dynasties and forge the Sokoto Caliphate.
issues: No changes; verdict confirmed. Independently verified every claim against Wikipedia (Hausa Kingdoms, Gobir, Yunfa, Jihad of Usman dan Fodio), Britannica, BlackPast, and African History Extra. existedIn1800 "yes" is correct — sovereign city-states in 1800, jihad began only 1804. Hausa Bakwai list (Daura, Kano, Katsina, Zazzau, Gobir, Rano, Biram) and Bayajidda legend confirmed; no single capital/overlord confirmed, so capital correctly left blank/null. 14th-c.
