name: Asante Empire
description: The Asante Empire was forged around 1701 when Osei Tutu I, aided by the priest Okomfo Anokye, united the Akan states of the Kumasi region and, according to tradition, bound them together under the sacred Golden Stool after defeating the rival Denkyira at the Battle of Feyiase. Osei Tutu built a centralised confederacy with Kumasi as its capital, and his successor Opoku Ware I greatly extended its reach across the forest zone in the early eighteenth century. The empire developed a sophisticated bureaucracy, standing army, and roads radiating from Kumasi, and it grew wealthy on gold and on the Atlantic trade in enslaved captives exchanged with European traders on the coast. Ruled by a line of Asantehenes from the Oyoko clan, it dominated much of what is now Ghana and repeatedly clashed with coastal Fante states and later with Britain. After a long series of Anglo-Asante wars through the nineteenth century, the empire was defeated and formally annexed by Britain in 1901, ending its sovereign existence.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Asante Empire was a powerful independent state centred on Kumasi and passing through a turbulent succession crisis. Osei Kwame Panyin, enstooled in 1777, had by this time been effectively destooled following disputes with the queen-mother Konadu Yaadom and his failure to appear for the Odwira festival, leaving the throne contested. The stool soon passed to the short-lived Opoku Fofie and then, about 1800 to 1804 depending on the source, to the vigorous Osei Bonsu, also called Osei Tutu Kwame. Despite the internal instability, the empire remained territorially intact and continued to profit from the gold and slave trades routed toward the British, Dutch, and Danish forts on the coast. Within a few years Osei Bonsu would launch his major campaigns against the Fante confederacy, culminating in the invasions and victories of 1806 to 1807.
issues: Corrected one factual error in yearDesc: the coastal European forts serving Asante trade around 1800 were British (Cape Coast), Dutch (Elmina), and DANISH (Christiansborg/Osu) — the record said "French," but the French held no significant fort on the Gold Coast in this period, so I changed "French" to "Danish." Everything else verified and kept: founding 1701 (Osei Tutu I, Okomfo Anokye, Golden Stool, Battle of Feyiase vs Denkyira) confirmed; capital Kumasi confirmed and coords -1.62/6.69 land on the city (actual -1.6244/6.6885, within tolerance); officialName Asanteman confirmed; description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography/number clauses and its facts (Opoku Ware I as expanding successor, Oyoko clan, Anglo-Asante wars, 1901 annexation) check out; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," and the disputed 1800 ruler is handled correctly by describing the Osei Kwame Panyin destoolment / Opoku Fofie / Osei Bonsu crisis (accession genuinely dated 1800 vs 1804 across sources) rather than falsely picking one, and the 1806–1807 Fante campaign dates are confirmed. Span end 1901 kept as a defensible choice (formal annexation 26 Sep 1901; Crown Colony 1902) as flagged in the record's own note. pop (~3 million; Wikipedia gives 3–4 million for the 19th c.) and area (~259,000 km²) kept as documented but approximate/peak-19th-c. figures, not precisely 1800-measured. GDP left dashed — no reliable Maddison-style figure exists for this precolonial polity.

name: First French Republic
officialName: French Republic (République française) — under the Consulate
description: The First French Republic was proclaimed on 22 September 1792 after the monarchy was abolished, and it executed King Louis XVI in January 1793. Its early years passed through the radical Jacobin Convention and the Reign of Terror under the Committee of Public Safety, followed by the Thermidorian Reaction and the more conservative but unstable Directory from 1795. On 9–10 November 1799 General Napoleon Bonaparte overthrew the Directory in the coup of 18 Brumaire, establishing the Consulate under the Constitution of the Year VIII with himself as First Consul wielding effective sole power. During the Consulate he centralized the state, promulgated the Napoleonic Civil Code, signed the Concordat of 1801 with the Papacy, and had himself made Consul for Life in 1802. The Republic endured in name until Napoleon was proclaimed Emperor of the French on 18 May 1804, transforming it into the First French Empire.
yearDesc: In 1800, the French Republic was governed by the Consulate with Napoleon Bonaparte as First Consul, having taken power only months earlier in the coup of Brumaire. In February 1800 he moved his residence into the Tuileries Palace in Paris and pressed forward with administrative and financial reforms, founding the Banque de France that same year. The Republic remained at war with the Second Coalition, and Napoleon personally led an army across the Alps into Italy, defeating the Austrians at the Battle of Marengo on 14 June 1800. That victory, reinforced by Moreau's triumph at Hohenlinden in December, forced Austria toward the Treaty of Lunéville of early 1801. Peace negotiations with Britain were also underway, culminating in the Peace of Amiens signed in 1802.
issues: No corrections needed — the record is accurate as supplied. Verified independently: existedIn1800 "yes" (First French Republic 1792–1804; in its Consulate phase in 1800 with Napoleon as First Consul after the 18 Brumaire coup of 9 Nov 1799). officialName correct (French Republic under the Consulate). Span 1792–1804 confirmed (Republic proclaimed Sept 1792, First Empire proclaimed 18 May 1804). Capital Paris; coordinates 2.35 E / 48.85 N land squarely on Paris. Description = exactly 5 sentences, political-only, no geography/number clause — all facts corroborated (Louis XVI executed Jan 1793, Terror, Directory from 1795, Constitution of Year VIII, Civil Code, Concordat of 1801, Consul for Life 1802, Emperor 18 May 1804). yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," — all events verified: Tuileries move 19 Feb 1800, Banque de France founded 18 Jan 1800, Battle of Marengo 14 June 1800, Moreau at Hohenlinden 3 Dec 1800, Treaty of Lunéville 9 Feb 1801, Peace of Amiens 25 Mar 1802. pop ~29 million is a well-cited estimate for France c.1800 (INED/Statista: grew from ~19.7M in 1700 to ~29M by 1800; ~27.3M cited for 1801), kept with the noted caveat that the actual Consulate Republic was larger due to annexed departments (Belgium, Rhineland) — boundary-sensitive but defensible. area and gdp correctly dashed: no clean, reliably sourceable single figure exists for the 1800 Republic's annexed extent (area) or a boundary-consistent aggregate (gdp), so both are left as "—" rather than fabricated. Note only: the "22 September 1792" proclamation date is a common convention (first day of Year I); the abolition vote was 21 September 1792 — both are widely used and this is not treated as an error.

name: Kingdom of Mysore
description: The Wodeyar dynasty had ruled Mysore since around 1399, but real power was seized in 1761 by the general Hyder Ali, whose son Tipu Sultan turned the kingdom into a formidable rival of the British East India Company. After Tipu was killed at the fall of Seringapatam in the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War of 1799, the victorious British partitioned the state and restored the Wodeyar line, crowning the five-year-old Krishnaraja Wodeyar III as maharaja under a subsidiary alliance. This restored kingdom was administered for its first decade by the capable Dewan Purnaiah as regent, with a British Resident overseeing external affairs, making Mysore a princely state within British paramountcy. Krishnaraja Wodeyar III took personal charge in 1810 but was later stripped of governing power in 1831 for alleged misrule, after which the British administered the state directly until it was restored to his adopted successor in 1881. The Wodeyars ruled as maharajas until the state acceded to independent India in 1947, ending the dynasty's long reign as a sovereign house.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Mysore was ruled in name by the child maharaja Krishnaraja Wodeyar III, who had been enthroned the previous year, with actual governance in the hands of the Dewan Purnaiah acting as regent. The state functioned under a subsidiary alliance with the British East India Company, whose Resident, Barry Close, supervised its affairs and stationed Company troops within its borders. Having been reduced to a rump of Tipu Sultan's former dominions after the 1799 partition among the British, the Nizam of Hyderabad, and the Marathas, the kingdom's core lands were centered on Mysore and Seringapatam. Purnaiah used these years to restore order, rebuild finances, revive temple and religious endowments that had lapsed, and reorganize revenue administration after the upheavals of the war. The surrounding region remained unsettled, as the freebooter Dhondia Wagh raised a revolt in former Mysore territory that was crushed by British forces under Arthur Wellesley in 1800.
issues: Corrected one date error in the description: Krishnaraja Wodeyar III took personal charge of the government in 1810 (not 1811) — sources confirm he attained his majority (age 16) in early 1810 and the reins were transferred from Purnaiah to him then (Purnaiah died 1812). Changed "1811" to "1810." All other fields verified and confirmed: existedIn1800=yes (restored Wodeyar princely state, no anachronism — Tipu died 4 May 1799, Krishnaraja III crowned 30 June 1799 at Mysore); span 1799–1947 correct for the restored iteration; capital Mysore with coords (76.65, 12.3) landing on Mysore city (~12.31N, 76.65E), period-correct (Bangalore only became the seat after 1831); area ~64,000 km²/~24,800 sq mi corroborated as the commonly cited post-1799 rump figure, kept approximate; pop and gdp correctly dashed (no reliable 1800 figures). yearDesc facts all corroborated: Barry Close as first Resident 1799–1801, 1799 partition among British/Nizam/Marathas, Purnaiah's fiscal and temple-endowment restoration, and Dhondia Wagh's revolt crushed by Arthur Wellesley (killed at the Battle of Conaghull, 10 Sept 1800). Both description and yearDesc are exactly 5 sentences; yearDesc begins "In 1800,".

name: Principality of Waldeck
description: The Principality of Waldeck and Pyrmont was created on 6 January 1712 when Emperor Charles VI elevated the ancient County of Waldeck, ruled by the House of Waldeck, to princely rank under Friedrich Anton Ulrich. Its princes governed a modest immediate territory from their baroque residence at Arolsen, punctuating a chronic shortage of revenue by hiring out soldiers as mercenaries, most famously the Waldeck regiments sent to serve Britain in the American Revolutionary War. Perpetually indebted, the dynasty briefly split off the small exclave of Pyrmont to a cadet line in 1805 before the two were formally reunited in 1812. The principality survived the Napoleonic upheavals as a member of the Confederation of the Rhine and afterward the German Confederation, though its finances forced it in 1867 to sign an accession treaty placing its administration under Prussia while keeping nominal sovereignty. It endured as one of the German Empire's smallest states until the last prince, Friedrich, abdicated in November 1918, after which it became a free state and was finally absorbed into Prussia in 1929.
yearDesc: In 1800, the principality was ruled by Friedrich Karl August, Prince of Waldeck and Pyrmont, who had reigned since 1763 and still governed Waldeck and Pyrmont as a single state. A career soldier who had served as a major-general in the Dutch army until 1794, he had earlier pursued ambitious modernization after visiting England in 1775, funding road-building, agriculture, wool and iron works, and a gymnasium at Korbach. These outlays, however, had driven the small state deep into debt, a burden he had tried to relieve by leasing troops abroad, including to Britain during the American War of Independence. As an immediate principality of the still-existing Holy Roman Empire, Waldeck retained its own sovereignty in 1800, its baroque capital at Arolsen the seat of the childless, unmarried prince. Within a few years its indebtedness would force him to hive off Pyrmont to his brother Georg in 1805, on the eve of the Empire's collapse and the principality's entry into the Confederation of the Rhine.
issues: No corrections needed — the record checked out against Wikipedia (English + German), Unofficial Royalty, and 1911 Britannica. name: Qing dynasty
yearDesc: In 1800, the Qing Empire was ruled by the Jiaqing Emperor, who had assumed full personal authority only the year before upon the death of his father, the retired Qianlong Emperor, in early 1799. Immediately after taking power Jiaqing had purged and executed the immensely corrupt favourite Heshen, whose graft had drained the treasury and rotted the military. The empire was mired in the White Lotus Rebellion, a massive religious and anti-tax uprising across the mountainous borderlands of Hubei, Sichuan, and Shaanxi that had begun in the mid-1790s and would not be suppressed until around 1804. Ruled from Peking and holding roughly China proper, Manchuria, Mongolia, Xinjiang, and Tibet, the Qing remained the most populous and one of the wealthiest states on earth, though the costly rebellion and endemic corruption exposed a dynasty past its zenith. These strains marked the beginning of a long decline that would deepen through the nineteenth century.
issues: No corrections needed; every checkable claim was independently confirmed. Existence/near-peak extent in 1800 confirmed (Qing 1636–1912, greatest extent 18th c.). Official name "Great Qing (Da Qing)" confirmed. Span 1636 (Hong Taiji proclamation) – 1912 (Feb 1912 abdication) confirmed. Capital Peking/Beijing period-correct; coords 116.4E/39.9N land on Beijing. yearDesc verified: Qianlong died 7 Feb 1799, Jiaqing gained full power then (era name began 1796 but real authority in 1799, as the text says); Heshen forced to suicide 22 Feb 1799; White Lotus Rebellion 1794–1804 in Hubei/Sichuan/Shaanxi mountains, crushed ~1804 — all correct; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences starting "In 1800,". reuseExisting true so description correctly "". None reached the dash threshold.

name: Tây Sơn dynasty
officialName: Tây Sơn dynasty (Đại Việt under the Tây Sơn)
description: The Tây Sơn dynasty arose from a peasant rebellion led by three brothers from Tây Sơn village in Bình Định, with the eldest, Nguyễn Nhạc, proclaiming himself emperor in 1778 after toppling the Nguyễn lords and challenging the Trịnh lords and the Lê emperors. Its most capable figure, Nguyễn Huệ, took the throne as Emperor Quang Trung in 1788 and crushed a Qing Chinese invasion at the Battle of Ngọc Hồi-Đống Đa in early 1789, reunifying much of Vietnam under Tây Sơn authority. Quang Trung's sudden death in 1792 left the throne to his young son Nguyễn Quang Toản, who reigned as Emperor Cảnh Thịnh while real power fell to regents whose purges and infighting badly weakened the regime. This internal decay allowed the surviving Nguyễn claimant, Nguyễn Ánh, to rebuild his forces in the south and gradually reconquer Tây Sơn territory. The dynasty was destroyed in 1802 when Nguyễn Ánh captured and executed Cảnh Thịnh, founding the Nguyễn dynasty and ending Tây Sơn rule after roughly a quarter-century.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Tây Sơn dynasty was ruled by Emperor Cảnh Thịnh (Nguyễn Quang Toản) from its capital at Phú Xuân, though the regime remained fractured by the court purges and defections that had followed Quang Trung's death. Throughout that year the Tây Sơn generals Trần Quang Diệu and Vũ Văn Dũng besieged the citadel of Quy Nhơn, held by Nguyễn Ánh's officer Võ Tánh, while occupying the surrounding coast and the strategic Thị Nại lagoon, yet they could not retake the fortress for the whole year. Nguyễn Ánh, unable to break the encirclement, kept his main forces in a holding posture near the front rather than pushing deeper north. The Tây Sơn still nominally controlled central and northern Vietnam, but their leadership was divided and their long-term position was eroding against Nguyễn Ánh's resurgent southern base. Within a year Phú Xuân itself would fall in July 1801, forcing Cảnh Thịnh to flee north, and by 1802 the dynasty would be extinguished entirely.
issues: CORRECTED the yearDesc, which had the 1800 combatant roles at Quy Nhơn reversed. The original said "the fortress of Quy Nhơn became a central battleground as Tây Sơn armies struggled to hold the strategic Thị Nại lagoon" and framed Nguyễn Ánh as "pressing their offensive" there. In reality, in 1800 Quy Nhơn (renamed Bình Định after Nguyễn Ánh captured it in June 1799) was held by the NGUYỄN garrison under Võ Tánh, and it was the TÂY SƠN (generals Trần Quang Diệu and Vũ Văn Dũng) who were on the offensive — besieging the citadel and controlling the surrounding coast including Thị Nại lagoon, but unable to retake the fortress all year. Nguyễn Ánh was in a defensive/holding posture near Quy Nhơn that year. I rewrote the yearDesc to reflect the correct roles (Tây Sơn besieging the Nguyễn-held citadel, holding Thị Nại) and made Phú Xuân's fall date explicit (17 July 1801). All other fields CONFIRMED accurate against Wikipedia and corroborating sources: existence in 1800 (yes); span 1778–1802 (Nguyễn Nhạc's imperial proclamation to the 1802 destruction); capital Phú Xuân/Huế; coordinates 107.583°E, 16.467°N (Huế ≈ 16.46°N, 107.57–107.60°E, within ±0.5°); official name Đại Việt under the Tây Sơn; ruler in 1800 Cảnh Thịnh (reign name used 1793–1801, so correct for 1800). The 5-sentence political-history description was verified accurate and left unchanged. pop/area/gdp correctly DASHED — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for this short-lived wartime polity (no Maddison series for the Tây Sơn state); did not fabricate any.

description: The Viceroyalty of New Spain was the first of Spain's American viceroyalties, established by royal decree in 1535 with Antonio de Mendoza as its inaugural viceroy and Mexico City, built atop the conquered Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan, as its seat of government. Ruled by a viceroy who served as the king's direct representative and was advised by the Audiencia, it grew into the administrative heart of Spain's empire in North America, the Caribbean, Central America, and across the Pacific to the Philippines. Its wealth rested on silver mining in centers such as Zacatecas and Guanajuato, whose bullion financed the Spanish crown and flowed to Asia via the Manila galleon trade. The Bourbon Reforms of the eighteenth century reorganized its governance into intendancies, tightened royal control, and reshaped its economy and defenses. The viceroyalty was ultimately dissolved in 1821 when the Mexican War of Independence ended Spanish rule and gave rise to an independent Mexico.
name: Viceroyalty of New Spain
officialName: Viceroyalty of New Spain (Virreinato de Nueva España)
yearDesc: In 1800, New Spain was governed from Mexico City under King Charles IV of Spain, with the viceroyalty passing from Miguel José de Azanza to Félix Berenguer de Marquina, who assumed office on 30 April 1800. Berenguer de Marquina's tenure was largely uneventful and is chiefly remembered for its defensive military measures, the formation of the Regiment of Grenadiers, and the suppression of indigenous unrest such as the Indio Mariano rebellion of 1801. The viceroyalty remained the richest possession of the Spanish crown, its silver mines feeding both the treasury and the Manila galleon trade across the Pacific. Spain's entanglement in the wider Napoleonic-era wars in Europe disrupted transatlantic shipping and heightened pressures on colonial revenue and defense. Beneath the surface calm, Enlightenment ideas and creole discontent with peninsular privilege were building the tensions that would erupt into the independence movement a decade later.
issues: Corrected the yearDesc: the record claimed Berenguer de Marquina's tenure "is chiefly remembered for a serious grain shortage and famine." This is unsupported/false — the English Wikipedia and en-academic biographies of Berenguer de Marquina make no mention of any famine, and the famous New Spain "años de hambre" (famine years) were 1749–50, 1785–86, and 1809–10, NOT 1800–1801. This appears to be a conflation with the 1785–86 famine (under an earlier viceroy). Replaced that sentence with his actual notable tenure facts (defensive measures, Regiment of Grenadiers, and the Indio Mariano rebellion of 1801), all corroborated by his biographies. VERIFIED and kept: existedIn1800=yes; officialName; span 1535–1821 (founding by royal decree 1535 with Antonio de Mendoza as first viceroy; dissolved 1821 at end of the War of Independence); capital Mexico City at lon -99.13/lat 19.43 (correct city center); area ~7,657,000 km² (matches Wikipedia infobox, given for 1790); viceroy transition Azanza→Berenguer de Marquina on 30 April 1800 (confirmed); King Charles IV reigning 1788–1808. Population kept as "~6 million" with the record's caveat — corroborated by Humboldt-based estimates (~5–6M) for the present-day-Mexico core, though the Wikipedia infobox gives a higher ~7M (1790) for the whole viceroyalty; kept the lower caveated core figure as the record documented. GDP left dashed (no reliable Maddison-style figure for the viceroyalty as a distinct unit). Description left unchanged (exactly 5 sentences, political history, accurate, no numeric figures).

name: Kingdom of Naples and Sicily
officialName: Kingdom of Naples and Kingdom of Sicily (two realms in Bourbon personal union under Ferdinand IV & III)
description: Bourbon rule over the southern Italian mainland and Sicily began in 1735 when Charles of Bourbon, a son of Philip V of Spain, conquered both territories and was installed as king, founding a cadet dynasty that governed them as an absolute monarchy. When Charles inherited the Spanish throne in 1759, he left his southern realms to his young son Ferdinand, who reigned as Ferdinand IV of Naples and Ferdinand III of Sicily over two crowns kept legally distinct despite their shared sovereign. Opposition to Revolutionary France led Ferdinand to invade Rome in 1798, but a French counterthrust drove him from Naples to Palermo and enabled the proclamation of the short-lived Parthenopean Republic in January 1799. That republic collapsed within months under Cardinal Ruffo's Sanfedist counter-revolution backed by British sea power, and the Bourbon throne was restored amid harsh reprisals against the republicans. After further Napoleonic upheavals, Ferdinand ultimately merged his two kingdoms into the single Kingdom of the Two Sicilies in 1816, ending their separate existence.
yearDesc: In 1800, Ferdinand IV of Naples and III of Sicily held both Bourbon crowns, having recovered the Neapolitan mainland after the fall of the Parthenopean Republic the previous year. The king and Queen Maria Carolina still resided in Palermo, where they had fled aboard Nelson's fleet in late 1798, so the court governed the reconquered kingdom from Sicily rather than from Naples. Cardinal Ruffo's royalist forces and their British allies had consolidated Bourbon control of the mainland, and a wave of trials and executions of former republicans continued to mark the restored regime. The two kingdoms remained constitutionally separate realms bound only by their common sovereign, firmly aligned against Revolutionary France within the anti-French coalitions. The royal family would not return to Naples itself until 1802, after the Peace of Amiens temporarily relieved French military pressure.
issues: All claims independently re-verified against Wikipedia, Britannica, and reputable history sources; verdict confirmed with no value changes. existedIn1800 "partial" is correct — in 1800 the Bourbon realm was two constitutionally separate kingdoms (Naples/Sicily) in personal union under Ferdinand IV & III, and the unified "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies" was not formalized until 1816, so the single map name is genuinely anachronistic. Span 1735–1816 (Bourbon dual-kingdom personal union from Charles's 1735 installation to the 1816 merger) confirmed. Capital Naples with lon 14.27 / lat 40.85 is on the city (Naples ≈ 40.85°N, 14.27°E, within tolerance); the historically important caveat that in 1800 the king actually resided in Palermo (until his 1802 return after the Peace of Amiens) is correctly flagged in existedNote/yearDesc and left as-is. The '13 June 1799' restoration anchor in existedNote is accurate as the date Ruffo's forces entered Naples (formal capitulation followed within days; Nelson arrived 24 June) — sound, kept. Second flight to Palermo (Jan 1806) and 1802 return both corroborated. Population 'c. 6 million' corroborated (Naples mainland ~5.0M + Sicily ~1M c. 1800) and kept as approximate. Area and GDP correctly dashed — no clean, reliably sourced ~1800 combined figure exists for this composite state (no Maddison entry). Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography/number clause; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning 'In 1800,'. No unverified facts added.

name: Sultanate of Muscat and Oman
officialName: Al Bu Said Sultanate of Muscat and Oman
description: The Al Bu Said dynasty rose to power in Oman under Ahmad bin Said, elected Imam in 1744 after expelling the Persians, and his descendants gradually shifted the seat of power from the interior toward the coastal port of Muscat. Sultan bin Ahmad seized control at Muscat in 1792 following the death of his nephew Hamad bin Said, arranging a power-sharing settlement at Barka that split temporal authority on the coast from the Ibadi Imamate held by his brother Said at Rustaq. His reign focused on securing Oman's ports, navy, and Indian Ocean commerce, building a maritime domain distinct from the landward Imamate and extending Omani influence to Zanzibar and the East African coast as well as the enclave of Gwadar on the Makran coast. He deepened ties with the British East India Company through treaties in 1798 and 1800 aimed against French and Dutch commercial rivalry, while facing pressure from expanding Wahhabi forces to the north. Sultan bin Ahmad died in 1804 on an expedition toward Basra, leaving his young sons Salim and Said as heirs under a regent, after which Said bin Sultan eventually consolidated power and presided over the dynasty's imperial high point.
yearDesc: In 1800, the state was ruled from Muscat by Sultan bin Ahmad, the fourth Al Bu Said ruler, who governed the coast while his brother Said held the Imamate in the interior at Rustaq. Its power rested on maritime trade across the Indian Ocean, with Muscat serving as a thriving entrepôt linking Arabia, Persia, India, and the East African coast. In this year Sultan bin Ahmad concluded a second treaty with the British East India Company, providing for a British agent to reside at Muscat and manage relations with other states. The realm also held distant possessions including Zanzibar and the Zanj coast in East Africa and the port of Gwadar on the Makran coast. At the same time it faced mounting pressure from Wahhabi forces advancing from central Arabia, who occupied Buraimi and besieged the governor of Sohar.
issues: Corrected capitalLon 58.59 -> 58.41: authoritative Muscat coordinates are 23.589N, 58.408E (Wikipedia); the old 58.59 sat ~0.18 deg too far east of the city center. Latitude 23.61 left as-is (within tolerance of 23.589). All other fields verified and unchanged. Confirmed against Wikipedia/Britannica/Encyclopedia.com: ruler Sultan bin Ahmad r. 1792-1804 (span correct); Hamad bin Said was his NEPHEW (son of brother Said bin Ahmad), so description's "nephew" is right; Barka settlement with brother Said as Ibadi Imam at Rustaq correct; Anglo-Omani treaties of 1798 (qawl-nama) and 1800 amendment providing for a British agent resident at Muscat confirmed, and their aim against French AND Dutch commercial presence is accurate; Zanzibar/Zanj coast and Gwadar (Makran) holdings confirmed; 1800 Wahhabi invasion occupying Buraimi and besieging Qais bin Ahmad (governor of Sohar) confirmed, so yearDesc's "governor of Sohar" is accurate; death 1804 on Basra expedition with sons Salim and Said under regent Mohammed bin Nasir confirmed. existedIn1800="partial" retained: the maritime state existed but the map-name "Sultanate of Muscat and Oman" is a later (post-1856) formal title, and a co-existing interior Ibadi Imamate qualifies full statehood under that single name. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed - no reliable ~1800 figures exist for Oman (no Maddison estimate); none fabricated. name: Kingdom of Hawaii
yearDesc: In 1800, Kamehameha I ruled a nearly unified Hawaiian polity, having conquered Maui, Molokaʻi, Lānaʻi, and Oʻahu by 1795, with only Kauaʻi and Niʻihau still independent under the ruler Kaumualiʻi. His authority rested on decisive victories such as the 1795 battle of Nuʻuanu on Oʻahu and on Western firearms and advisors, notably the Englishman John Young and the Irishman Isaac Davis. The kingdom had no fixed capital in the European sense; the royal seat followed the king, who around this time held court on Hawaiʻi Island before shifting his center to Oʻahu and Honolulu Harbour in the years that followed to oversee the growing foreign shipping trade. The islands were increasingly drawn into the Pacific commerce in sandalwood and the provisioning of visiting ships, even as introduced diseases were driving a steep decline in the Native Hawaiian population. Kamehameha would complete the unification in 1810 when Kaumualiʻi ceded Kauaʻi peacefully, sparing the kingdom a final war.

Since no single verifiable fixed capital city exists for 1800, the capital name and coordinates are dashed/nulled rather than asserting a wrong one. name: United States of America
yearDesc: In 1800, the United States was a young federal republic of sixteen states led by Federalist president John Adams, whose single term was drawing to a close. The seat of government moved that year from Philadelphia to the newly built federal city of Washington in the District of Columbia, where Congress first convened in November and Adams took up residence in the still-unfinished President's House. In the bitterly contested presidential election of 1800, Adams was defeated by the Democratic-Republican ticket, but because electors did not yet vote separately for president and vice president, Jefferson and his running mate Aaron Burr finished in an electoral tie. The House of Representatives resolved the deadlock in Jefferson's favor on the thirty-sixth ballot in February 1801, and the nation's settled lands reached westward to the Mississippi River with vast territory beyond still held by Native nations, Spain, and France. The 1800 census counted a population of about 5.3 million, including roughly 893,000 enslaved people concentrated in the Southern states.

Adams was defeated outright (Jefferson 73, Burr 73, Adams 65); the electoral TIE was between Jefferson and his running mate Aaron Burr, and that Jefferson–Burr tie is what the House resolved (36th ballot, Feb 17, 1801). (federal seat relocated 1800, Congress convened Nov 1800; correct period name); lon/lat -77.04/38.9 within ±0.5° of DC (~38.90°N, 77.04°W); pop 5,308,483 and ~893,000 enslaved (1800 census, 893,602); 16 states; description "" (reuseExisting true).

name: Saint Martin
officialName: Saint Martin (French Saint-Martin, north; Dutch Sint Maarten, south)
yearDesc: In 1800, Saint Martin was a small Caribbean island that had been partitioned between France and the Dutch since the 1648 Treaty of Concordia, with the French holding the northern portion and the Dutch the southern. After the Dutch Republic collapsed into the French-client Batavian Republic in 1795, French forces took control of the whole island, so both the French north and the nominally Dutch south stood under French occupation. The island's economy rested on salt harvesting from its ponds and on sugar, cotton, and tobacco estates worked by a large enslaved African majority. Marigot served as the principal town of the French side and Philipsburg, founded in 1763 around the salt-pond fort on Great Bay, as the chief town of the Dutch side. British forces would seize the entire island in March 1801, so 1800 fell in a brief final interval of French wartime control before that occupation.

name: Oyo Empire
description: Founded by legend by Oranyan, the last prince of Ile-Ife, the Oyo Empire grew from a Yoruba kingdom into West Africa's dominant power, ruled by a sacred monarch titled the Alaafin. Royal authority was constitutionally checked by the Oyo Mesi, a council of seven notables led by the Bashorun, and by the Ogboni society, giving Oyo an unusually balanced political structure. From the late sixteenth century its cavalry armies subjugated hundreds of tributary states, extending Oyo's reach over neighbouring Yoruba lands and the kingdom of Dahomey. Internal crisis set in when the powerful Bashorun Gaha dominated the throne until Alaafin Abiodun executed him in 1774, after which Abiodun favoured Atlantic commerce over the army and left the empire militarily weakened. Successive revolts, the secession of Ilorin under the war-chief Afonja, and Fulani incursions unravelled the empire, culminating in the abandonment of Oyo-Ile around 1835.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Oyo Empire was a Yoruba imperial state in advancing decline, its central authority badly shaken by the recent forced suicide of Alaafin Awole. The throne passed through weak and contested successions, and provincial governors and tributaries increasingly ignored or defied the capital at Oyo-Ile. The rebellion of Afonja, the Aare Ona Kakanfo based at Ilorin who commanded much of Oyo's cavalry, was pulling the empire's military heart away from the Alaafin's control. Pressure from the Muslim north and the loss of tribute and trade revenue steadily eroded the imperial economy and army. Though still nominally sovereign over a wide swath of Yorubaland and its neighbours, Oyo in 1800 was fragmenting toward the collapse that would destroy its capital within a generation.

name: Kaarta
description: Kaarta was a Bambara kingdom ruled by the Massassi (Coulibaly) clan, first founded around 1650 near Sounsan and re-established in 1754 by Sey Bamana Coulibaly after a Segou-led invasion had destroyed the earlier state and killed its ruler Foulakoro. Under Faama Sira Bo, who reigned from 1761 to 1781, it re-emerged as a major regional power, fixed a permanent capital at Diangounté, and subjugated neighboring polities such as Diarra and Khasso. Political authority was tightly held by the Massassi lineage, giving Kaarta unusually smooth successions compared with its great rival Segou, though the two Bambara states waged repeated wars for dominance of the western Sudan. Sira Bo's successor Desse Koro was defeated by Mansong Diarra of Segou, and the kingdom's power later revived under Bodian Moriba, who reigned from 1818 to 1832 and made peace with Segou. Kaarta was destroyed as an independent force in 1854 when El Hadj Umar Tall's Tijaniyya jihad seized Nioro and executed the last faama, Mamady Kandian, ending the Massassi dynasty.
yearDesc: In 1800, Kaarta was an independent Bambara kingdom of the western Sahel governed by the Massassi (Coulibaly) clan from its capital at Diangounté. Its ruler in this period was Faama Desse Koro, who had succeeded Sira Bo after 1781, though his precise regnal dates are not securely recorded. Kaarta was locked in rivalry with the neighboring Bambara Empire of Segou, then at the height of its strength under Mansong Diarra, who ruled Segou from about 1790 to 1808. Desse Koro was defeated in battle by Mansong, marking a low point in Kaarta's fortunes as Segou pressed its campaigns against it. Despite this setback the kingdom endured as a distinct Bambara state and would recover its regional standing under Bodian Moriba a generation later.

Capital Diangounté correct and period-correct (fixed by Sira Bo r.1761-1781, in place by 1800); coordinates 14.545/-9.513 verified within ±0.5° against Diangounté Camara (getamap 14.55/-9.51667; commune 14.5448/-9.5127). name: Kingdom of Cambodia
description: The Post-Angkor Kingdom of Cambodia emerged after the abandonment of Angkor in the fifteenth century, ruled by a Khmer monarchy that shifted its capitals to Longvek and, from 1618 under Chey Chetha II, to Oudong. From the seventeenth century onward the kingdom was reduced to a contested buffer, its throne repeatedly disputed by rival princes who sought backing from the more powerful Siam and Vietnam. Ang Eng was installed as king with Siamese support and crowned at Bangkok, ceding Battambang and Siem Reap to Siam, and after his death in 1796 a regency governed until his son Ang Chan II was crowned in 1806. Ang Chan II leaned toward Vietnam to secure his throne against Siamese-backed brothers, triggering the invasions and rebellions of 1811–1813 that shifted Cambodia from Siamese into Vietnamese dominance. The kingdom endured decades of foreign intervention and near-annexation before Ang Duong and his successor Norodom turned to France, culminating in the French protectorate established in 1863.
yearDesc: In 1800, Cambodia was a weak kingdom caught in an interregnum, its throne vacant of a crowned monarch since Ang Eng's death in 1796. In 1800, the boy-heir Ang Chan II remained under Siamese guardianship and had not yet been allowed to take the throne, while Prince Pok served as regent and governed the country. In 1800, the royal capital was Oudong, the seat of the Khmer court since the early seventeenth century. In 1800, Cambodia acknowledged the overlordship of both Siam and Vietnam, paying tribute to each and functioning as a buffer between the two expanding powers. In 1800, the provinces of Battambang and Siem Reap remained detached under Siamese control, having been ceded during Ang Eng's reign.
issues: CORRECTED pop from "c. 2,090,000" to "—". That figure is contradicted by the record: multiple sources state Cambodia's population "hovered below 1 million" through the 19th century, with the earliest census-era figure ~946,000 (some sources 853,000) in 1874, rising to only ~2.4 million by 1921. A ~2,090,000 estimate for 1800 is implausible and uncorroborated (it exceeds the 1874 figure by >2x), so it is dashed rather than left standing. name: Sultanate of Aussa
description: The Sultanate of Aussa was founded in 1734 when Data Kadafo, head of the Mudaito clan of the Afar, seized power and established the Mudaito dynasty, succeeding the earlier Imamate of Aussa. It emerged as the leading Afar monarchy, and other Afar chiefs nominally acknowledged the primacy of its sultan. Its rulers governed a Muslim polity in which the sovereign consolidated authority through control of trade routes, agriculture along the Awash, and a martial defense of the capital against outside raiders. Through the nineteenth century the sultanate preserved its independence from the Khedivate of Egypt and remained autonomous within the expanding Ethiopian Empire. It endured until the Italian invasion of 1936, when it was absorbed into Italian East Africa during the Second Italo-Ethiopian War.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Sultanate of Aussa was ruled by Sultan Aydahis ibn Kadhafo Mahammad, who reigned from 1779 until 1801. The Mudaito dynasty he belonged to had held power for roughly two generations, and the state functioned as an independent Afar monarchy centered on its capital. During this decade its ruler organized the defense of the capital, reportedly recruiting matchlock-armed men from Aden in Yemen to repel attackers. Contemporary European accounts described the sultan as a brave and martial ruler whose armory included cannons and matchlocks. The sultanate stood outside any imperial control at this date, dominating the Afar lowlands as the foremost Afar power.
issues: No changes — all claims independently verified against Wikipedia (Sultanate of Aussa, Mudaito dynasty, Asaita) and corroborating sources. The 1800 ruler Aydahis ibn Kadhafo Mahammad (r. 1779–1801) is confirmed by both the Sultanate and Mudaito-dynasty ruler lists, spanning 1800. The late-18th-c. recruitment of Yemen matchlockmen from Aden, the arsenal of cannons/matchlocks, and William Cornwallis Harris's "brave and martial sultan" account are all in the Wikipedia article; Harris's "Yusuf ibn Idjahis" is a garbled rendering of Aydahis (the reigning sultan), so attributing the account to Aydahis in yearDesc is correct. name: Kingdom of Great Britain
description: The Kingdom of Great Britain was created on 1 May 1707 when the Treaty of Union merged the Kingdom of England and the Kingdom of Scotland into a single state governed by one Parliament at Westminster. It was ruled successively by the last Stuart monarch Anne and then the Hanoverian kings George I, George II, and George III, with real executive power resting increasingly on ministers commanding a Commons majority. Its politics were shaped by Whig ascendancy, recurring wars with France and Spain, the suppression of the Jacobite risings of 1715 and 1745, and the loss of the thirteen American colonies confirmed in 1783. Under George III and William Pitt the Younger it consolidated a powerful fiscal-military state, an expanding overseas empire, and a parliamentary system dominated by a landed and mercantile elite. The kingdom ceased to exist as a separate entity when the Acts of Union 1800 joined it with the Kingdom of Ireland to form the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland on 1 January 1801.
yearDesc: In 1800, Great Britain was ruled by King George III with William Pitt the Younger serving as Prime Minister and dominant political figure. In 1800, the kingdom was locked in the War of the Second Coalition against Revolutionary France, sustaining a continental struggle largely through naval power and subsidies to allies. In 1800, its Parliament passed the Act of Union with Ireland, and the Irish Parliament passed its counterpart, arranging the merger that would create the United Kingdom on 1 January 1801. In 1800, wartime taxation, harvest failures, and high grain prices strained the population, while Pitt's earlier introduction of income tax in 1799 helped finance the war effort. In 1800, the country stood at the leading edge of early industrialization, with growing textile manufacturing, coal and iron output, and expanding maritime commerce underpinning its global reach.

name: Maratha Confederacy
description: Founded when Shivaji Bhonsle was crowned Chhatrapati in 1674, the Maratha polity grew from a Deccan resistance state into the dominant power of eighteenth-century India. After Aurangzeb's death effective authority passed from the Chhatrapati at Satara to the hereditary Peshwas of the Bhat family, under whom Baji Rao I and Madhavrao I carried Maratha arms across northern and central India. The catastrophic defeat at the Third Battle of Panipat in 1761 checked expansion, and over the following decades the leading houses of Scindia, Holkar, Bhonsle, and Gaekwad hardened into semi-autonomous princes bound loosely to the Peshwa in a confederacy rather than a centralized empire. The death of the veteran minister Nana Fadnavis in 1800 removed the last figure able to hold the chiefs together, leaving them to pursue rival ambitions and drift toward open conflict. Defeated by Holkar and driven to seek British protection through the Treaty of Bassein in 1802, the confederacy was dismantled through the Second and Third Anglo-Maratha Wars, ending with its annexation by the East India Company in 1818.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Maratha Confederacy remained the largest indigenous power in India but was gravely weakened by internal division and rivalry among its chiefs. Baji Rao II held the office of Peshwa at Pune, though real authority increasingly rested with the ambitious general Daulat Rao Scindia. The death of Nana Fadnavis that same year stripped the confederacy of its last unifying statesman and left the houses of Scindia, Holkar, Bhonsle, and Gaekwad pursuing self-interested and mutually hostile policies. This disunity created the opening the British East India Company sought, as no single leader could coordinate the confederacy's still-formidable armies. Within two years Yashwant Rao Holkar would defeat the Peshwa at the Battle of Poona, driving Baji Rao II into the fateful alliance with the British that hastened the confederacy's collapse.

name: Kingdom of Bhutan
officialName: Druk Yul (the theocratic state of Bhutan under the dual system of government)
description: Bhutan was unified in 1616 by the exiled Tibetan lama Ngawang Namgyal, the Zhabdrung, who repelled repeated Tibetan invasions and welded rival valleys and monastic factions into a single state. He established a dual system of government, the Chösi, dividing authority between a spiritual head embodied in the Zhabdrung line and a temporal ruler, the Druk Desi or Deb Raja, with the Je Khenpo leading the monastic order. After the Zhabdrung's death was concealed for decades and successor incarnations proved weak, real power drained away into recurring civil wars and the rivalry of regional governors, the penlops of Paro and Trongsa chief among them. The Druk Desi, nominally elected for a three-year term, was in practice the strongest contending governor, and central authority over the penlops steadily eroded through the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. This fractious dual order endured until 1907, when the penlop of Trongsa, Ugyen Wangchuck, abolished it and founded the hereditary Wangchuck monarchy.
yearDesc: In 1800, Bhutan was ruled by Druk Namgyel, the twenty-second Druk Desi, who held office from 1799 to 1803 under the enduring dual system. The state was internally unstable, with the office of Druk Desi weakened by short tenures, contested elections, and the growing autonomy of provincial penlops and dzongpöns. Following the 1774 peace treaty with the British East India Company after the Cooch Behar conflict, Britain had displaced Tibet as the chief external power on Bhutan's southern frontier, though relations remained cautious and distant. Bhutan continued to administer poorly demarcated tracts along the Bengal Duars, a source of friction that would later erupt into war. The government moved seasonally between its winter seat at Punakha and Thimphu, sustaining the Buddhist theocratic order the Zhabdrung had founded even as centralized control frayed.

name: Kanara
officialName: Canara District, Madras Presidency (British East India Company)
description: The Canara district was created by the British East India Company in 1799, carved from the coastal territory seized when Tipu Sultan of Mysore was defeated and killed at the fall of Seringapatam on 4 May 1799. This tract had been absorbed into Mysore by Hyder Ali in the 1760s and held by Tipu after him, having earlier been governed by the Keladi Nayakas under the shadow of the fallen Vijayanagara Empire. The Company organised the annexed coast as a single collectorate within the Madras Presidency and appointed Captain Thomas Munro as its first Collector, tasking him with establishing revenue administration and British authority. Munro's tenure laid groundwork for the ryotwari land-revenue system of direct settlement between the state and individual cultivators that later spread across the Madras Presidency. The unified district endured until 1859, when it was bifurcated into North and South Canara, with North Canara subsequently transferred to the Bombay Presidency.
yearDesc: In 1800, Canara was a newly formed British collectorate on the south-western Indian coast, barely a year old under Company rule. Its first Collector, Captain Thomas Munro, administered the district from Mangalore and continued in that post until October 1800, imposing order and a fresh revenue settlement on territory only just wrested from Mysore. The population was still adjusting to the transfer of sovereignty from Tipu Sultan's regime to the East India Company, whose overarching authority in the region had been consolidated by Governor-General Richard Wellesley. As part of the Madras Presidency, the district answered to Company officials at Madras rather than to any indigenous ruler. It remained a single undivided Canara district, decades away from the 1859 split into North and South Canara.

name: Kong Empire
description: The Kong Empire was founded around 1710 by Seku Watara, a Dyula merchant-warrior claiming descent from the Malinké Keita lineage, who united rival Dyula war-leaders and overthrew Lasiri Gbambele to seize the trading city of Kong. Under Seku and his brother Famagan the state expanded rapidly through the savanna, seizing towns and trade routes as far as Bobo-Dioulasso and campaigning westward toward Segou. Governed by Muslim Dyula merchants and the Watara "war houses," it functioned less as a centralized kingdom than as an instrumental state built to protect commerce in gold and kola nuts while becoming a renowned center of Islamic scholarship. After Seku's death around 1745 no successor could master the whole Watara clan, and the empire fragmented into a constellation of semi-independent chiefdoms bound by nominal loyalty to Kong. Weakened by internal rivalries and later pressure from the Kenedougou kingdom, it fell to Samory Touré, whose forces sacked and burned Kong in 1897–1898, ending the empire.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kong Empire was ruled by the fama Mori Maghari, who had succeeded Koumbi Ouattara and maintained Kong's prestige as a hub of trade and Islamic learning. In 1800, the state remained a loose confederation of Watara war-houses and Dyula merchant communities, controlling key trans-savanna routes carrying gold and kola nuts rather than a tightly unified realm. In 1800, the death of Mori Maghari in that very year is conventionally marked as the beginning of the empire's tribulations, as authority passed to weaker successors. In 1800, ethnic and religious groups within the empire were increasingly organizing resistance that eroded the central power of the Kong ruling house. In 1800, the empire nonetheless still held its core territory in the northeastern savanna and its walled capital continued to function as a flourishing commercial and scholarly center.

name: Oromo states
officialName: Oromo states (the Gibe kingdoms and Gadaa confederacies of the southern Ethiopian highlands)
description: The Oromo states were not a single polity but a cluster of communities and emerging kingdoms in the southern Ethiopian highlands, where the Oromo had settled during their great sixteenth-century migrations and long governed themselves through the egalitarian, generation-based Gadaa system. From the later eighteenth century, competition over land, cattle, and the reviving long-distance caravan trade produced ambitious war leaders who converted the office of moti from a temporary Gadaa war chief into a hereditary monarchy, borrowing the concept of sacral kingship in part from the neighbouring kingdom of Kaffa. The earliest of these Gibe kingdoms was Limmu-Ennarea, consolidated around 1800-1802 by Bofo, known as Abba Gomol, followed over the next generation by Gumma under Oncho Jilcha, Gomma, Jimma under the Abba Jifar dynasty, and Gera under Tullu Gunji. These states grew wealthy as entrepôts on the routes carrying gold, coffee, ivory, and enslaved people toward the Red Sea and Gojjam, and Islam spread among their ruling classes and merchants. Their independence ended in the late nineteenth century when Emperor Menelik II's expanding Ethiopian Empire conquered and annexed them, with Limmu-Ennarea absorbed by 1891 and Jimma retaining tributary autonomy until 1932.
yearDesc: In 1800, the region was overwhelmingly Oromo country still ordered by the Gadaa system, with hereditary monarchy only beginning to crystallize at its northwestern edge. In 1800, Bofo, called Abba Gomol, was rising to power among the Limmu Oromo and, around 1800-1802, welding scattered clans into the Kingdom of Limmu-Ennarea from his capital at Sappa, having overrun the older Ennarea state whose Gonga-speaking ruling elite had taken refuge in Kaffa. In 1800, the other Gibe monarchies of Gumma, Gomma, Jimma, and Gera had not yet been founded, so no unified bloc of Oromo kingdoms existed and much of the surrounding highland remained governed by Gadaa councils rather than kings. In 1800, the reviving caravan trade in gold, coffee, ivory, and slaves was already drawing Muslim merchants into the Gibe basin and enriching the emerging chiefs who controlled its markets. In 1800, these communities lay beyond the reach of the Christian Ethiopian state to their north, which was itself fragmented in the decentralized Zemene Mesafint, leaving the Oromo of the Gibe fully autonomous.
issues: Corrected one unverified/incorrect claim in yearDesc sentence 2: the original said Bofo cemented his position "through a marriage alliance with a lineage of the older Ennarea state." Sources (Sewasew/Wikipedia on Limmu-Ennarea) show the opposite — the incoming Limmu Oromo CONQUERED the older Ennarea (Enarea) kingdom and its Gonga-speaking political elite fled to Kaffa (displacement, not alliance); the Kaffa marriage alliance was a later act by Bofo's SON, Abba Bagibo, not Bofo himself. Replaced with the verified fact that Bofo ruled from his capital Sappa having overrun the old Ennarea state. All other claims CONFIRMED against Wikipedia (Gibe region, Kingdom of Limmu-Ennarea, Saqqa, Zemene Mesafint), Sewasew, and Mohammed Hassen's dating: existedIn1800 = partial (Oromo firmly established; only Limmu-Ennarea c. 1800-1802 exists as a monarchy, Gumma 1810s, Gomma 1820s, Jimma 1830, Gera 1835); Bofo = Abba Gomol (a.k.a. Abbaa Gomol I), reigned to 1825; Kaffa kingship borrowing; Gadaa-to-moti transition; 16th-c migration; Menelik II conquest 1886-1900, Limmu-Ennarea annexed 1891, Jimma tributary until 1932; Zemene Mesafint (1769-1855) decentralization. Pop/area/GDP DASHED — not reliably sourceable for c. 1800. Description unchanged (5 sentences, all verified, no geography/number clause); yearDesc remains 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,".

name: Sikh Confederacy
officialName: Sikh Confederacy (the Punjab misls under the Sarbat Khalsa)
description: The Sikh Confederacy was formally established on 29 March 1748 when the Sarbat Khalsa passed a gurmata reorganizing the scattered Sikh militias into a structured alliance of twelve sovereign misls, each led by its own sardar. It was governed collectively rather than by a single monarch: the biannual Sarbat Khalsa assembly at Amritsar reached binding decisions by gurmata, and the combined armies formed the Dal Khalsa, led successively by Nawab Kapur Singh, Jassa Singh Ahluwalia, and later jathedars. Through the later eighteenth century the misls expelled Mughal and Afghan authority from most of the Punjab, though the individual chiefs frequently warred among themselves for territory and revenue. Beginning in the 1790s Ranjit Singh of the Sukerchakia Misl used marriage alliances, diplomacy, and conquest to subdue his rivals, capturing Lahore in July 1799. This consolidation dissolved the confederal structure, which gave way to Ranjit Singh's centralized Sikh Empire proclaimed in 1801, while the Phulkian states to the southeast kept a separate existence and later became British-allied princely states.
yearDesc: In 1800, the old confederacy of twelve independent misls had effectively ceased to function as a collective body, its authority passing to Ranjit Singh of the Sukerchakia Misl. In 1800, Ranjit Singh held Lahore, which he had seized the previous year and made his seat of power, though he still governed nominally as its Afghan-confirmed governor rather than as a sovereign. In 1800, he was actively subduing or allying with the remaining misls of central Punjab, backed by his mother-in-law Rani Sada Kaur of the Kanhaiya Misl. In 1800, the region remained a patchwork of Sikh-ruled territories not yet welded into a single administration, with the Cis-Sutlej and Phulkian chiefs still autonomous. In 1800, Ranjit Singh had not yet been formally proclaimed Maharaja, an event that would follow on Vaisakhi in April 1801.

name: Portuguese Guinea
officialName: Rios de Guiné (Portuguese possessions in Guinea, a dependency of the Captaincy-General of Cape Verde)
yearDesc: In 1800, the Portuguese presence in Guinea consisted of a handful of coastal enclaves, chiefly the fortified towns of Bissau and Cacheu, administered from afar as a dependency of Portuguese Cape Verde whose seat lay at Santiago. Bissau, refounded and given a rebuilt stone fort, the Fortaleza de São José da Amura, by the Grão-Pará and Maranhão Company in 1775, had become the main garrison and slave-trading center. Cacheu, one of the oldest European settlements in the region, remained a second fortified post further north on its river. Real Portuguese control did not extend past the range of these forts, and the surrounding Papel, Balanta, and other peoples remained independent, with attacks on and killings of Portuguese agents recurrent. The settlements existed largely to tax and ship enslaved Africans across the Atlantic, above all to Brazil, and their prosperity rose and fell with that trade.

name: Kingdom of Imerina
description: The Kingdom of Imerina was founded around 1540 by Andriamanelo, who began subjugating the earlier Vazimba communities of the central highlands and established a dynasty that ruled from a succession of fortified hill towns. Under later kings such as Ralambo and Andrianjaka the realm consolidated, adopted the name Imerina, and made Antananarivo a royal seat, but a partition by Andriamasinavalona in the late 17th century plunged the kingdom into roughly seventy-seven years of civil war among rival principalities. Andrianampoinimerina, who seized power around 1787 by deposing his uncle Andrianjafy, reunified the fragmented kingdom by 1795 and became its most celebrated ruler, codifying laws, organizing markets and irrigation, and building a conscript army with the ambition of uniting the whole island. His son Radama I opened the kingdom to British alliance, Christian missionaries, and modernization, extending Merina dominance over much of Madagascar, while the long reign of Ranavalona I later resisted European influence. The kingdom endured as the dominant Malagasy state until France conquered the island and abolished the monarchy in 1897, exiling the last sovereign, Ranavalona III.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Imerina was ruled by Andrianampoinimerina, roughly midway through his reign of about 1787 to 1810. In 1800, he governed a reunified Imerina from Antananarivo, having moved his political capital there in 1794 while Ambohimanga remained the sacred royal town. In 1800, he pressed a program of consolidation and expansion, absorbing or pressuring neighboring highland peoples such as the Betsileo, Sihanaka, and Bezanozano while the coastal Sakalava resisted. In 1800, his administration rested on codified customary law, controlled markets with standardized measures, extensive irrigated rice cultivation, and a corvée-and-conscription system famously expressed in his call for an army of the "hundred thousand." In 1800, he is credited with major building works at Antananarivo, including the reconstruction of the Besakana royal residence, marking the highland kingdom's rise as the paramount power on Madagascar.
issues: No corrections needed — every claim independently verified against Wikipedia (Merina Kingdom, Andrianampoinimerina, Andriamasinavalona) and Britannica. existedIn1800=yes confirmed: Andrianampoinimerina reigned c.1787–1810, reunified Imerina by 1795, actively expanding in 1800. Span 1540–1897 confirmed (Andriamanelo founding ~1540; French annexation 1897). officialName "Fanjakan'Imerina" is the attested native Malagasy name. Capital Antananarivo correct and period-accurate (political capital moved there 1794); coordinates 47.52/-18.88 sit on the actual city (Antananarivo ≈ -18.88 to -18.91, 47.52), a correct replacement for the off-target highland centroid 47.1/-20.1 that was supplied. name: Margraviate of Baden
description: The House of Baden, a branch descended from the Swabian Zähringen dynasty, had held the margravial title since the twelfth century, but the territory was long divided, splitting in 1535 into the Catholic Baden-Baden and the Protestant Baden-Durlach lines. This reunified iteration was created in 1771 when Charles Frederick of Baden-Durlach, who had ruled since 1738, inherited Baden-Baden upon the extinction of its ruling line and consolidated the fragmented lands under a single Protestant ruler. Charles Frederick governed as an enlightened reformer, abolishing serfdom in his lands and cultivating close ties with leading thinkers of the age. Through shrewd diplomacy backed by France and by Alexander I of Russia, he positioned the small state to profit from the upheavals of the Revolutionary and Napoleonic era. The Margraviate's existence ended in 1803 when the Imperial Diet's mediatisation settlement elevated Charles Frederick to Prince-Elector, transforming the state into the Electorate of Baden, which in turn became the Grand Duchy of Baden in 1806.
yearDesc: In 1800, Baden was a compact Margraviate ruled by the aged Charles Frederick from his purpose-built capital at Karlsruhe. Its lands lay scattered along the upper Rhine as one of the many small principalities of the Holy Roman Empire, hemmed in by larger neighbours. Charles Frederick's enlightened administration was already well established, having pursued legal reform, education, and agricultural improvement over decades of rule. The wars of the French Revolution had brought French armies to the Rhine, and Baden manoeuvred diplomatically to preserve and enlarge itself amid the collapse of the imperial order. Within three years these efforts would bear fruit, as the mediatisation of 1803 vastly expanded Baden's territory and raised it to an Electorate.

name: central Asian khanates
officialName: The Central Asian and Caucasus Khanates (Emirate of Bukhara, Khanates of Khiva and Kokand, Kazakh Hordes, and Caucasus khanates)
description: These successor states arose from the fragmentation of Timurid and Shaybanid Central Asia, as new Uzbek tribal dynasties seized the settled oasis khanates during the eighteenth century: the non-Chingissid Manghits formalized the Emirate of Bukhara under Shah Murad in 1785, the Qungrats consolidated power in Khiva, and the Ming dynasty built the Khanate of Kokand from the Fergana Valley after breaking away from Bukhara around 1709. Alongside these settled emirates, the nomadic Kazakhs were divided into three hordes—the Great, Middle, and Junior jüz—whose khans had sworn largely symbolic allegiance to the Russian crown from 1731 while remaining effectively self-governing. Rulers such as Kokand's Alim Khan expanded aggressively, seizing Tashkent and Khujand, while Bukhara, Khiva, and Kokand waged chronic wars against one another over oases, caravan routes, and control of the Amu Darya and Syr Darya basins. The states were governed as Islamic monarchies in which emirs and khans ruled through tribal aristocracies, the ulama, and Sharia courts, and their economies rested on caravan trade, agriculture, and a notorious slave market. Encircled by expanding Russian and, in the Caucasus, Persian and Russian power, these polities were progressively conquered or reduced to protectorates across the nineteenth century, with Kokand annexed outright in 1876 and Bukhara and Khiva surviving as Russian vassals until 1920.
yearDesc: In 1800, the region was divided among rival Muslim states rather than unified under any single throne. In Bukhara the Manghit founder Shah Murad died and was succeeded by his son Emir Haydar, a pious ruler devoted to the Naqshbandi Sufi order who leaned heavily on scholars and religious judges. In Kokand, Alim Khan had recently taken power following the death of Narbuta Biy in 1799 and was beginning the ruthless expansion that would make him the first of his line to claim the title of khan. Khiva was governed by the Qungrat inaqs, who held real power on behalf of puppet khans and would openly assume the khanship under Iltuzar in 1804. The three Kazakh hordes ranged across the northern steppe under their own sultans and khans, nominally under Russian suzerainty but in practice autonomous, while the small Caucasus khanates maneuvered between Persian and advancing Russian influence.

Kokand founded 1709 by Shahrukh Biy (Ming tribe) breaking from Bukhara; Alim Khan took power after Narbuta Biy's death 1799, was first of his line to claim 'khan', and seized Tashkent (c.1810) and Khujand — confirmed. name: Swabian Circle
capitalName: Ulm (seat of the circle diet; no true capital)
description: The Swabian Circle was created in 1500 under Emperor Maximilian I as one of the administrative circles into which the Holy Roman Empire was divided, with the full set of ten circles fixed by 1512. It never had a single ruler but was jointly directed by two co-directors, the Prince-Bishop of Constance and the Duke of Württemberg, whose delegates convened the circle diet to coordinate imperial taxation, defense, coinage regulation, and the keeping of the peace. Comprising dozens of small ecclesiastical states, free imperial cities, counties, and lordships, it was among the most fragmented yet best-organized of the circles, maintaining an effective collective government and even fielding its own army from 1694. It repeatedly mobilized troops and levied contributions during the wars against France, and its cohesion made it a notable example of small-state cooperation within the Empire. The circle was shattered by the secularization and mediatization of the Reichsdeputationshauptschluss in 1803, dissolved with the Holy Roman Empire in 1806, and formally abolished in 1808.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Swabian Circle remained a working institution of the Holy Roman Empire, still binding together a patchwork of roughly ninety small territories in the southwest. In 1800, only the Duchy of Württemberg, the Margraviate of Baden, and the Prince-Bishopric of Augsburg carried real weight among its many minor members, while the co-directorship rested with the Bishop of Constance and the Duke of Württemberg. In 1800, its diet continued to meet at the imperial city of Ulm to organize contributions and troops, and the circle was caught up in the wars of the Second Coalition against Revolutionary France. In 1800, French victories brought fighting and occupation to Swabian lands, straining the circle's finances and defensive obligations. In 1800, the circle stood on the eve of the upheaval that the 1803 mediatization would bring, when most of its ecclesiastical states and free cities were absorbed by Württemberg and Baden.

name: Kingdom of Prussia
description: The Kingdom of Prussia was proclaimed in 1701 when the Hohenzollern Elector of Brandenburg, Frederick III, crowned himself King Frederick I, elevating the scattered Brandenburg-Prussian lands into a monarchy. Under Frederick William I and especially Frederick II "the Great," it built a formidable centralized bureaucracy and standing army, seized Silesia from Austria, and shared in the partitions of Poland, becoming one of Europe's great powers. Defeat and dismemberment by Napoleon after Jena-Auerstedt in 1806 spurred sweeping Stein-Hardenberg reforms, after which Prussia helped lead the coalition that overthrew him. Through the nineteenth century it drove German unification under Bismarck, defeating Denmark, Austria, and France, until its king became German Emperor in 1871. The kingdom endured as the dominant state of the German Empire until the monarchy was abolished amid revolution at the close of the First World War in 1918.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Prussia was ruled by King Frederick William III, who had succeeded to the throne in 1797. It held to a policy of armed neutrality in northern Germany that it had maintained since the 1795 Peace of Basel, staying out of the ongoing War of the Second Coalition against revolutionary France. In December 1800 it joined the Second League of Armed Neutrality alongside Russia, Denmark, and Sweden to resist British naval interference, a coalition that soon collapsed in 1801. Its government rested on an entrenched royal bureaucracy and a powerful professional army, though the reforms that would later modernize the state lay still ahead. This decade of neutrality spared much of central, eastern, and northern Germany from war until Prussia's catastrophic defeat by Napoleon in 1806.

Reliable late-1790s figures are ~8.5M (Encyclopedia.com, after 1795) and 8.7M (Wikipedia Kingdom of Prussia infobox, 1797) — inconsistent with 9.7M and none pinned to exactly 1800, so the unsupported value is dashed per policy. name: Northern Circars
officialName: The Northern Circars (Company territory, Madras Presidency)
description: The Northern Circars were a string of coastal Telugu districts that the British East India Company wrested from French influence after Lieutenant-Colonel Francis Forde captured Masulipatam in 1759, and which the Company held as a revenue territory rather than a sovereign realm. In 1765 the Mughal emperor Shah Alam II granted the five Circars to the Company, a cession confirmed by the Nizam of Hyderabad in the treaties of 1766 and 1768, under which the Nizam received an annuity in exchange for relinquishing the tract. Guntur, held for life by the Nizam's brother Basalat Jang, was excluded until his death in 1782 and only passed to Company administration in 1788, completing the group. The Company governed the region through Chiefs and Councils seated at towns such as Masulipatam until these were abolished in 1794 and the territory reorganized into collectorate districts. The Nizam's residual claims were finally purchased outright in 1823, and the Circars were later absorbed into the districts of the Madras Presidency, their separate identity fading by the mid-nineteenth century.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Northern Circars were firmly under British East India Company administration as part of the Madras Presidency, with the Nizam of Hyderabad retaining only a nominal residual claim settled by annuity. The Chiefs and Councils that had earlier governed the tract had been abolished in 1794, and Company officials were moving toward a district-based collectorate system that would be formalized in 1802. Revenue policy in these years leaned on settlements with local zamindars, with permanent-settlement arrangements modeled on Bengal being attempted for hereditary landlords. The region remained peaceful under Company control, its acquisition having come largely through diplomacy and financial settlement rather than direct conquest. Masulipatam continued as the principal port and long-standing administrative town, while Guntur, incorporated only in 1788, had by now been fully integrated into the Circars.

(3) span 1765–1859 kept — 1765 grant to 1859 dissolution is a defensible, well-sourced representation of the tract's distinct existence (Wikipedia's infobox alternatively frames the *division* as 1823–1947, but the record's span better captures the Circars as an entity). (4) CAPITAL — judgment call left as Masulipatam: Wikipedia's infobox names Eluru as capital of the post-1823 division, but for an 1800 snapshot (post-1794 abolition, pre-1823 division) there was no single formal capital; Masulipatam was the historic EIC Chief-and-Council seat and principal port (and a district HQ from 1802), making it the more period-accurate pin than the anachronistic post-1823 Eluru. NOT changed. (5) Coordinates 81.13E/16.17N VERIFIED as an exact match for Machilipatnam/Masulipatam. name: Sultanate of Darfur
description: The Sultanate of Darfur was ruled by the Keira dynasty of the Fur people, whose founder Sulayman Solong is traditionally credited with establishing the Muslim state in the seventeenth century after supplanting the earlier Tunjur rulers. Its sultans built a centralized administration over a mosaic of Fur and other communities, using Islam and control of the trans-Saharan trade in slaves, ivory, and other goods to project authority across a decentralized realm. The kingdom reached its apogee under Sultan Abd al-Rahman al-Rashid and his son Muhammad al-Fadl, who reigned into the nineteenth century and consolidated the dynasty's grip on the region and its Arab tribes. The sultanate fell in October 1874 when its forces were defeated and Sultan Ibrahim was killed at the Battle of al-Manawashi, and it was annexed by the slave-trader and warlord al-Zubayr Rahma Mansur. A revived Keira sultanate under Ali Dinar existed from 1898 until 1916, when it was conquered and absorbed by Anglo-Egyptian forces.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Sultanate of Darfur was an independent Muslim state ruled by Sultan Abd al-Rahman al-Rashid of the Keira dynasty, at or near the peak of its power. In 1800, the sultan governed from the newly fixed capital at El Fasher, which he had established around 1790 in place of the dynasty's earlier itinerant royal camps. In 1800, the sultanate was engaged in wider diplomacy, having received the honorific title al-Rashid from the Ottoman sultan in 1792 and, in 1799, having written to congratulate Napoleon Bonaparte on his campaign in Egypt. In 1800, its wealth rested on the trans-Saharan caravan trade running north toward Egypt, exporting slaves, ivory, and other goods in exchange for manufactured wares. In 1800, the aging Abd al-Rahman was nearing the end of his reign, and he would die in 1801 and be succeeded by his son Muhammad al-Fadl.

(5) yearDesc facts confirmed: Ottoman title/embassy 1791–92, 1799 Napoleon congratulation letter, death 1801, succession by Muhammad al-Fadl. name: Zaydi Imamate
description: The Qasimid Zaydi Imamate was proclaimed in 1597 by Imam al-Mansur al-Qasim, who launched a revolt against Ottoman rule and founded a hereditary line of Zaydi Shia imams drawn from his descendants. His successors al-Mu'ayyad Muhammad and al-Mutawakkil Isma'il drove the Ottomans entirely out of Yemen by the 1630s and expanded the state to its greatest extent, reaching from Asir in the north to Zafar in the east. From the late seventeenth century the imamate steadily contracted as family disputes weakened central authority, tribal and regional rulers such as the Sultanate of Lahej broke away, and the collapse of the Yemeni coffee monopoly sapped its revenues. The rising Wahhabi movement stripped the state of its coastal Tihama possessions in the early nineteenth century, and Egyptian intervention under Muhammad Ali further eroded imamate power, after which the throne changed hands with great frequency and several imams were assassinated. The independent Qasimid polity effectively ended when the Ottomans reincorporated Yemen into their empire around 1849, though Zaydi imams continued to contest authority in the highlands for decades afterward.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Zaydi Imamate was ruled from Sana'a by Imam al-Mansur Ali I, who had reigned since 1775. His government was visibly decaying, as the aging imam left day-to-day affairs to his ministers while court intrigue mounted and real power in Sana'a was held by his wazir Hasan al-Ulufi. The state still controlled the Zaydi highlands but faced the rapidly expanding Wahhabi movement pressing in from the north and the vulnerable Tihama coast. Within a few years, from 1803 onward, pro-Wahhabi forces under Sharif Hamud would seize the coastal lowlands, a loss that lay just ahead of this moment. The imamate thus stood in 1800 as a shrinking but still-functioning polity, its authority fraying at the edges even as Sana'a remained its recognized center.

Capital Sana'a with lon 44.21 / lat 15.35 is correct for the city (period-correct name). name: Nova Scotia
description: Nova Scotia became firmly British by the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713 and was re-founded as a settler colony in 1749 when Edward Cornwallis established Halifax as its capital in place of Annapolis Royal. In 1758 it convened an elected House of Assembly, the first representative legislature in what would become Canada, giving it a tradition of colonial self-government alongside a Crown-appointed governor and council. The influx of roughly thirty thousand Loyalists after the American Revolution transformed the colony and prompted London to partition it in 1784, carving off New Brunswick and Cape Breton Island as separate colonies. Governed in this period by lieutenant governors such as John Parr and Sir John Wentworth, the colony saw recurring friction between the executive and an increasingly assertive Assembly over patronage and control of spending. It remained a distinct British North American colony, later winning responsible government in 1848, until it entered Canadian Confederation in 1867.
yearDesc: In 1800, Nova Scotia was a self-standing British colony administered from Halifax, its Loyalist-swollen society now a generation removed from the Revolution. Sir John Wentworth, appointed lieutenant governor in 1792 and made a baronet in 1795, presided over the executive while working with an elected House of Assembly. Halifax served as a major Royal Navy base and imperial garrison, giving the colony strategic weight during the wars against Revolutionary France. Wentworth's later years brought growing conflict with assembly leaders, notably William Cottnam Tonge, over who should control expenditure on roads and patronage. New Brunswick and Cape Breton, detached in 1784, remained separate colonies, so 1800 Nova Scotia was smaller than the province of a few decades earlier or later.
issues: CORRECTED one factual figure in the description. Original read "roughly fourteen thousand Loyalists ... transformed the colony and prompted London to partition it in 1784," but 14,000 is specifically the number of Loyalists who settled in present-day NEW BRUNSWICK (the part split off), not the influx into Nova Scotia as a whole. Sources (Wikipedia History of Nova Scotia; Dictionary of Canadian Biography) put the total Loyalist influx into the pre-partition colony at ~30,000 (of whom ~14,000 went to New Brunswick, leaving ~16,000 in peninsular Nova Scotia); some sources say ~33,000–34,000. Changed to "roughly thirty thousand Loyalists" (kept as words, not a stat-grid number) so the figure that "transformed the colony and prompted partition" reflects the whole influx, not just the New Brunswick share. All other claims CONFIRMED: existedIn1800=yes (Province of Nova Scotia was a British colony in 1800, governed from Halifax); officialName "Province of Nova Scotia" correct; span 1784–1867 kept (coherent framing of the post-1784-partition iteration — the reduced colony minus New Brunswick and Cape Breton — through Confederation); Halifax capital, founded 1749 by Cornwallis replacing Annapolis Royal (Treaty of Utrecht 1713); coords -63.57/44.65 on Halifax (within tolerance); 1758 first elected assembly (first in what became Canada) verified; 1784 partition creating New Brunswick + separating Cape Breton (reannexed to NS 1820) verified; Wentworth lieutenant governor from 14 May 1792, baronet 16 May 1795 verified (DCB, Wikipedia); Tonge conflict over road/appropriation spending verified (DCB); responsible government 1848 (first British colony) and Confederation 1 July 1867 verified.
