Note retained: the map name "Nova Scotia" bundles New Brunswick, but by 1800 they were legally separate colonies — the polity properly means Nova Scotia minus New Brunswick and Cape Breton.

name: Mossi kingdoms
officialName: Mossi kingdoms (paramount realm: the Kingdom of Ouagadougou under the Mogho Naba)
description: The Mossi kingdoms arose in the Volta basin from cavalry-borne conquerors whose foundation traditions trace to Princess Yennenga and her son Ouédraogo, with the Ouagadougou dynasty conventionally dated to the ruler Oubri around the late eleventh century and the principal kingdoms consolidating by roughly the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. They never formed a single centralized empire but functioned as a set of autonomous kingdoms—chiefly Ouagadougou, Yatenga, Tenkodogo, Boussouma, and Fada N'gourma—bound by shared descent, ritual, and military ties, with the Mogho Naba of Ouagadougou recognized as paramount arbiter while the northwestern kingdom of Yatenga rivaled him and acted largely independently. Governed through a hierarchy of territorial chiefs and a court of titled ministers, the Mossi famously resisted incorporation into the great Islamic states of the Sahel, repelling Songhai and Sahelian pressures and retaining their indigenous religious and political order for centuries. From the late eighteenth century their rulers increasingly accommodated Islam—the Mogho Naba Doulougou permitted a mosque and an imam at Ouagadougou—though the kings blended Muslim recognition with traditional authority rather than converting the realm. Weakened by internal rivalries and sporadic warfare among the kingdoms through the nineteenth century, the Mossi states fell to French conquest in 1896-1897, when Ouagadougou was occupied and its ruler Wobgho deposed, ending their independence.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Mossi kingdoms were fully independent, comprising about twenty autonomous polities in the Volta basin with Ouagadougou, Yatenga, Tenkodogo, and Boussouma the most prominent. In 1800, the paramount Mogho Naba at Ouagadougou—most likely Naba Doulougou, whose reign is variously dated to the years around 1783-1802 or 1796-1825—held nominal suzerainty and arbitrated disputes among the central and southern kingdoms. In 1800, the northwestern kingdom of Yatenga remained effectively independent of Ouagadougou and a standing rival, so no single throne ruled all the Mossi. In 1800, Islam was gaining a foothold at court through Yarse Muslim traders, with a mosque and an imam permitted at Ouagadougou under Doulougou, even as the kings upheld their traditional religion and rites. In 1800, the kingdoms remained untouched by European rule, that conquest lying nearly a century in the future.

name: Senegal
officialName: Établissements français du Sénégal (French Establishments of Senegal — the posts of Saint-Louis and Gorée)
yearDesc: In 1800, "Senegal" existed only as a pair of small French trading posts on the coast — the island town of Saint-Louis at the mouth of the Senegal River and the island of Gorée off the future Dakar — while the interior was ruled by independent African kingdoms such as Waalo, Cayor, and Futa Toro. In 1800, both posts were still in French hands, contrary to the notion that they were already under British occupation; Britain would not seize Gorée until April 1801 nor Saint-Louis until July 1809. In 1800, Saint-Louis served as the administrative seat, home to a French garrison and a mixed community of métis traders and the influential Afro-French signares. In 1800, the local economy remained bound up in the Atlantic slave trade alongside gum arabic exported through the river trade, though the trade would soon come under pressure from abolition. In 1800, France's presence was confined to these fortified enclaves and a few river factories, exercising no territorial control over the Senegalese mainland.
pop: Saint-Louis ~5,000 (late 18th c., excl. transient enslaved); Gorée small

name: Kingdom of Cochin
description: The Kingdom of Cochin arose from the Perumpadappu Swaroopam ruling house in the early twelfth century after the breakup of the later Chera realm, governed by a matrilineal Hindu dynasty. From the sixteenth century it repeatedly sought European protection to counter the rival Zamorin of Calicut, aligning first with the Portuguese, then coming under Dutch East India Company suzerainty from 1663. Overrun by Mysore under Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan in the later eighteenth century, the raja was forced into tributary submission before turning to the British. The forceful reformer Rama Varma IX, remembered as Sakthan Thampuran, ruled from 1790 to 1805, centralizing authority, curbing feudal chiefs such as the Paliath Achan, and signing the 1791 Powney Treaty that made Cochin a subsidiary state of the British East India Company. It survived thereafter as a princely state under British paramountcy until it merged with Travancore in 1949.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Cochin was ruled by Rama Varma IX, popularly called Sakthan Thampuran, who reigned from 1790 until his death in 1805. It was a subsidiary and tributary state of the British East India Company under the treaty concluded with George Powney on 6 January 1791, having earlier been forced to pay tribute to Mysore. Sakthan Thampuran had shifted the seat of administration to Thrissur, where he resided at the Vadakkechira Palace and developed the town as the kingdom's economic and cultural centre. His reign was marked by administrative centralization, the suppression of overmighty feudal lords, and the reorganization of trade and temple affairs. The kingdom was internally consolidated but externally dependent, its foreign relations and territorial integrity now bound to British protection.

name: Paraguay
officialName: Intendancy of Paraguay (Intendencia del Paraguay, also Intendancy of Asunción)
yearDesc: In 1800, Paraguay was the Intendancy of Paraguay, an interior province of the Spanish Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata governed from Buenos Aires. In 1800, it was administered by an Intendant Governor seated at Asunción, its sole city and capital, with the surrounding territory organized into the districts of Concepción, Curuguaty, Villarrica, and San Pedro de Ycuamandiyú. In 1800, the province remained a relatively isolated, cattle-raising and yerba-mate-producing frontier, its commerce taxed and channeled downriver through Buenos Aires. In 1800, the former Jesuit Guaraní missions—secularized after the Jesuit expulsion of 1767—had declined, and Spanish authority faced ongoing tension with indigenous peoples on its northern and Chaco frontiers. In 1800, Paraguay was still more than a decade from the 1811 revolution that would end Spanish rule and make it an independent republic.

name: Kingdom of Kongo
officialName: Kingdom of Kongo (Reino do Congo; Kongo dya Ntotila)
description: The Kingdom of Kongo emerged around 1390 from the union of KiKongo-speaking peoples under the founding king Lukeni lua Nimi, and its rulers bore the title manikongo. From the early sixteenth century its kings converted to Catholicism and cultivated close diplomatic ties with Portugal, while the ntotila governed a centralized realm of provinces from the capital. Defeat by the Portuguese at the Battle of Mbwila in 1665 shattered central authority and unleashed a decades-long civil war between the rival houses of Kinlaza and Kimpanzu. Pedro IV reunified the kingdom by 1709 through a rotational succession pact meant to alternate the throne between the two houses, but the monarchy never recovered its former power and the provinces grew increasingly autonomous. The much-weakened kingdom persisted through the nineteenth century until it became a Portuguese vassal in 1857, and Portugal formally abolished its monarchy in 1914.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Kongo was a fragmented shadow of its former self, nominally reunified but weakened by a century of dynastic conflict. In 1800, the throne was held by Henrique II Masaki ma Mpanzu, who reigned from 1794 to 1803 under a fragile arrangement dividing the succession among competing branches. In 1800, real authority was fractured, with provinces such as Soyo and Nsundi acting as de facto independent powers beyond the king's control. In 1800, the kingdom remained a Catholic monarchy oriented toward Portugal, and the transatlantic slave trade continued to drain and destabilize its population. In 1800, the seat of the diminished court remained the capital São Salvador, which had never regained the prominence lost during the civil-war destruction of the late seventeenth century.
issues: Corrected one factual error in the description: the founder was named "Lukeni lua Nsanze," but Lukeni lua Nsanze was the mother (daughter of the ruler of Mbata), NOT the founder. The actual founding king of Kongo was Lukeni lua Nimi (c. 1380–1420, reigned c. 1390). Changed "under the founder Lukeni lua Nsanze" to "under the founding king Lukeni lua Nimi" (confirmed on Wikipedia's Kingdom of Kongo and List of rulers of Kongo). All other claims confirmed and kept: existedIn1800 = yes (kingdom persisted with a king at São Salvador); officialName; span 1390–1857 (Wikipedia infobox gives vassal 1857–1910, monarchy abolished 1914); capital São Salvador with lon 14.248 / lat -6.268 essentially exact for M'banza-Kongo (actual ~14.248°E, -6.267°S); 1800 ruler Henrique II Masaki ma Mpanzu (r. 10 Jan 1794–1803) verified in List of rulers of Kongo, succeeded by Garcia V (1803); Battle of Mbwila 1665 with António I killed; Pedro IV reunification 1709; Kinlaza/Kimpanzu houses — all confirmed. pop/area/gdp left dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for this fragmented polity. yearDesc verified (5 sentences, each begins "In 1800,"); description now 5 sentences, no geography clause, no numeric figures.

name: Kingdom of Dahomey
description: The Kingdom of Dahomey emerged around 1600 as an offshoot of the royal dynasty of Allada, with Houegbadja traditionally regarded as its foundational king for building the royal palaces at Abomey and beginning the conquest of neighboring peoples. Under King Agaja it expanded dramatically in the early eighteenth century, conquering Allada and Whydah to gain direct access to the Atlantic coast, though this expansion drew it into subordination to the powerful Oyo Empire, to which it paid tribute from the 1730s. Dahomey developed a highly centralized state with a professional standing army, an organized bureaucracy and taxation, and a famous corps of female soldiers, financing itself largely through warfare and the export of war captives as slaves. King Ghezo, who seized the throne in 1818, later broke free of Oyo tributary status and gradually shifted the economy toward palm oil as the Atlantic slave trade declined. After fierce resistance the kingdom was defeated by France in the Franco-Dahomean Wars of the 1890s, reduced to a protectorate in 1894, and fully annexed as a French colony by 1904.
yearDesc: In 1800, Dahomey was ruled by King Adandozan, who had come to the throne in 1797 with the early years of his reign managed by a regency of elder statesmen. The kingdom remained a militarized slave-trading power centered on its capital at Abomey, exporting captives through the coastal port of Ouidah. It continued to owe tribute to the Oyo Empire, whose renewed strength soon constrained Adandozan's raiding campaigns against rival ports. Adandozan attempted to revive the flagging slave trade through military raids and, when those largely failed, turned to diplomatic pressure on European and Brazilian traders and to agricultural reforms. His harsh and increasingly unpopular rule sowed the divisions that would culminate in his overthrow by his brother Ghezo in 1818.
issues: No changes; all claims independently verified and confirmed against Wikipedia (Adandozan, Dahomey) and corroborating sources. Existence in 1800: confirmed — Dahomey was a functioning independent Fon kingdom under King Adandozan (r. 1797–1818), still an Oyo tributary. Ruler: Adandozan came to the throne May 1797 following his father Agonglo's assassination; a regent held power for roughly the first seven years, so a regency was operative in 1800 (Wikipedia states he gained full authority c. 1804) — yearDesc claim confirmed. Capital: Abomey confirmed; coordinates lon 1.99 / lat 7.18 match actual Abomey (7.183 N, 1.991 E) within tolerance. Span: c. 1600 founding (Houegbadja conventionally foundational king) and 1904 full French annexation (protectorate 1894) both confirmed. Description dates cross-checked: Agaja conquered Allada (1724) and Whydah (1727) = "early eighteenth century"; tribute from 1730s (~1732); Ghezo seized throne 1818 and ended Oyo tribute later (1823); shift to palm oil as slave trade declined — all confirmed; exactly 5 sentences, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP figures. yearDesc: exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,"; Oyo reasserting power to block his raids (by ~1805), 1804 diplomacy to Portugal/Britain plus the Brazilian (de Sousa) dimension, agricultural reform, and overthrow by brother Ghezo in 1818 — all corroborated. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable c. 1800 figures exist for Dahomey; none fabricated.

name: Principality of Schaumburg-Lippe
description: The County of Schaumburg-Lippe was created in 1647 when the larger County of Schaumburg was partitioned among Hesse-Kassel, Brunswick-Lüneburg, and the House of Lippe following the extinction of the Holstein-Schaumburg male line, with the Lippe branch retaining Bückeburg. Ruled by a cadet line of the House of Lippe, it survived as one of the smallest immediate territories of the Holy Roman Empire, its independence repeatedly secured through appeals to imperial law against powerful neighbours such as Hesse-Kassel and Hanover. Its most celebrated ruler, Count Wilhelm, modernized the state's army and fortifications in the mid-18th century, giving the tiny county an outsized military reputation. In 1807 the county was raised to the Principality of Schaumburg-Lippe as it joined Napoleon's Confederation of the Rhine, and it later entered the German Confederation, the North German Confederation, and the German Empire. The monarchy endured until the German Revolution of 1918, when it became the Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe, which was ultimately absorbed into Lower Saxony in 1946.
yearDesc: In 1800, Schaumburg-Lippe was still a county, not yet a principality, ruled nominally by the young Count Georg Wilhelm, who had succeeded as an infant in 1787. Because he was a minor, the county was governed by a regency; his mother Juliane of Hesse-Philippsthal had died in November 1799, after which Count Johann Ludwig von Wallmoden-Gimborn served as regent. The county remained an immediate fief of the Holy Roman Empire, its fragile autonomy having recently been defended before the imperial courts against encroachment by Hesse-Kassel. Its capital and princely residence was at Bückeburg, with Stadthagen as a secondary town. It would be another seven years before the county was elevated to a principality and Georg Wilhelm assumed full personal rule.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified against Wikipedia and reputable sources. existedIn1800 = "partial" CONFIRMED: in 1800 it was the County (Grafschaft) of Schaumburg-Lippe, elevated to a principality only on 15 Dec 1807 upon joining the Confederation of the Rhine, so the map label "Principality" is genuinely anachronistic for 1800. officialName, span (1647 county creation via partition after the Holstein-Schaumburg extinction; 1807 end of county iteration), and capital Bückeburg all confirmed. Bückeburg coords (52.261N, 9.049E) match actual (52.2607, 9.0492) within tolerance. Description = 5 political-history sentences, no geography/number clauses — all facts verified (1647 partition among Hesse-Kassel/Brunswick-Lüneburg/Lippe; Count Wilhelm's mid-18th-c. army and fortification reforms incl. Wilhelmstein and early conscription; 1807 elevation; German/North German Confederation and German Empire; 1918 Free State; 1946 into Lower Saxony). yearDesc = 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," — Georg Wilhelm born 1784, succeeded Feb 1787 as an infant, mother Juliane died Nov 1799 (sources: 9 Nov 1799), Wallmoden-Gimborn regent thereafter, full rule from 1807: all confirmed; Stadthagen confirmed as the only other town. area c. 340 km² CONFIRMED by multiple sources. pop dashed (no reliable ~1800 figure; sources give 1861 ~29,000, later 40,000+ but nothing for 1800) — correct not to fabricate. gdp dashed (no figure for so minor a state) — correct.

name: Sultanate of Aïr
description: The Sultanate of Aïr was founded in 1405 by Tuareg confederacies who invited an outside arbiter, Yunus, as their first sultan to mediate inter-tribal disputes, establishing a monarchy whose ruler was styled amenukal or sultan. The state was conquered by the Songhai Empire in the early sixteenth century and later fell under the authority of Kebbi, but it recovered its independence after the collapse of Songhai power around 1591. Its sultans held limited authority over the drum-groups of the Tuareg confederation, serving as judges and war-leaders while internal disputes remained frequent. During the eighteenth century, the southward migration of northern Tuareg confederacies into Aïr provoked recurring unrest and contributed to a long political and economic decline. The sultanate came under French suzerainty in 1906, and after the failed Kaocen revolt of 1916–1917 its sultan was reduced to a French-appointed mediator, though the office endures today within Niger.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Sultanate of Aïr persisted as an autonomous Tuareg monarchy under its sultan at Agadez, but it was a much-diminished successor to the prosperous trading state of earlier centuries. Its economy still rested on the trans-Saharan caravan trade, particularly the salt and date routes linking Agadez to the Bilma and Fachi oases and the markets of Hausaland to the south. The sultan's authority remained limited and largely mediatory, exercised over often fractious Tuareg drum-groups rather than as a centralized state power. The eighteenth-century influx of northern Tuareg groups had left the region politically unsettled, deepening the decline that would leave Agadez a shadow of its former self by the time Heinrich Barth visited in 1850. No European power yet held sway over the region, French colonial control lying more than a century in the future.
issues: No changes made; all claims independently corroborated against Wikipedia (Sultanate of Agadez article) and geographic sources. existedIn1800 "yes" confirmed — autonomous Tuareg polity between Songhai's 1591 collapse and French suzerainty in 1906, no anachronism. Founding 1405 (first sultan Yunus), Songhai conquest early 16th c., Kebbi authority, 1591 independence, 1906 French control, and Kaocen revolt 1916–1917 all confirmed. Capital Agadez is period-correct with the correct 1800-era name; supplied coordinates (7.9908°E, 16.9722°N) match actual Agadez (~7.9911°E, 16.9733°N) to within ~0.002°, well inside tolerance — the original map centroid (8.1, 20.7) was far north of the real capital, but the record already uses the correct Agadez coordinates. officialName (Sultanate of Aïr / Sultanate of Agadez) is a documented formal name. Verdict: confirmed.

name: Malay sultanates
description: The Malay sultanates were a family of independent Muslim monarchies that traced their prestige to the Malacca Sultanate, founded around 1400 and shattered by the Portuguese conquest of 1511. From the fallen dynasty sprang Johor and Perak, while older states such as Kedah and the Borneo sultanate of Brunei ran their own royal lines, each ruled by a sultan claiming descent or legitimacy within this shared Malay-Muslim political order. Over the eighteenth century the balance of power shifted repeatedly: Bugis migrants seized the office of Yang di-Pertuan Muda and dominated Johor-Riau, Siam pressed the northern states of Kedah and Kelantan into tributary vassalage, and Brunei's once-vast Bornean empire steadily decayed through succession strife and lost trade. The sultanates were never politically unified but formed a common cultural sphere bound by Islam, the Malay language, and intermarrying dynasties. From 1786 onward British and Dutch encroachment progressively reduced them to protected states, though the sultanate institution itself survived and endures in Malaysia and Brunei today.
yearDesc: In 1800, the peninsula and coastal Borneo were divided among these separate sultanates rather than any single kingdom, with no common ruler or capital. The premier line, Johor-Riau-Lingga under Sultan Mahmud Shah III, had moved its seat to Daik on Lingga in 1788 but was effectively governed by the Bugis Yang di-Pertuan Muda based in the Riau islands. Kedah, Kelantan and Terengganu lay under Siamese suzerainty, while Selangor and Perak remained shaped by Bugis and dynastic rivalries, and Brunei's authority over Borneo had contracted sharply. The British East India Company held Penang, ceded from Kedah in 1786, as a growing free port and naval station on the sultanates' northwestern flank. The stage was thus set for the nineteenth-century partition of this Malay world between British and Dutch spheres of influence.
issues: Corrected one anachronism in yearDesc sentence 2: the Bugis Yang di-Pertuan Muda was NOT "based at Penyengat" in 1800. Penyengat was only given as a royal dowry c. 1803-1804 and the YDM seat relocated there from Ulu Riau (Bintan) under YDM Jaafar (r. 1806-1832), in the early 19th century. In 1800 the Bugis viceroy's seat was at Ulu Riau/Riau Lama on Bintan. Changed "based at Penyengat" to the safe, accurate "based in the Riau islands" (the YDM governed Riau/Bintan and dependencies). Everything else confirmed against sources: existedIn1800 yes (Johor-Riau-Lingga, Kedah, Perak, Selangor, Terengganu, Pahang, Kelantan, Brunei etc. all independent in 1800); Malacca founded c.1400, fell to Portugal 1511, spawned Johor + Perak (Wikipedia/Britannica); Sultan Mahmud Shah III moved capital to Daik, Lingga in 1788; Bugis held the Yang di-Pertuan Muda office in Johor-Riau; Kedah/Kelantan/Terengganu paid bunga mas tribute under Siamese suzerainty (tribute resumed 1786 after Ayutthaya's fall); Selangor a Bugis-founded sultanate (1766); Brunei contracted to NW Borneo by c.1800; Penang ceded from Kedah to the EIC in 1786 and renamed Prince of Wales Island (Sultan Abdullah Mukarram Shah). Description is 5 sentences of political history with no geography/number clauses; yearDesc is 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". officialName kept as the collective descriptor (no single formal name for a network of polities). Capital left blank/null and pop/area/gdp dashed — correct, no single seat and no reliable aggregate 1800 figures exist. Span 1400-Present retained (Malacca origin to present-day Malaysia/Brunei).

name: Nguyễn domain
officialName: Domain of the Nguyễn Lord (Nguyễn Vương), southern Vietnam / Gia Định
description: This southern Vietnamese domain grew from the cause of Nguyễn Phúc Ánh, a surviving prince of the Nguyễn lords whose family had been overthrown by the Tây Sơn rebellion in the 1770s. After years as a fugitive he recaptured Saigon in 1788 and made Gia Định his permanent base, proclaiming himself Nguyễn Vương and rallying southern landowners, Chinese merchants, and Catholic and French supporters including Bishop Pigneau de Behaine. Through the 1790s he built a disciplined army and modern navy, funded by the productive Mekong delta, and waged a protracted civil war against the Tây Sơn dynasty. From 1799 he pressed northward, taking Quy Nhơn, Phú Xuân, and eventually the Tây Sơn heartland. In 1802 he captured Huế and Hanoi, reunified the country, and proclaimed himself Emperor Gia Long, transforming this wartime domain into the imperial Nguyễn dynasty.
yearDesc: In 1800, Nguyễn Ánh ruled a consolidated southern realm centered on Gia Định (Saigon), controlling the Mekong delta and the coast up toward the contested central provinces. In that year his forces were locked in the drawn-out siege of Quy Nhơn against the Tây Sơn, whose armies had marched south to besiege the Bình Định citadel his general Võ Tánh held on the central coast. He commanded a formidable military of tens of thousands of troops and a large fleet, supplied through trade in delta rice and sugar for European weapons and materiel. He still held the title Nguyễn Vương rather than emperor, governing as a claimant restoring his family's legitimacy against the Tây Sơn regime in the north. Within two years these campaigns would culminate in the fall of Huế and Hanoi, reunification, and his enthronement as Gia Long.
issues: CORRECTED the yearDesc's second sentence. Original claimed that in 1800 "his loyalists pushed as far north as Quảng Bình and the Gianh River" — this is an anachronism/unverified: the Gianh River (northern Quảng Bình) was the old 17th–18th-c. Trịnh–Nguyễn boundary, not Nguyễn Ánh's 1800 front. In 1800 his front line was at Quy Nhơn/Bình Định on the central coast; Phú Xuân (Huế, south of Quảng Bình) was not captured until 3 May 1801, so his forces had not reached, let alone passed, Quảng Bình in 1800. Also clarified the siege direction: in 1800 the Tây Sơn (Trần Quang Diệu, Vũ Văn Dũng) marched SOUTH to besiege the Nguyễn-held Quy Nhơn/Bình Định citadel defended by Võ Tánh — the original "locked in the drawn-out siege of Quy Nhơn against the Tây Sơn" was ambiguous about who besieged whom, now made explicit and accurate. VERIFIED and kept: existedIn1800=yes with correct anachronism note (dynasty founded 1802 as Gia Long, ruled 1800 as Nguyễn Vương); officialName (descriptive, no formal state name existed); span 1780–1802 (1780 = self-proclaimed Nguyễn Vương); capital Gia Định/Saigon at 106.7,10.78 (correct city and coordinates); 5-sentence description (accurate political history, Saigon recaptured 1788, Pigneau de Behaine, unification 1802); pop/area/gdp DASHED (no reliable isolated figures for this mid-war southern fragment). The "tens of thousands of troops / large fleet" phrasing in yearDesc is a general, broadly corroborated magnitude, not a hard grid figure, so retained.

name: Kingdom of Sardinia
officialName: Kingdom of Sardinia (States of His Majesty the King of Sardinia)
description: The Kingdom of Sardinia in its Savoyard form was created by the Treaty of The Hague in 1720, when the House of Savoy under Victor Amadeus II was compelled to exchange Sicily for the island of Sardinia and its royal title. The Savoyard rulers governed a composite monarchy that joined the island to their far more populous and wealthy mainland domains of Savoy, Piedmont, Aosta, and Nice, with the ruling class, government, and cultural life centred on the peninsula. Through the eighteenth century the dynasty pursued administrative centralization and cautious reform while maintaining Turin as the heart of the state. Drawn into the Revolutionary Wars against France, the kingdom was defeated by Napoleon in 1796 and saw its mainland occupied, its king exiled to the island, and Piedmont ultimately annexed to France. Restored after Napoleon's fall, the House of Savoy later transformed this kingdom into the engine of Italian unification, its monarch becoming King of Italy in 1861 and thereby ending the Sardinian title.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Sardinia survived only as a de jure state, its Piedmontese and Savoyard mainland having been occupied by French forces since 1798 and formally reorganized toward annexation. In 1800, King Charles Emmanuel IV lived as an exile in central and southern Italy, having left the island of Sardinia in September 1799 for Florence, Rome, and Naples, while the island itself remained beyond French reach and was governed in his name by a viceroy. In 1800, the Battle of Marengo in June restored French control over Piedmont after a brief Austro-Russian occupation of Turin the previous year. In 1800, the king clung to the hope of a mainland restoration that Austrian and later French dominance repeatedly frustrated. In 1800, the wandering Savoyard court represented a dynasty in retreat, two years before Charles Emmanuel IV freely abdicated in favour of his brother Victor Emmanuel I.
issues: CORRECTED two false claims that the king was on the island / "ruling from Cagliari" in 1800. Sources (Wikipedia: Charles Emmanuel IV of Sardinia) establish he reached Cagliari on 3 March 1799 but LEFT the island by September 1799, landing at Livorno on 22 Sept 1799, and spent 1800–1802 as an exile in central/southern Italy (Villa del Poggio Imperiale near Florence, then Rome, Frascati, Naples, Caserta) — he was NOT on Sardinia in 1800, and never ruled directly from Cagliari for any sustained period. Rewrote yearDesc sentence 2 (was: "resided on the island of Sardinia, ruling from Cagliari... governed directly by its king rather than a viceroy") to reflect his exile in mainland Italy with the island governed by a viceroy in his name. Rewrote yearDesc sentence 5 ("surviving Savoyard court on the island") to "wandering Savoyard court," removing the false island claim; kept the verified "two years before... freely abdicated... Victor Emmanuel I" (abdication 4 June 1802 was voluntary, prompted by his wife's death). CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800 yes; officialName "States of His Majesty the King of Sardinia" (attested formal name); span 1720–1861 (Treaty of The Hague 17 Feb 1720; Kingdom of Italy proclaimed 17 March 1861); Battle of Marengo 14 June 1800 restoring French control after 1799 Austro-Russian occupation; 1798 French occupation forced Charles Emmanuel IV's abdication of mainland territories. Description kept (exactly 5 sentences, accurate, no numeric figures). Capital kept as Turin per the source anchor's "de jure capital Turin" convention and correct coords (7.6869, 45.0703); NOTE: Wikipedia actually lists Cagliari as the formal de jure capital 1324–1847 and Turin as the de facto administrative seat, so the reasoning in the original 'confidence' note (that the king ruled from Cagliari in 1800) was itself unsound, but the Turin marker for the Piedmont-centred Savoyard state is retained per the anchor. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable single 1800 figure for this occupied composite monarchy.

name: Principality of Lippe
officialName: Fürstentum Lippe (Principality of Lippe, also called Lippe-Detmold)
description: The state traced its origins to the medieval Lordship of Lippe, first attested in 1123 under Bernhard I, which was elevated to an Imperial County in 1528 under Simon V and reunited under the Detmold line, whose princes ruled from 1720. In 1789 the County of Lippe-Detmold was raised to a Principality of the Holy Roman Empire, and Leopold I became its first prince. Leopold I was declared mentally incapacitated in 1790 and placed under imperial guardianship, so government was exercised on his behalf during much of his reign until his death in 1802. The house was ruled through the nineteenth century by a succession of princes, mostly named Leopold, and remained a small but sovereign member of the German Confederation and later the German Empire. The principality endured until 12 November 1918, when Prince Leopold IV abdicated in the German Revolution and the state became the Free State of Lippe.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Principality of Lippe was a small sovereign state of the Holy Roman Empire ruled by Prince Leopold I from his residence at Detmold. Because Leopold I had been declared incapacitated by a mental disorder in 1790, the affairs of the principality were managed under an arrangement of guardianship that had been conditionally eased in 1795. As one of the many minor principalities of Westphalia, Lippe belonged to the Lower Rhenish–Westphalian Circle and held imperial immediacy within the Empire. Its status as a principality, granted only in 1789, was still recent, and the ruling House of Lippe had already split into several branches with the main line seated at Detmold. Within a few years the wider order would be overturned, as Leopold I died in 1802 and the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire in 1806 pushed Lippe to join Napoleon's Confederation of the Rhine in 1807.
issues: No corrections needed; every claim was independently re-verified and confirmed. existedIn1800 "yes" — the County of Lippe-Detmold was raised to a Principality of the HRE in 1789 (Leopold I becoming first prince), so it existed as the Principality of Lippe in 1800 (Wikipedia: Principality of Lippe; Leopold I, Prince of Lippe). officialName, span (1789–1918, ended 12 Nov 1918 with Leopold IV's abdication → Free State of Lippe), and capital Detmold (capital 1468–1918, name unchanged in 1800) all confirmed. Coordinates 8.8772/51.9375 match modern Detmold (sources cluster ~51.936–51.938, ~8.873–8.879), well within ±0.5°. Description: exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause or numeric figures; the medieval chain (1123 Bernhard I, 1528 Imperial County under Simon V, Detmold princes from 1720 per the 1720 elevation of Simon Heinrich Adolf), 1789 elevation, 1790 incapacity/guardianship/1802 death, and 1918 end all corroborated. yearDesc: exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,"; Lower Rhenish–Westphalian Circle membership, imperial immediacy, 1795 conditional easing, 1802 death, 1806 HRE dissolution and 1807 Confederation of the Rhine accession all confirmed. pop dashed — no reliable 1800 census exists (nearest documented figure is much later; even the record's cited 1816 census of 80,728 could not be independently corroborated, so dashing is correct). area kept as ≈1,215 km² — the 1,215 km² figure is a stable 19th/early-20th-century (1910) measurement for boundaries that were essentially unchanged, appropriately marked approximate. gdp dashed — no Maddison or comparable figure exists for this micro-state.

name: Kingdom of Portugal
officialName: Kingdom of Portugal and the Algarves
description: The Kingdom of Portugal traced its origin to 1139 when Afonso Henriques was proclaimed king, but the polity of this era dated from 1640, when the Duke of Braganza was acclaimed João IV and the Portuguese Restoration War ended sixty years of dynastic union with Spain. Under the House of Braganza the crown consolidated a global maritime empire while at home an absolutist monarchy governed through a small court nobility. The mid-eighteenth-century ministry of the Marquis of Pombal centralized royal authority, expelled the Jesuits, and rebuilt the capital after the 1755 earthquake, leaving a lasting imprint on the state. By the turn of the nineteenth century the monarchy was bound to a long-standing alliance with Britain and increasingly strained by the wars of Revolutionary and Napoleonic Europe. The dynasty survived a flight to Brazil in 1807 and later constitutional upheavals, and the Braganza monarchy endured until it was overthrown by the republican revolution of 1910.
yearDesc: In 1800, Portugal was ruled in name by the incapacitated Queen Maria I, with her son Prince John governing as regent after formally assuming the title in 1799. The kingdom clung to its ancient alliance with Britain while resisting mounting pressure from Napoleonic France and its Spanish ally to close its ports to British trade. This diplomatic tightrope would break the following year in the brief War of the Oranges, when a Franco-Spanish invasion forced Portugal to cede Olivença to Spain. The court remained at Lisbon and continued to draw great wealth from Brazil, whose gold and colonial commerce underpinned royal finances. The metropolitan population stood at roughly three million, and the country's economy had been in relative decline since the mid-eighteenth century.
issues: All claims independently re-verified against Wikipedia and reputable history sources; no corrections needed, verdict confirmed. CONFIRMED: (1) Polity existed in 1800 as an independent kingdom — Queen Maria I nominally reigned but was declared insane (1792, deemed incurable by Dr. Francis Willis); her son João governed and formally took the title Prince Regent in 1799, so the map name is not anachronistic. (2) Official name "Kingdom of Portugal and the Algarves" (Reino de Portugal e dos Algarves) is correct. (3) Span 1640–1910 correct: Braganza restoration 1640 to the republican revolution overthrowing Manuel II in 1910. (4) Capital Lisbon; coordinates (-9.14, 38.72) fall within the accepted range for the city (sources 38.71–38.73 lat, -9.13 to -9.15 lon), well under the ±0.5° tolerance. (5) Description is exactly 5 sentences, all political history, no geography clause or pop/area/GDP number; 1139/Afonso Henriques founding, 1640 Restoration, Pombal (centralized authority, expelled Jesuits 1759, rebuilt Lisbon after the 1755 earthquake), British alliance, 1807 flight to Brazil, and 1910 fall all confirmed. (6) yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,"; the 1801 War of the Oranges (Franco-Spanish invasion, Treaty of Badajoz 6 June 1801, cession of Olivença to Spain) is correct as a "following year" event; court at Lisbon, Brazilian gold/wealth, and mid-18th-century relative economic decline confirmed. POPULATION kept: sources give ~2.8–3.1 million for 1800/1801 metropolitan Portugal, so "c. 3 million" is a sound estimate (already flagged as an estimate). AREA and GDP correctly DASHED: no clean period-specific metropolitan-area figure and no reliable single 1800 total-GDP figure available without fabrication (Maddison offers only per-capita).

name: Portuguese India
description: The Estado da Índia was established in 1505 as the administrative heart of Portugal's eastern empire, with Goa captured in 1510 becoming its principal Asian base and seat of the viceroy. At its height it governed Portuguese possessions across the Indian Ocean, but from the mid-seventeenth century Dutch and later Maratha and British pressure stripped away most of its holdings, leaving by the eighteenth century only the enclaves of Goa, Daman and Diu. Governance ran through a viceroy or governor appointed from Lisbon, typically serving fixed terms and administering the territory as a Crown colony. During the Napoleonic Wars a British garrison occupied Goa from 1799, formally as a protectorate under the Anglo-Portuguese alliance, while Portuguese civil institutions continued to function under the governor. The Estado endured as a Portuguese colony until 1961, when Indian armed forces annexed the remaining territories.
yearDesc: In 1800, Portuguese India comprised the reduced coastal enclaves of Goa, Daman and Diu, administered as the Estado da Índia under a Portuguese governor. In 1800, a British detachment under the Anglo-Portuguese alliance had garrisoned Goa since 1799, guarding it against feared French seizure while the governor Francisco António da Veiga Cabral retained control of civil institutions. In 1800, the de jure capital remained Velha Goa (Old Goa), though decades of epidemic and decay had driven the government's functioning residence to Panjim (Nova Goa), where viceroys had lived since 1759. In 1800, the once-thriving city of Old Goa was largely depopulated and semi-abandoned, its administrative offices progressively relocating to Panjim over the following decades. In 1800, the territory was a modest remnant of a formerly vast maritime empire, sustained chiefly by its role as a Catholic and commercial foothold on India's western coast.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified against Wikipedia and reputable history sources. VERIFIED: existedIn1800=yes (Estado da Índia existed continuously in 1800). officialName "Estado da Índia" confirmed as the correct Portuguese formal name. span 1505–1961 confirmed (founded 1505, annexed by India 1961). capital Velha Goa (Old Goa) was the de jure capital in 1800 — formal transfer to Panjim/Nova Goa occurred only in 1843, though the viceroy's residence moved to Panjim on 1 Dec 1759; the record captures this nuance correctly. name: Ottoman Empire
yearDesc: In 1800, the Ottoman Empire was ruled by Sultan Selim III, whose Nizam-i Cedid ("New Order") program sought to build a European-style professional army and reform the administration and treasury. Its territory stretched across southeastern Europe, Anatolia, the Levant, Iraq, the Hejaz, and much of North Africa, though central control over distant provinces and powerful local notables was weakening. The empire was entangled in the wider Napoleonic wars, with Bonaparte's army having invaded Ottoman Egypt in 1798 and Ottoman forces campaigning alongside Britain and Russia to expel the French. Provincial power was increasingly held by semi-autonomous governors and ayan such as the Mamluks in Egypt and pashas in the Balkans and Anatolia. Selim's reforms provoked fierce resistance from the Janissary corps and traditionalist factions, tensions that would culminate in his deposition in 1807.

name: Wadai Empire
description: The Sultanate of Wadai was founded around 1635 when Abd al-Karim overthrew the ruling Tunjur and established the Muslim Kolak dynasty, building his seat at Wara. Its sultans ruled a Maba-led state that made Islam the official religion while much of the population retained traditional beliefs, and it long stood in rivalry with the neighbouring sultanates of Darfur to the east and Bagirmi to the west. Under Sultan Muhammad Sabun, who seized power in 1804, Wadai expanded aggressively, opened a direct trans-Saharan trade route northward, minted its own coinage, and in 1805 crushed Bagirmi and reduced it to a tributary. The state reached its height in the mid-nineteenth century on the profits of the caravan and slave trade before its capital shifted to Abéché around 1850. It was conquered by France, which took Abéché in 1909 and formally ended the sultanate's independence in 1912.
yearDesc: In 1800, Wadai was an independent Muslim sultanate ruled from its walled capital at Wara by the Kolak dynasty. In 1800, the throne was held by Sultan Salih Derret, who reigned from about 1795 until 1803. In 1800, the state remained a middling regional power, hemmed between the stronger sultanate of Darfur and its tributary-rival Bagirmi, and had not yet undergone the expansion that would follow. In 1800, its prosperity rested on trans-Saharan caravan commerce, tribute, and slaving, with Wara's fortress-palace serving as the seat of government. In 1800, the dynastic tensions were already sharp, and within a few years Salih Derret's son Muhammad Sabun would seize power and transform Wadai into a far more assertive kingdom.

name: Rupert's Land
officialName: Rupert's Land (the Hudson's Bay Company territory, held by the Governor and Company of Adventurers of England Trading into Hudson's Bay)
description: Rupert's Land was created in 1670 when King Charles II granted a royal charter to the Hudson's Bay Company, naming his cousin Prince Rupert of the Rhine as the enterprise's first governor and vesting the company as the "true and absolute Lords and Proprietors" of the vast territory. Rather than a settled polity, it was administered as a commercial fur-trading monopoly run from scattered company forts, with a London-based governor and committee directing operations and courts convened only on an ad hoc basis by local officers. Through the eighteenth century the company faced pressure from French traders and, from the 1780s, fierce commercial rivalry with the Montreal-based North West Company, which pushed inland trade to violent extremes. That conflict, including the 1816 Battle of Seven Oaks, prompted British intervention and the 1821 merger of the two firms, after which the Crown extended the reorganized company's monopoly across the continent. In 1869–1870 the Hudson's Bay Company surrendered its charter to the British Crown for £300,000, and Rupert's Land was transferred to the new Dominion of Canada in 1870.
yearDesc: In 1800, Rupert's Land remained a Hudson's Bay Company fur-trade domain rather than a governed state, with only a handful of Europeans stationed among Indigenous nations who conducted the bulk of the trade. Authority ran from the company's London governor and committee down to officers at coastal and inland posts, with York Factory serving as the principal depot and gateway to the interior. The company was locked in intense and often bitter competition with the North West Company, whose Montreal-based traders were expanding aggressively across the western interior. To meet this rivalry the Hudson's Bay Company was building more inland posts to intercept trade before it reached its competitors. No formal civil government or legislature existed, and daily life across the territory was shaped by the fur economy and relations with First Nations and emerging Métis communities.
area: ~3,900,000 km² (1670 charter claim; the Hudson Bay drainage basin, largely unsurveyed)

(2) officialName confirmed — the HBC's formal corporate title "Governor and Company of Adventurers of England Trading into Hudson's Bay" is correct. (3) span 1670–1870 confirmed. (4) Capital York Factory confirmed as the principal depot circa 1800; coordinates 57.00°N / 92.30°W verified against gazetteer data (actual site ~57.0016°N, 92.3029°W) — within tolerance, no change. (5) Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or numeric figure; the "true and absolute Lords and Proprietors" charter language, Prince Rupert as first governor, 1816 Battle of Seven Oaks, 1821 NWC merger, and £300,000 1869–1870 surrender to the Dominion of Canada are all corroborated (Wikipedia, Canadian Encyclopedia, Deed of Surrender). (6) yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," with accurate NWC-rivalry and inland-post-expansion detail. Note: "capital" is loose since Rupert's Land had no seat of government; York Factory was the company's principal depot, with ultimate direction from the HBC committee in London.

name: Papal States
description: The Papal States originated in 756 when Pepin the Short granted conquered Lombard territory to Pope Stephen II, creating a domain over which the Bishop of Rome ruled as a temporal sovereign. For over a millennium the reigning pope governed the state as an elective absolute monarch, administering its provinces through cardinal legates and papal officials in a distinctively theocratic regime. Its temporal power was shattered by the French Revolution: Pius VI was deposed when General Berthier proclaimed a Roman Republic in 1798, and the pope died a French captive at Valence in 1799. His successor Pius VII, elected in Venice in March 1800, recovered Rome and restored papal government, but Napoleon annexed the entire state to the French Empire in 1809 and held Pius VII prisoner. The Congress of Vienna reinstated the Papal States in 1814-1815, and they endured until their absorption into a unified Italy, with Rome falling in 1870.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Papal States had just been resurrected after the collapse of the French-imposed Roman Republic and the death in exile of Pope Pius VI. In 1800, the newly elected Pius VII, chosen at a conclave held in Venice in March under Austrian protection, made his way to a Rome that had been recovered from French occupation. In 1800, the French Consulate under Napoleon formally acquiesced to the restoration, and the pope re-entered his capital in July to resume temporal rule. In 1800, Cardinal Ercole Consalvi was appointed Secretary of State and began reorganizing the shaken administration of the recovered territories. In 1800, however, the restored state remained fragile and dependent on the goodwill of France, a vulnerability that would culminate in renewed occupation and outright annexation within the decade.

Existence in 1800: CONFIRMED — Pius VI died a French captive at Valence 29 Aug 1799; the 1799-1800 conclave in Venice elected Pius VII on 14 March 1800; after the Battle of Marengo (14 June 1800) Napoleon recognized the pope and restored the Papal States, and Pius VII entered Rome on 3 July 1800. So the polity existed as a papal-ruled state by mid-1800. Official name "State of the Church (Stato Pontificio)": CONFIRMED (Wikipedia lists official name "State of the Church," Italian "Stato Pontificio"). Span 1800-1809: reasonable for this restored iteration — restoration July 1800 to Napoleon's annexation decree (17 May 1809, effective; Pius VII arrested/exiled July 1809). Description: exactly 5 sentences, pure political history, no geography/number clauses; every fact verified (756 Pepin/Stephen II; Berthier proclaimed Roman Republic 1798; Pius VI died Valence 1799; Pius VII elected Venice March 1800; French annexation 1809; Congress of Vienna 1814-15; Rome fell 1870). yearDesc: exactly 5 sentences all beginning "In 1800,"; verified — Venice conclave under Austrian protection, July 1800 re-entry, and Consalvi named (pro-)Secretary of State in 1800 (formally Cardinal Secretary of State 11 Aug 1800). Value retained because it is prefixed "c.", marked as an estimate, and lands within the real bracketing range; not dashed, but treat as approximate.

name: Qajar Persia
officialName: The Guarded Domains of Iran (Mamalek-e Mahruse-ye Iran)
description: The Qajar dynasty was founded by Agha Mohammad Khan, a chieftain of the Turkic Qajar tribe, who defeated the rival Zand and Afsharid claimants and unified Iran after the death of Karim Khan Zand, being crowned shah in 1796 and establishing Tehran as his capital. After his assassination in 1797 he was succeeded by his nephew Fath-Ali Shah, whose long reign consolidated the dynasty's central authority and revived Persianate court culture and monarchical splendor. The Qajars ruled a decentralized realm through provincial governors, often royal princes, and drew their legitimacy from Shia Islam and the ideal of the "Guarded Domains" of Iran. During the nineteenth century the dynasty suffered heavy territorial losses to the Russian Empire in the Caucasus and grew increasingly enmeshed in Anglo-Russian rivalry, ceding economic concessions and sovereignty. Weakened by the Constitutional Revolution of 1905–1911 and foreign intervention, the dynasty was finally deposed in 1925 when Reza Khan founded the Pahlavi dynasty.
yearDesc: In 1800, Iran was ruled by Fath-Ali Shah Qajar, who had ascended the throne three years earlier and governed from the new capital at Tehran. The dynasty was still consolidating its hold, and the shah relied on royal princes and tribal governors to administer the provinces. Iran retained nominal authority over the khanates of the Caucasus, though Russian expansion toward Georgia was beginning to threaten this control, culminating in Russia's annexation of eastern Georgia by 1801. European powers were courting the Qajar court as the Napoleonic Wars unfolded, with Britain seeking to secure the approaches to India. The realm remained a traditional agrarian and pastoral society whose central government wielded limited direct power over distant regions.
issues: All claims independently verified against Wikipedia (Qajar Iran, Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar, Fath-Ali Shah Qajar, Guarded Domains of Iran, Georgia within the Russian Empire), Encyclopaedia Iranica, and Britannica; verdict confirmed with no changes. Span 1789–1925 corroborated (dynasty ruled Iran 1789–1925, deposed by Reza Khan 1925). name: Republic of Lucca
officialName: Most Serene Republic of Lucca (Serenissima Repubblica Lucchese)
description: The Republic of Lucca emerged as a self-governing commune after the Tuscan city bought its feudal liberties around 1160 and thereafter defended its independence beneath the motto "Libertas." Under Castruccio Castracani in the early fourteenth century it briefly became an expansionist military power, but for most of its history it survived as a compact city-state hemmed in by Florence and later by the Medici and Habsburg-Lorraine grand dukes of Tuscany. Beginning as a broad communal government, its constitution narrowed over the centuries until a reform of 1628 entrenched a closed patrician oligarchy, in which a Council and rotating magistracies led by the Gonfaloniere governed on behalf of a small hereditary nobility. Its longevity owed much to cautious neutrality, careful diplomacy, and a diversified silk-based mercantile economy that let it outlast nearly every other Italian republic. The state was finally extinguished in 1805 when Napoleon converted it into the Principality of Lucca and Piombino under his sister Elisa Bonaparte Baciocchi.
yearDesc: In 1800, Lucca was a small aristocratic republic caught in the crossfire of the War of the Second Coalition, its centuries-old oligarchic constitution suspended after the French had overturned it in early 1799. In 1800, following an Austrian interlude, French forces reoccupied the city on 9 July and installed a provisional government associated with the commissioner Antoine Christophe Saliceti. In 1800, the traditional patrician bodies that had ruled through the Gonfaloniere and the Councils no longer functioned freely, the state being reshaped under French protectorate influence. In 1800, Lucca's fate hinged on the wider struggle between France and Austria for control of Tuscany and northern Italy, decided that year in France's favour. In 1800, the republic still nominally survived and would see a restored oligarchic constitution promulgated in 1801, only to be abolished by Napoleon in 1805.
issues: No changes — all claims independently corroborated against Wikipedia (Republic of Lucca, Principality of Lucca and Piombino) and corroborating sources. existedIn1800 "partial" is correct: the republic existed as a polity in 1800 but its old oligarchic constitution was in abeyance (French overthrew the oligarchy Jan 1799; Austrian occupation from July 1799; French reoccupied Lucca 9 July 1800 under a provisional government tied to commissioner Saliceti; oligarchy formally restored by the 1801 constitution). Span 1160–1805 confirmed (bought feudal rights from Margrave Welf VI in 1160; extinguished 1805 as the Principality of Lucca and Piombino under Elisa Bonaparte Baciocchi). Capital Lucca at ~10.50E/43.84N is correct (actual ~10.503E/43.843N, within tolerance). Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause and no pop/area/GDP figures (Castruccio Castracani, 1628 oligarchy reform, Gonfaloniere, Libertas motto, silk economy, 1805 Napoleonic end all verified). yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800," with the 1799–1800 occupation sequence (French Jan 1799 → Austrian July 1799 → French 9 July 1800 → 1801 restored constitution → 1805 abolition) accurately reflected. Pop/area/GDP correctly dashed — no reliable state-level figures for the ~1800 republic; not fabricated.

name: Kingdom of Spain
description: The Kingdom of Spain in this iteration was ruled by the House of Bourbon, which acceded in 1700 with Philip V and consolidated a unified monarchy through the eighteenth-century Nueva Planta reforms that abolished the separate Aragonese constitutions. Under the Enlightened absolutism of Charles III the crown pursued administrative centralization, colonial reform, and cautious modernization while retaining a vast overseas empire. His successor Charles IV, reigning from 1788, proved a weak monarch who left governance to favourites and ministers amid the shock of the French Revolution. Spanish policy lurched from war against revolutionary France to a subordinate alliance with it, culminating in dependence on Napoleon and the loss of naval power. This iteration was fatally destabilized by the 1808 Bayonne abdications and the Peninsular War, though the Bourbon monarchy was restored in 1814 and, after interruptions, continues to head the Spanish state today.
yearDesc: In 1800, Spain was ruled by the Bourbon king Charles IV, with government dominated by court favourites and the growing shadow of Napoleonic France. The able but controversial minister Mariano Luis de Urquijo served as First Secretary of State through most of the year until his dismissal that December, while the disgraced favourite Manuel de Godoy retained influence and would return to power in 1801. On 1 October the crown signed the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso with France, secretly agreeing to retrocede the Louisiana territory in exchange for promised compensation in Italy for the Duke of Parma. Spain remained bound to France as a junior partner in the ongoing war against Britain, its treasury strained and its fleet weakened after earlier naval defeats. Its immense American and Pacific empire still stood intact, but the metropolitan monarchy was drifting toward the crisis that would erupt in 1808.
issues: GDP DASHED: the record's "~$11 billion (1990 int$)" is not independently corroborable — it is a compound estimate (Maddison per-capita × census population), and the underlying Spain-1800 per-capita itself varies materially by Maddison vintage (~$900–$1,205/1990 int$) while no source directly reports a Spain 1800 total GDP; per the adversarial mandate an unconfirmable derived figure is dashed rather than left standing. All other fields CONFIRMED: existedIn1800=yes (Bourbon Kingdom under Charles IV); officialName correct; span 1700–Present correct (Bourbon dynasty acceded 1700 with Philip V, reigns today); capital Madrid with lon/lat on the city; description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or numbers (Charles III r.1759–88, Charles IV from 1788 — accurate); yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," and every claim checks out — Urquijo dismissed 13 Dec 1800, Godoy resigned 1798 and returned to power 1801, Third Treaty of San Ildefonso signed 1 Oct 1800 (Louisiana retrocession vs. Italian compensation for the Duke of Parma), Spain allied to France at war with Britain. The existedNote anchor-correction is accurate (Godoy was NOT chief minister in 1800; Urquijo held the office until 13 Dec 1800). pop verified against the 1797 Godoy census (10,541,221). area ~505,000 km² kept as a defensible approximation of metropolitan Spain (peninsula + Balearics + Canaries) c.1800, though note it is the total metropolitan figure, not strictly "peninsular Spain proper" (peninsular-only ≈ 492,500 km²) as the confidence text characterized it.

name: Russian Empire
yearDesc: In 1800, the Russian Empire was ruled by Tsar Paul I, who had succeeded his mother Catherine the Great in 1796 and pursued erratic domestic and foreign policies that alienated the nobility. Russia had joined the Second Coalition against Revolutionary France in 1798, sending armies under Alexander Suvorov into Italy and Switzerland, but by 1800 Paul had broken with Britain and Austria and was moving toward rapprochement with Napoleon. Governed as an autocracy from Saint Petersburg, the empire rested on a vast serf population bound to noble estates and the state, with roughly nine-tenths of its people living in the countryside. Its borders reached from the Baltic and the recently partitioned Polish lands in the west across Siberia to the Pacific and the Russian colonies in Alaska. Paul's growing tyranny and his reversal of Catherine's policies led to a palace conspiracy that would kill him in March 1801, bringing his son Alexander I to the throne.

name: Luba Empire
description: Luba oral tradition traces the empire's sacred kingship to the mythic hunter Kalala Ilunga, who is said to have overthrown the despot Nkongolo Mwamba and founded a centralized state whose ruler bore the title mulopwe. The polity crystallized around Lake Boya in the Upemba Depression and governed through a court of nobles, tributary chiefs, and the Bambudye secret society, which preserved royal history and legitimized succession. Rather than ruling by direct conquest, the kings extended influence by demanding tribute and exporting sacred royal fire to vassal "fire kingdoms" on the frontier, while monopolizing trade in salt, copper, and iron. The empire greatly expanded during the eighteenth century, notably under King Kadilo and his successors, becoming a dominant power of the southern Congo savanna. It fragmented after an 1889 succession dispute amid disruptive slave- and ivory-raiding by East African traders, and was soon absorbed into the Congo Free State.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Luba Empire was ruled by the mulopwe Ilunga Sungu, whose reign of roughly 1780 to 1809 marked a peak of Luba power and expansion. In 1800, the state governed the savanna heartland around Lake Boya from its capital near Mwibele, projecting authority over a network of tributary chiefs and vassal frontier kingdoms. In 1800, its wealth rested on control of long-distance trade routes and monopolies over salt, copper, and iron passing through the Congo basin. In 1800, the sacred kingship remained bound to the Bambudye society and the ritual apparatus that legitimized the ruler as guardian of the land. In 1800, the empire had not yet felt the destabilizing East African slave and ivory raids that would later shatter it, standing instead as the preeminent power of the region.

Multiple independent sources place the capital Mwibele / Lake Boya / Kabongo at ~25.6-25.7 E, ~7.3-7.4 S: Mwibele locality 25.68 E / -7.36 S; Lake Boya (Wikipedia) 25.65 E / -7.37 S (7 deg 22' S, 25 deg 39' E); Kabongo town -7.32 / 25.58. King Kadilo (r. c.1700) 18th-c. expansion, Bambudye society, mulopwe title, fire kingdoms, and salt/copper/iron trade monopoly all confirmed. name: Turks and Caicos Islands
officialName: Turks and Caicos Islands (dependency of the Colony of the Bahamas)
description: The islands were first permanently settled from 1681 by Bermudian salt rakers who worked Grand Turk seasonally and long resisted outside control, resenting Bahamian attempts to tax and license their salt trade through Nassau. After the American Revolution ended in 1783, British Loyalist refugees settled the Caicos group and established cotton plantations worked by enslaved Africans, though this plantation economy collapsed by around 1820. In 1799 Britain formally annexed both the Turks and the Caicos groups as part of the Colony of the Bahamas, ending the ambiguous Bermudian–Bahamian jurisdictional disputes in the Crown's favour. Governed thereafter from Nassau, the islands remained a peripheral salt-producing dependency with little local autonomy for the first half of the nineteenth century. Resentment at Bahamian rule and taxation eventually drove the inhabitants to petition successfully for separation, and in 1848 the islands were made a distinct colony under a council president.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Turks and Caicos Islands were a small British-controlled Caribbean group administered as a dependency of the Bahamas following their formal annexation the previous year in 1799. Their economy rested chiefly on salt raking, worked largely by Bermudians on Grand Turk and the surrounding salt cays, supplemented by the recently established but fragile Loyalist cotton plantations on the Caicos. Political authority was exercised from Nassau rather than locally, and the islands possessed no separate legislature or governor of their own. Cockburn Town on Grand Turk served as the principal settlement and seat of local affairs, a role it had held since 1766. Lingering friction persisted between the independent-minded salt rakers and the Bahamian government over licensing and taxation of the lucrative salt exports.
issues: No corrections needed — every claim independently verified against Wikipedia and encyclopedic sources. name: Kingdom of Sweden
description: The modern Kingdom of Sweden emerged when Gustav Vasa broke the realm away from the Kalmar Union and was elected king in 1523, founding a hereditary Vasa monarchy. Sweden rose to become a European great power during the seventeenth-century "Age of Greatness," dominating the Baltic under warrior-kings such as Gustavus Adolphus and Charles XII before that empire collapsed in the Great Northern War. The eighteenth-century "Age of Liberty" shifted power to the Riksdag of the Estates, until Gustav III restored strong royal authority through his coup of 1772 and a further seizure of power in 1789. His son Gustav IV Adolf, who reigned from 1792, was overthrown in an 1809 coup after the disastrous loss of Finland to Russia, prompting a new constitution and the election of the French marshal Bernadotte as heir. The kingdom has continued to the present day as a constitutional monarchy, having remained at peace since 1814.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Sweden was ruled by the young King Gustav IV Adolf and still governed Finland as an integral eastern part of the realm. In this year the king summoned the Estates to Norrköping and confronted severe financial disorder inherited from his predecessor's Russian war, worsened by the crop failures of 1798 and 1799. Seeking to keep Sweden out of the Napoleonic conflict, Gustav IV Adolf joined the short-lived Second League of Armed Neutrality, whose founding convention of 16 December 1800 aligned Sweden with Russia and Denmark-Norway against British interference with neutral shipping. The kingdom was governed as an absolute monarchy under the framework Gustav III had established, with Stockholm as its capital and administrative heart. This precarious neutrality soon unravelled, foreshadowing the war that would cost Sweden all of Finland in 1809 and topple the king.
pop: c. 3.2 million (Sweden c. 2.4 million plus Finland c. 0.83 million)

name: Konbaung dynasty
description: The Konbaung dynasty was founded in 1752 by Alaungpaya, a village headman from Shwebo who rallied Upper Burma to overthrow the Restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom and reunify the country. His successors, notably Hsinbyushin, waged aggressive expansionist wars, sacking the Siamese capital Ayutthaya in 1767 and repelling four Chinese Qing invasions between 1765 and 1769. King Bodawpaya, who seized the throne in 1782, continued this expansion, annexing the Kingdom of Arakan in 1784–1785 and pushing Burmese frontiers toward Bengal and Assam. This westward growth eventually brought the dynasty into conflict with British India, producing three Anglo-Burmese Wars that steadily stripped away its territory across the nineteenth century. The dynasty ended in 1885 when British forces occupied Mandalay, deposed King Thibaw, and abolished the Burmese monarchy, incorporating the kingdom into British India.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Konbaung dynasty was ruled by King Bodawpaya from his capital at Amarapura, which he had established as his royal seat in the early 1780s. The kingdom stood near its territorial height, having recently absorbed Arakan in 1784–1785 and consolidated control over Upper and Lower Burma. Bodawpaya presided over an ambitious, centralizing court, conducting kingdom-wide revenue and population surveys and undertaking massive religious projects such as the unfinished Mingun Pagoda. The absorption of Arakan had pushed the Burmese frontier up to the border of British-held Bengal, sowing the tensions and refugee flows that would later ignite the First Anglo-Burmese War. At this date the dynasty remained an independent and assertive regional power, its confrontation with British India still more than two decades in the future.

name: Portuguese East Africa
officialName: Captaincy-General of Mozambique, Sofala and Rivers of Sena
yearDesc: In 1800, Portuguese Mozambique was a loose collection of coastal trading settlements and Zambezi-valley prazo estates rather than a unified colony, governed as a captaincy-general that had been detached from Portuguese India in 1752. Its administrative seat was Mozambique Island, where the Governor-General resided and the coastal fortress commanded the ivory, gold, and slave trades. Real Portuguese authority reached little beyond the ports of Sofala, Quelimane, Inhambane, and the inland outposts of Sena and Tete, with the interior dominated by the prazeiros and their Chikunda slave armies. The colony's Indian Ocean commerce had declined under competition from Omani and Mazrui Arabs to the north, even as the Atlantic-bound slave trade to Brazil expanded. Effective control over the vast interior remained minimal, and the settlements functioned largely as autonomous commercial enclaves tied to Goa, Lisbon, and the wider Indian Ocean world.

name: Kingdom of Bagirmi
description: The Sultanate of Bagirmi coalesced from a group of chiefdoms around 1522 under a ruling dynasty whose kings bore the title Mbang and reigned from the capital at Massenya. Islam became the state religion under Mbang Abdullah in the late sixteenth century, though many subjects continued to follow traditional beliefs, and the realm long alternated between independence and tributary subordination to its powerful neighbor Bornu to the west. Under Mbang Muhammad al-Amin in the second half of the eighteenth century Bagirmi threw off Bornu's overlordship and reasserted its independence. In 1805/1806 the rising sultanate of Wadai under Sabun sacked Massenya, killed the reigning Mbang, and reduced Bagirmi to a tributary that its eastern neighbor repeatedly plundered thereafter. Weakened further by the conquests of the warlord Rabih az-Zubayr in 1893, the sultanate placed itself under French protection by treaty in 1897 and was absorbed into French colonial rule, surviving today only as a ceremonial monarchy within Chad.
yearDesc: In 1800, Bagirmi stood as an independent Islamic sultanate ruled from Massenya by its Mbang, having recently shaken off tributary ties to Bornu. Its court presided over a Muslim ruling elite governing a population that still largely practiced indigenous religions, and its wealth rested heavily on slave-raiding against the non-Muslim peoples to its south. The state was hemmed in by two stronger powers, the older empire of Bornu to the west and the ascendant sultanate of Wadai to the east, and it maneuvered warily between them. This independence proved short-lived, for within roughly five years the Wadai ruler Sabun would sack Massenya, kill the Mbang, and force Bagirmi into vassalage. Thus 1800 marks a final interval of full sovereignty before the sultanate's long decline into tributary status.

name: Tokugawa shogunate
officialName: Tokugawa bakufu (Edo shogunate), the government of the Sei-i Taishōgun
description: The Tokugawa shogunate was founded in 1603 when Tokugawa Ieyasu, victorious at the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600, was appointed Sei-i Taishōgun and established a hereditary military government at Edo while the emperor remained a ceremonial figure at Kyoto. Successive Tokugawa shoguns bound the roughly 260 daimyo domains to the center through the alternate-attendance system, hostage families at Edo, and strict control of the imperial court, the temples, and foreign contact. From the 1630s the regime enforced the sakoku policy, expelling most Europeans, banning Christianity, and restricting overseas trade to closely supervised contacts with the Dutch, Chinese, Koreans, and Ryukyuans. The long peace it imposed fostered urban growth, commerce, and a vibrant merchant culture, even as recurring famines, fiscal strain, and rigid status hierarchies bred periodic reform drives and rural unrest. Weakened by financial crisis and the arrival of Western powers, the shogunate collapsed in 1868 when the last shogun resigned and imperial rule was restored under the Meiji Restoration.
yearDesc: In 1800, the shogunate was led by Tokugawa Ienari, the eleventh shogun, who had taken office in 1787 and would rule until 1837 in the longest tenure of any Tokugawa shogun. In 1800, the conservative Kansei Reforms initiated by the chief councillor Matsudaira Sadanobu continued to shape policy even though Sadanobu himself had left office in 1793, promoting fiscal austerity, agricultural recovery, orthodox Neo-Confucianism, and censorship. In 1800, Japan remained under the sakoku isolation regime, with foreign trade confined largely to the Dutch and Chinese at Nagasaki, though Russian and other Western vessels were beginning to probe the northern frontiers. In 1800, the country was outwardly peaceful and Edo was among the world's largest cities, yet the samurai class and shogunal finances were increasingly strained while merchants prospered. In 1800, ultimate ceremonial authority still rested with the emperor at Kyoto, but real governing power lay with the bakufu at Edo Castle.

name: Rozvi Empire
description: The Rozvi Empire was founded around 1683 by Changamire Dombo, a Shona leader who broke from the Mutapa state and defeated the Torwa dynasty to seize the Butua kingdom in the southwest. Ruled by the Changamire dynasty through a collateral brother-to-brother succession that frequently bred conflict, the state expelled Portuguese traders in the 1690s and asserted dominance over much of the plateau, its name derived from a Shona word evoking its warriors' reputation for plunder. It operated as a centralized tributary monarchy in which the mambo, advised by a council of chief councillors, collected goods and labor and redistributed protection, rain and fertility ceremonies, and drought relief. Governance radiated in concentric zones from a core ruled directly by the mambo outward to royal appointees and more distant tributary territories. The empire was shattered by Nguni and Ndebele groups during the 1830s Mfecane, and its last ruler, Tohwechipi, finally surrendered to Mzilikazi in 1866, ending the Changamire line.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Rozvi Empire was in visible decline, gripped by a catastrophic drought that struck around 1795–1800 and caused mass starvation across the plateau. The reigning mambo was Changamire Rupandamananga, nicknamed Rupengo, whose troubled reign coincided with the crisis. The famine triggered a series of civil wars that destabilized the state and persisted for at least the following decade. The empire also struggled to absorb or repel migrations of Tswana, Birwa, Hlengwe, and Karanga peoples moving through its territory. Despite these strains the Rozvi state remained intact and would endure for several more decades before the Mfecane invasions of the 1830s.

Collateral brother-to-brother succession, 1690s Portuguese expulsion (feiras attacked 1693), name from Shona "to plunder", mambo + council of chief councillors, concentric zones, and 1830s Mfecane collapse with Tohwechipi surrendering to Mzilikazi in 1866 all confirmed. name: Kingdom of Ireland
description: The Kingdom of Ireland was established by the Crown of Ireland Act of 1542, when Henry VIII of England was proclaimed King of Ireland, replacing the earlier lordship and binding the island to the English crown in personal union. Though formally a kingdom, it functioned as a dependency, its parliament long bound by Poynings' Law and its politics dominated by the Protestant Anglo-Irish minority through the established Church of Ireland while penal laws suppressed the Catholic majority. Its history was shaped by the Tudor conquest, the Confederate Wars of the 1640s, the Cromwellian conquest, and the Williamite War of 1689 to 1691, which entrenched the Protestant Ascendancy. In 1782 Grattan's Parliament won legislative independence, but the failed United Irishmen rebellion of 1798 alarmed the government and accelerated moves toward union. The kingdom was abolished when the Acts of Union came into force on 1 January 1801, merging Ireland with Great Britain to form the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Ireland was in personal union with Great Britain under King George III, with executive authority exercised through the Lord Lieutenant in Dublin. In 1800, the Irish Parliament in Dublin passed the Act of Union, its final major act, secured amid extensive bribery, patronage, and the granting of peerages to overcome opposition. In 1800, the country was still recovering from the bloody suppression of the 1798 rebellion of the United Irishmen and the abortive French landing at Killala. In 1800, political life remained dominated by the Protestant Ascendancy, while the promise of Catholic emancipation, dangled to win support for union, went unfulfilled because of the King's objections. In 1800, the kingdom stood on the eve of abolition, its separate parliament and statehood set to end at midnight on 31 December 1800.

name: Viceroyalty of New Granada
officialName: Viceroyalty of the New Kingdom of Granada
description: The Viceroyalty of New Granada was created by Bourbon royal decree in 1717 to detach the northern Andean provinces from the Viceroyalty of Peru, suppressed in 1723 for financial reasons, and definitively reestablished in 1739. Governed from Santa Fe de Bogotá by a viceroy appointed by the Spanish crown, it consolidated the audiencias of Santa Fe, Quito, and Panama and later oversaw the Captaincy General of Venezuela. Its administration embodied the Bourbon Reforms, tightening royal control, expanding tobacco and aguardiente monopolies, and strengthening defenses against British incursions in the Caribbean. The heavy fiscal exactions provoked resistance, most notably the Comunero Revolt of 1781, which was suppressed but foreshadowed deeper creole discontent. The viceroyalty collapsed after 1810 when local juntas rejected Napoleonic-occupied Spain's authority, dissolving into the independence struggles that produced Gran Colombia.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Viceroyalty of New Granada was ruled by Viceroy Pedro Mendinueta y Múzquiz, who held office from 1797 to 1803. The colony was administered from Santa Fe de Bogotá as a mature and strategically important division of Spain's American empire. Royal revenues, buoyed by the tobacco and aguardiente monopolies, had leveled off at around three million pesos after 1800. Creole intellectual life was stirring, exemplified by the Royal Botanical Expedition and the scientific circle around José Celestino Mutis. Beneath the outward stability, resentment of Bourbon fiscal policy and peninsular privilege was mounting toward the crisis that would erupt after 1808.

(5) Viceroy Pedro Mendinueta y Múzquiz confirmed in office 1797–1803 (took office Jan 1797, returned to Spain Sept 1803).
