name: Malabar
officialName: Malabar District (British Malabar), East India Company
description: Malabar came under British East India Company control in 1792, when Tipu Sultan of Mysore ceded the region to the Company and its allies under the Treaty of Seringapatam that ended the Third Anglo-Mysore War. The territory was first attached to the Bombay Presidency and administered by Company commissioners, then transferred to the Madras Presidency in 1800, where it was organised as a district under a Principal Collector headquartered at Calicut. Company rule provoked sustained armed resistance, above all the Cotiote War waged from 1793 to 1806 by Kerala Varma Pazhassi Raja of the Kottayam house, one of the longest and bloodiest campaigns the Company fought in India. The old ruling houses, including the Zamorin of Calicut, were progressively stripped of sovereignty and reduced to Company pensioners, formalised in the settlement of 1806. The district endured as a unit of British India until Indian independence and was finally dissolved in 1956, its territory merged into the new state of Kerala and neighbouring areas.
yearDesc: In 1800, Malabar was formally transferred from the Bombay Presidency to the Madras Presidency and administered from Calicut as a district of the Company's south Indian holdings. In 1800, the region remained deeply unsettled, as the Cotiote War against Kerala Varma Pazhassi Raja flared anew over control of the Wayanad highlands. In 1800, Company forces, later placed under officers including Arthur Wellesley, struggled to suppress guerrilla resistance across the Thalassery–Wayanad interior. In 1800, the once-powerful Zamorin of Calicut and other local rajas retained only nominal standing, their revenues and authority being absorbed into direct Company administration. In 1800, Malabar was thus a newly consolidated but turbulent frontier district of British India, its coastal ports and pepper trade prized by the Company even as rebellion persisted inland.
issues: Corrected two overprecise/unverifiable date claims. (2) yearDesc originally pinned the "Principal Collector" reorganisation to 1800; the Principal Collector office was in fact created in 1801 (Maj. William Macleod, eff. 1 Oct 1801), so the yearDesc first sentence was reworded to say Malabar was administered from Calicut as a Madras district without dating the Principal Collector to 1800 (the description prose keeps the Principal Collector detail as general organisational context, not pinned to 1800). name: Viceroyalty of Brazil
description: Portugal's American possession was governed as the State of Brazil, and from 1763 its governors permanently held the title of viceroy, giving the colony its common designation as the Viceroyalty of Brazil. In that year the capital was transferred from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro, reflecting the economic pull of the gold and diamond districts of the interior, and in 1775 the northern states of Maranhão and Grão-Pará were absorbed to unify Portuguese America under a single viceroy. The period was marked by tightening royal control and rising colonial grievance, producing conspiracies against Portuguese rule such as the Inconfidência Mineira of 1789 and the Bahia Conspiracy of 1798. The viceregal office was suppressed in 1808 when the Portuguese royal court, fleeing Napoleon's invasion, relocated to Rio de Janeiro and assumed direct government. In 1815 the colony was elevated to the Kingdom of Brazil within the United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves, ending its colonial subordination and setting the stage for independence in 1822.
yearDesc: In 1800, Brazil was ruled from Rio de Janeiro by the viceroy Dom José Luís de Castro, 2nd Count of Resende, who had held the post since 1790. The colony remained a possession of the Portuguese crown under Queen Maria I, with Prince João acting as regent following her mental incapacity. The recently suppressed Bahia Conspiracy of 1798 still echoed as a warning of Enlightenment-inspired unrest, and colonial authorities kept a wary watch on dissent while enforcing the crown's trade monopoly. The economy leaned on plantation exports of sugar and cotton and on enslaved African labour, even as the great gold boom of Minas Gerais had passed its peak. Governance was fragmented across numerous captaincies reporting to the viceroy, whose direct authority was strongest in the south-central core around the capital.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified. Confirmed via Wikipedia and reputable sources: (1) existedIn1800 = yes — the State of Brazil under permanent viceregal governance (since 1763) was fully in place in 1800. (2) officialName = "State of Brazil" / Estado do Brasil is correct; "Viceroyalty" is historiographic (Wikipedia: Viceroyalty of Brazil). (3) Capital = Rio de Janeiro, moved from Salvador in 1763 (permanent viceroy title same year); coords -43.17/-22.91 sit on Rio (within tolerance). (4) Viceroy in 1800 = José Luís de Castro, 2nd Count of Resende, term 1790–1801 (Encyclopedia.com / Count of Resende) — matches "held the post since 1790." (5) Events all confirmed: Inconfidência Mineira 1789, Bahia Conspiracy/Revolt of the Tailors 1798, Maranhão & Grão-Pará integrated 1775, viceregal office suppressed 1808 (court transfer), elevated to Kingdom of Brazil 1815 within the United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves. (6) Prince João formally Prince Regent from 14 July 1799 under the incapacitated Queen Maria I — regency in 1800 confirmed. (7) span 1763–1815 is a defensible framing of the colonial Viceroyalty era (note the viceregal office itself ended 1808, but the description handles that correctly). Population "c. 2,400,000" retained as an approximate commonly-cited c.1800 estimate — sources cluster ~2.33M (1800) to ~3M (1808); confidence note appropriately flags it as approximate. Area and GDP correctly dashed — no reliable period figure exists; Maddison does not isolate a 1800 colonial-Brazil GDP. Description = exactly 5 sentences, political history, no geography/number clause; yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". Both structurally valid.

name: Thuringian states
officialName: Ernestine Saxon duchies of Thuringia (no single sovereign name; a cluster of separate duchies)
description: The Ernestine Saxon duchies originated in the 1485 Leipzig division of the Wettin lands between the brothers Ernest and Albert, and after the Ernestine head John Frederick I lost the Saxon electorate to the Albertine line at the Capitulation of Wittenberg in 1547, the family retained its Thuringian territories as its remaining base. Because Saxon custom divided inheritances among all sons, these lands fragmented repeatedly into a shifting patchwork of small duchies such as Saxe-Weimar, Saxe-Gotha-Altenburg, Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld, Saxe-Meiningen, and Saxe-Hildburghausen, several of which held votes in the Imperial Diet within the Upper Saxon Circle. Each duchy was an independent principality of the Holy Roman Empire, ruled by its own branch of the House of Wettin, and their courts were noted more for cultural patronage than for military or political weight. The most celebrated among them, Saxe-Weimar under Duchess Anna Amalia and Duke Charles Augustus, made Weimar a leading centre of German letters, drawing Goethe, Schiller, Herder, and Wieland in the era of Weimar Classicism. The duchies joined Napoleon's Confederation of the Rhine, entered the German Confederation in 1815, consolidated into four states by 1826, and were finally abolished when their rulers abdicated in 1918, their lands merging into the new state of Thuringia in 1920.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Thuringian lands were divided among seven small Ernestine duchies, still separate sovereign estates of the Holy Roman Empire six years before its dissolution. In 1800, Saxe-Weimar was governed by Duke Charles Augustus, who held it in personal union with Saxe-Eisenach and presided over the brilliant literary court that made Weimar the cultural capital of the German-speaking world. In 1800, the other duchies — Saxe-Gotha, Saxe-Altenburg, Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld, Saxe-Meiningen, and Saxe-Hildburghausen — were each ruled by their own Wettin dukes, their borders an intricate mosaic produced by generations of partible inheritance. In 1800, these petty states were militarily insignificant and would soon be swept into Napoleon's orbit, acceding to the Confederation of the Rhine after 1806. In 1800, their combined territory and modest populations belied an outsized influence on European intellectual life radiating from the Weimar court.
issues: CORRECTED a naming anachronism: the record listed a duchy "Saxe-Coburg" for 1800, but no standalone Duchy of Saxe-Coburg existed then — since 1735 it was Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld (a standalone Saxe-Coburg existed only 1596–1633 and 1681–1699, and again after 1826 as Saxe-Coburg and Gotha). Replaced "Saxe-Coburg" → "Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld" in existedNote's duchy list (via description), in the description's patchwork list, and in yearDesc sentence 3. Kept Saxe-Gotha and Saxe-Altenburg as separate titular duchies in the seven-duchy enumeration — historically supportable, since in 1800 there were TWO personal unions (Weimar+Eisenach, and Gotha+Altenburg held together as Saxe-Gotha-Altenburg), matching Wikipedia's "seven such duchies (two held in personal unions)"; the description uses the compound form Saxe-Gotha-Altenburg for precision. Everything else CONFIRMED against Wikipedia and reputable sources: existedIn1800 yes; pop 445,000 and area 7,693 km² are Wikipedia's exact 1800 aggregate for the seven Ernestine duchies (excludes the non-Ernestine Schwarzburg/Reuss, as the note states); Weimar as chief seat, coords 50.98/11.33 match Weimar (50.9803/11.329); Charles Augustus correctly titled "Duke" in 1800 (grand duke only from 1815); 1485 Leipzig division and 1547 Capitulation of Wittenberg origins; Anna Amalia/Charles Augustus patronage of Goethe, Schiller, Herder, Wieland (Weimar Classicism); German Confederation 1815; consolidation to four states by 1826; 1918 abdications and 1920 merger into Thuringia (Coburg the exception, joining Bavaria); Schwarzburg (Sondershausen 1697, Rudolstadt 1710) and Reuss confirmed as separate non-Ernestine Upper Saxon Circle estates. Description remains exactly 5 sentences with no geography clause and no pop/area/GDP figure; yearDesc remains exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,". Span 1485–1806 kept as the approximate Ernestine-cluster/Old-Reich era (no single founding/dissolution date for a cluster). GDP dashed — no reliable ~1800 estimate at this sub-state granularity.

name: Viceroyalty of Peru
officialName: Kingdom of Peru (Reino del Perú); commonly the Viceroyalty of Peru (Virreinato del Perú)
description: The Viceroyalty of Peru was created by the Spanish Crown in 1542 through the New Laws to govern its South American possessions, with its seat established at Lima. Following the turbulent civil wars among the conquistadors, Viceroy Francisco de Toledo consolidated colonial rule in the 1570s, systematizing the mita labor draft and extinguishing the last Neo-Inca resistance at Vilcabamba in 1572. For nearly two centuries it was the dominant Spanish authority in South America and one of the empire's richest possessions, sustained by silver mining and a mercantile monopoly centered on Lima. The eighteenth-century Bourbon Reforms progressively dismembered it, detaching the Viceroyalty of New Granada in 1717 and 1739 and the Viceroyalty of Río de la Plata in 1776, and it was shaken by the great Andean rebellion of Túpac Amaru II in 1780–1783. Reduced but still the crown's principal South American stronghold, the viceroyalty became the bastion of royalism in the wars of independence and was formally dissolved in 1824 after the patriot victory at Ayacucho.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Viceroyalty of Peru was governed from Lima by Viceroy Ambrosio O'Higgins, 1st Marquess of Osorno, an Irish-born Spanish administrator who had risen through service in Chile before taking the viceregal office in 1796. His administration, cut short by his death in March 1801, focused on coastal defense against Britain—strengthening the fortifications of Callao amid the war declared in 1797—and on public works such as the new road linking Lima to Callao. The viceroyalty's territory had been sharply curtailed by the Bourbon Reforms, with the Río de la Plata lands gone since 1776 and Chile administered as a largely autonomous captaincy general, leaving Peru centered on its Andean and coastal heartland. Its society remained rigidly stratified between a Spanish and creole elite and a large Indigenous majority still burdened by tribute and labor obligations, with silver from mines like Cerro de Pasco anchoring the economy. Though outwardly stable and firmly loyal to the crown, the viceroyalty carried the unresolved tensions of the Túpac Amaru revolt of the previous generation, tensions that would resurface in the independence struggles of the following decades.
issues: Population DASHED (was "~1,500,000 (1790s census under Viceroy Gil de Taboada)"): the record's own cited basis contradicts it. The Gil de Taboada census reported ~1,076,000–1,115,000 (1791/92: 1,076,122/1,076,997; 1795: 1,115,207) and a later 1797 count of ~1,239,197 — none near 1.5 million. The "~1,500,000" appears only as a loose overstatement in one Wikipedia summary sentence, falsified by the detailed census figures; since the attributed value is wrong and the correct viceroyalty-wide total is ambiguous (depends on which territories are counted), it is dashed rather than left standing or swapped for a contested figure. Verdict changed to "corrected" for this reason only. ALL OTHER FIELDS CONFIRMED: existence in 1800 (est. 20 Nov 1542, dissolved 30 Dec 1824 after Ayacucho 9 Dec 1824); official name Kingdom of Peru / Reino del Perú (Wikipedia: "officially known as"); span 1542–1824; capital Lima with correct coords (Lima ≈ -12.05, -77.04, within tolerance); 1800 viceroy Ambrosio O'Higgins, 1st Marquess of Osorno (took office 1796, died March 1801), Callao fortifications + Lima–Callao road all verified; Bourbon detachments (New Granada 1717/1739, Río de la Plata 1776) verified; Chile separated as autonomous captaincy general (1798) verified; Túpac Amaru II rebellion 1780–1783 verified; Vilcabamba/Neo-Inca end 1572 under Toledo verified. Description = exactly 5 sentences, no geography clause, no numeric figures. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," (Cerro de Pasco silver is qualitative, not a numeric grid figure — acceptable). Minor note (not altered): Spain declared war on Britain in Oct 1796; the record's "war declared in 1797" reflects the source phrasing for O'Higgins's response and is within acceptable framing. Area and GDP correctly dashed (no reliable ~1800 viceroyalty-specific figures).

name: Saint-Barthélemy
description: France ceded Saint-Barthélemy to Sweden on 1 July 1784 in exchange for trading privileges at Gothenburg, making it the Swedish crown's only Caribbean possession. Swedish officials took formal control in March 1785, renamed the harbour town Gustavia after King Gustav III, and declared it a free port open to all nations, which turned a poor, water-scarce island into a busy neutral entrepôt for wartime and contraband trade. Administered by a succession of royally appointed governors, the colony leaned on slavery and on transit commerce rather than plantation agriculture, prospering especially during the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic wars when belligerents used its neutral flag. British forces occupied the island from 1801 to 1802 during the French Revolutionary Wars, after which Swedish rule resumed and continued through decades of gradual commercial decline. Sweden finally sold the impoverished colony back to France in 1878, ending nearly a century of Swedish administration.
yearDesc: In 1800, Saint-Barthélemy was a Swedish free-port colony centred on Gustavia, governed at the turn of the year by Georg Henrik af Trolle before Hans Henrik Anckarheim took office in early 1801. Its neutral status let it thrive as a transshipment hub while the surrounding Caribbean was disrupted by the French Revolutionary Wars, drawing hundreds of merchant ships each year. The colony's economy rested on trade, warehousing, and enslaved labour rather than on sugar plantations, since the arid island could not support large-scale agriculture. Swedish authority was thin, exercised through a small garrison and colonial administration answerable to Stockholm. Within months of 1800, in March 1801, a British fleet would compel the outnumbered Swedish garrison to surrender, beginning a roughly sixteen-month British occupation.
issues:
CORRECTED 2 items; other fields confirmed.

1) description sentence 3 — FIXED an anachronism: it said the colony prospered "especially during the American Revolutionary and Napoleonic wars." The American Revolutionary War (1775–1783) ended before the island was even Swedish (1 July 1784) and before Gustavia was declared a free port (7 Sept 1785), so the Swedish neutral-flag trade boom could not have occurred then. The documented boom under Swedish colours was 1793–1815 (scholarship: "Contraband Trade under Swedish Colours: St. Barthélemy's Moment in the Sun, 1793–1815"). Changed to "the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic wars." (A source note that Gustav III was "attracted by the island's prosperity during the American Revolutionary War" refers to pre-Swedish, French-era prosperity, not the free-port boom — so the original phrasing was genuinely wrong.)

2) pop — CHANGED "c. 3,000" → "c. 5,000". The original was an invented interpolation contradicting the sources and violates "never add an unverified fact." Wikipedia's Swedish-colony article explicitly lists the 1800 population as ~5,000, and multiple sources state the island "grew from ~700 (1785) to ~6,000 by 1800 / the beginning of the 19th century" (~5,000 of them in Gustavia). c. 5,000 is the directly sourced 1800 figure; treated as approximate (rapid, war-driven growth; peak-era ~6,000).

CONFIRMED: existedIn1800 = yes (Swedish colony 1784–1878, firmly extant in 1800). officialName "Swedish Colony of Saint-Barthélemy" matches Wikipedia's title for the polity. span 1784–1878 correct (ceded 1 July 1784; France reoccupied 16 March 1878). capitalName Gustavia correct and period-accurate (named 1785 after Gustav III). Governor timeline in yearDesc verified: af Trolle 17 Nov 1795 – 26 Jan 1801, Anckarheim appointed 26 Sept 1800, took command 26 Jan 1801. British occupation 19 March 1801 – 10 July 1802 verified (~16 months). area 21 km² correct (21.24 km²). gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 estimate exists for so small a colony (not in Maddison).

ADJUSTED capital coordinates from -62.8508/17.8964 to -62.8506/17.8979 to sit exactly on Gustavia per Wikipedia (17°53′52″N 62°51′02″W); the original was already within tolerance, this is a minor snap.

name: Kingdom of Nepal
officialName: Kingdom of Nepal (Gorkha Kingdom; Nepali: गोरखा अधिराज्य)
description: The unified Kingdom of Nepal was founded in 1768 when Prithvi Narayan Shah of the Gorkha Shah dynasty conquered the three Malla kingdoms of the Kathmandu Valley and proclaimed a single state. His successors pressed a vigorous expansionist campaign, absorbing the fragmented Baise and Chaubisi hill principalities and pushing the frontier eastward and westward along the Himalaya. Rule was frequently exercised by regents and powerful court factions, since several kings ascended the throne as minors, producing chronic palace intrigue among rival noble houses such as the Pandes, Basnyats, and Thapas. Overexpansion brought conflict with the British East India Company in the Anglo-Nepalese War of 1814–1816, whose Treaty of Sugauli stripped away much of the conquered territory and fixed Nepal's modern boundaries. The Shah monarchy endured, later eclipsed for a century by the hereditary Rana prime ministers, until it was finally abolished and Nepal declared a republic in 2008.
yearDesc: In 1800, the throne was held by the infant king Girvan Yuddha Bikram Shah, who had reigned since his father Rana Bahadur Shah's abdication in March 1799. With Rana Bahadur's senior queen Rajrajeshwari away in Varanasi, the regency in Kathmandu was exercised by his junior queen, Subarnaprabha, amid a contentious court of Kajis in which Damodar Pande was the most influential figure. The deposed Rana Bahadur Shah, unable to reassert his power after most military officers sided with the courtiers, fled in May 1800 to the British-controlled city of Varanasi. The kingdom was still in an expansionist phase, holding recently conquered Kumaon and pressing its frontier westward toward Garhwal and into the Tarai lowlands. Kathmandu remained the royal capital, and the persistent factional rivalry of these years foreshadowed both the coming war with British India and the later ascendancy of Bhimsen Thapa.
issues: CORRECTED the yearDesc regent error. Original claimed "Real authority lay with the regent, Queen Rajrajeshwari" — this is wrong for 1800. In May 1800 Queen Rajrajeshwari (Rana Bahadur's senior/first wife) accompanied the ex-king to Varanasi; she did not re-enter Nepal until 26 July 1801 and only formally assumed the regency on 17 December 1802. The acting regent in Kathmandu in 1800 was his junior/second wife, Queen Subarnaprabha, who "stayed back in Kathmandu to serve as regent" (Wikipedia: Rana Bahadur Shah, Damodar Pande). Rewrote sentence 2 accordingly. Also softened "dominant figure" to "most influential figure" for Damodar Pande — corroborated as the most influential Kaji, though the formal Mul Kaji title around 1799–1800 belonged to Kirtiman Singh Basnyat (assassinated 28 Sept 1801), so calling Pande the outright dominant/chief could mislead. Tightened the expansion sentence: Kumaon was already conquered (1790) by 1800, and Garhwal fell only in 1803–04, so changed "toward the western hill states of Kumaon and Garhwal" to "holding recently conquered Kumaon and pressing its frontier westward toward Garhwal." CONFIRMED unchanged: existedIn1800 yes (kingdom founded 1768, firmly established by 1800); span 1768–2008 (republic declared 28 May 2008); officialName / Gorkha Adhirajya Nepali script; capital Kathmandu with coords 85.324, 27.7172 (correct, within tolerance); founding 1768 by Prithvi Narayan Shah conquering the three Malla kingdoms; 5-sentence political description; Rana Bahadur abdication 23 March 1799 and infant king Girvan Yuddha Bikram Shah; May 1800 flight to Varanasi; Anglo-Nepalese War 1814–1816 and Treaty of Sugauli. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable 1800 figures exist (territory still shifting toward its pre-1814 peak), not fabricated.

name: Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata
officialName: Virreinato del Río de la Plata
description: The Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata was created by royal decree of King Charles III of Spain in 1776 as part of the Bourbon Reforms, carved out of territories previously governed by the Viceroyalty of Peru to counter Portuguese encroachment and rampant contraband in the region. Its first viceroy was Pedro Antonio de Cevallos, followed by reform-minded administrators such as Juan José de Vértiz y Salcedo, who developed Buenos Aires into a major administrative and commercial center. As the last and shortest-lived of Spain's American viceroyalties, it centralized authority over a vast and previously peripheral domain and was reorganized internally through the intendancy system introduced in 1782. In 1806 and 1807 it repelled two British invasions largely through locally raised militias, an experience that fostered a growing sense of Creole self-reliance and autonomy. The Napoleonic upheaval in Spain precipitated its collapse, and the May Revolution of 1810 deposed the viceroy and installed a governing junta in Buenos Aires, effectively ending Spanish viceregal rule and launching the region's wars of independence.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata was an intact and fully functioning Spanish domain governed from Buenos Aires by Viceroy Gabriel de Avilés y del Fierro, the Marqués de Avilés, who had taken office in 1799. Its administration operated through the intendancy system, binding together the provinces of the Río de la Plata basin under Bourbon reformist governance. With Spain at war against Britain, the viceregal authorities focused on coastal defense and on regulating trade, including permitting neutral and foreign shipping at Buenos Aires while combating illicit commerce. The economy rested heavily on cattle ranching, hides, and the silver of Upper Peru, whose Potosí mining wealth flowed through the Atlantic port. The revolutionary crises of the British invasions and the May Revolution still lay several years ahead, leaving the viceroyalty outwardly stable and firmly under Spanish control.
issues: No corrections needed; record confirmed. All claims independently verified against Wikipedia and other reputable sources. Founding (1776, Charles III, Bourbon Reforms), official name (Virreinato del Río de la Plata), capital (Buenos Aires; coords match modern city, correct for 1800), first viceroy (Pedro Antonio de Cevallos), Vértiz as reform administrator, intendancy system (Real Ordenanza 1782), British invasions (1806/1807), and dissolution (May Revolution 1810) all confirmed. The 1800 viceroy is correctly identified as Gabriel de Avilés y del Fierro, Marqués de Avilés, who took office March 1799 and served until 1801 — accurate for the year 1800. Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography/number clauses; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800," with no numeric figures. Population c. 1,240,000 corroborated by the 1801 census (kept as approximate). Span kept as 1776–1810 (effective end of functioning viceregal rule at the May Revolution; the formal Spanish abolition was 1825, but 1776–1810 is the standard functioning span and matches the anchor). Area and GDP correctly dashed — no cleanly sourceable single 1800 figure exists for this multi-country polity.

name: Tuʻi Tonga Empire
description: The Tuʻi Tonga line was founded around 950 CE under the semi-legendary ʻAhoʻeitu and grew into a Polynesian maritime empire that extracted tribute, the ʻinasi, from a network of Pacific island chiefdoms, reaching its height under rulers such as Momo and Tuʻitātui between the 12th and 15th centuries. Its overseas hegemony collapsed with the loss of Samoa after the defeat of Tuʻi Tonga Talakaifaiki, and internal strife and repeated assassinations of rulers pushed the line into decline. Around 1470–1535 Kauʻulufonua I created the Tuʻi Haʻatakalaua title to handle daily governance, leaving the Tuʻi Tonga as a sacred high chief with elevated status but little worldly power, and in 1610 the Tuʻi Kanokupolu line emerged and eventually became Tonga's dominant secular authority. From the 17th century the Tuʻi Tonga functioned chiefly as a religious paramount seated at Muʻa, while the three great lineages coexisted uneasily. The murder of the Tuʻi Kanokupolu Tukuʻaho in 1799 plunged Tonga into roughly fifty years of civil war, and the Tuʻi Tonga title itself ended with the death of Laufilitonga in 1865, being formally abolished in 1875.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Tuʻi Tonga was Fuanunuiava, son of the earlier Tuʻi Tonga Paulaho, who had taken the title from his uncle Maʻulupekotofa around 1795 and would hold it until his death in 1810. In 1800, his role was overwhelmingly sacred and ceremonial, centered on religious obligations at the langi tomb complex and residence at Muʻa (Lapaha) on Tongatapu, with real political and military power lying elsewhere. In 1800, Tonga had just been thrown into turmoil by the 1799 assassination of the Tuʻi Kanokupolu Tukuʻaho, carried out by a faction of chiefs including Tupouniua, Finau ʻUlukālala II, and Fatafehi Fuanunuiava. In 1800, this opened a protracted civil war that set factions across Tongatapu, Haʻapai, and Vavaʻu against one another and left no single authority in control of the islands. In 1800, therefore, the "Tuʻi Tonga Empire" survived only as an ancient sacred title and its seat at Muʻa, no longer a functioning empire or a unified state.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently confirmed. existedIn1800="partial" is correct: the Tuʻi Tonga line (Fuanunuiava) and its sacred seat at Muʻa still existed in 1800, but the "empire"/thalassocracy was long defunct — the correct nuance, not "yes" or "no". Verified against Wikipedia (Tuʻi Tonga, Tuʻi Tonga Empire, History of Tonga, Tukuʻaho): founding c. 950 CE under ʻAhoʻeitu; last holder Laufilitonga died 1865 (title formally abolished 1875) — span "c. 950 – 1865" stands. Fuanunuiava took the title from his uncle Maʻulupekotofa c. 1795, was a son of Tuʻi Tonga Paulaho, died 1810 — confirmed. The 1799 assassination of Tuʻi Kanokupolu Tukuʻaho by a faction including Tupouniua, Finau ʻUlukālala II, and Fuanunuiava, triggering ~50 years of civil war — confirmed. Capital: Wikipedia gives Muʻa/Lapaha at 21.179°S, 175.117°W; the record's lat -21.18 matches and lon -175.09 is off by only ~0.03° (well within ±0.5°) — kept as-is; the erroneous supplied map centroid (lon -52.6, Atlantic) was already correctly replaced with real Tongatapu coordinates. description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause and no demographic figures; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,". pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for this sacred-title polity (no defined territory, census, or Maddison series).

name: Rattanakosin Kingdom
yearDesc: In 1800, the Rattanakosin Kingdom of Siam was ruled by its founder, King Phutthayotfa Chulalok (Rama I), from the newly built capital at Bangkok, established in 1782 after the fall of Thonburi. In 1800, the kingdom had recently repelled the massive Burmese invasion of the Nine Armies' War of 1785–1786 and continued to skirmish with Bodawpaya's Konbaung Burma along the western frontier. In 1800, Siam exercised suzerainty over the three Lao kingdoms of Luang Prabang, Vientiane, and Champasak, and had installed the young King Ang Eng as a vassal ruler in Cambodia in 1794. In 1800, its influence extended over the Lanna principalities around Chiang Mai and over tributary Malay sultanates on the northern peninsula, forming a broad mandala of loosely held dependencies. In 1800, Rama I was consolidating the reunified state by reviving Ayutthaya-era administrative institutions and restoring the Theravada Buddhist sangha and the Thai literary tradition devastated by the earlier sack of Ayutthaya.

Replaced with accurate ongoing-in-1800 consolidation (revival of Ayutthaya-era administration; restoration of the Theravada sangha and Thai literature), which was genuinely underway throughout Rama I's reign. name: Regency of Tunis
description: The Regency of Tunis was founded in 1705 when al-Husayn I ibn Ali, an officer of Greek Cretan descent, seized power and established the Husainid dynasty, supplanting the earlier Muradid rulers. Although the Husainid beys held the Ottoman title of beylerbeyi and acknowledged the sultan's nominal suzerainty, they governed as effectively independent hereditary monarchs who concluded their own treaties with France, Britain, and the Italian states. Its economy long rested on Mediterranean corsairing alongside a state-monopolized grain and agricultural export trade dominated by a Turkish-speaking ruling caste. The regency was repeatedly at odds with neighbouring Algiers and Venice and periodically had to manage restive janissary garrisons and tribal populations. Husainid rule endured until the French invasion of 1881, when the Treaty of Bardo reduced Tunisia to a French protectorate while leaving the beys as figurehead sovereigns.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Regency of Tunis was ruled by Hammuda ibn Ali, the capable and long-reigning fifth Husainid bey, who governed from the Bardo Palace outside Tunis. In 1800, the state was formally an Ottoman vassal but conducted its foreign and military affairs with near-complete autonomy from Constantinople. In 1800, its revenues drew on corsair raiding, which briefly revived amid the Napoleonic Wars, together with grain exports channelled through government monopolies. In 1800, tensions with the neighbouring Regency of Algiers were mounting, foreshadowing the Algerian invasions Hammuda would repel in 1807 and 1813. In 1800, Hammuda's authority remained firm, though he would later face and suppress a janissary revolt in 1811, disbanding that elite corps.
issues: No corrections needed; all claims independently verified against Wikipedia, Britannica, and the scholarly work of Asma Moalla ("The Regency of Tunis and the Ottoman Porte, 1777–1814"). Existence in 1800 confirmed: Husainid Regency ran continuously 1705–1881; in 1800 ruled by Hammuda ibn Ali (r. 26 May 1782 – 15 Sept 1814), the fifth Husainid bey — confirmed. Span 1705–1881 confirmed (founded by Husayn I in July 1705; ended by Treaty of Bardo, 12 May 1881). officialName "Eyalet (Regency) of Tunis" supported: Ottoman correspondence used "eyâlet" and it was listed among the "privileged provinces" (eyâlât-ı mümtâze); Moalla's monograph frames it as a North African eyâlet. Capital Tunis confirmed; coordinates 10.18/36.81 lie within ±0.5° of Tunis city (36.819, 10.166) — kept. Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no standalone geography clause and no pop/area/GDP figure; all facts (Cretan Greek origin, supplanting Muradids, beylerbeyi title with nominal Ottoman suzerainty, autonomous treaty-making, corsairing + grain-monopoly economy, conflicts with Algiers/Venice, janissary/tribal management, 1881 Bardo protectorate) verified. yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences each beginning "In 1800,"; verified fifth bey, Bardo Palace seat, Ottoman-vassal-but-autonomous status, corsair/grain revenues, mounting Algiers tension foreshadowing the 1807 and 1813 Tunisian–Algerian wars (both repelled), and the 1811 janissary revolt suppressed by disbanding the corps. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable regency-wide 1800 figures exist (Tunis city population estimates are city-only, not the state). Verdict: confirmed.

name: Regency of Algiers
description: The Regency of Algiers was founded in 1516 when the corsair brothers Aruj and Hayreddin Barbarossa seized the city and placed it under Ottoman suzerainty, succeeding the Kingdom of Tlemcen. Initially governed by Ottoman-appointed pashas, real power shifted over the seventeenth century to the janissary corps and the corsair captains, and from 1671 the state was headed by an elected dey, with the deys assuming the pasha title themselves from 1710 and largely ceasing to accept representatives from the Sublime Porte. Throughout its history the Regency waged maritime holy war against Christian shipping in the Mediterranean, and its corsairs made it a formidable and much-feared naval power whose ransoming of captives and tribute demands shaped its relations with Europe. Its politics were turbulent, with deys frequently deposed or assassinated by the janissary-dominated council, the Odjak. The state endured until 1830, when a French expeditionary force landed and captured Algiers, ending Ottoman-era rule and beginning the French colonization of Algeria.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Regency of Algiers was ruled by Dey Mustafa Pasha, who had taken power in 1798 and governed from Algiers over a state that was de jure an Ottoman province but de facto independent. Its corsairs remained active across the Mediterranean, preying on European and later American shipping and extracting tribute and ransom from foreign powers. The dey's authority was constrained by the janissary Odjak, the guild of corsair sea captains, and largely autonomous provincial beys in Constantine, Oran, and Titteri. This period was marked by political instability and economic strain, and failed harvests would bring famine from 1803 to 1805. Mustafa Pasha survived plots against him in 1801 and 1804 before being assassinated by the Odjak in August 1805.
issues: No corrections needed — every claim independently corroborated against Wikipedia (Regency of Algiers, Mustapha ben Ibrahim Pacha, governors list) and Grokipedia/Jefferson-papers sources. existedIn1800 "yes": Regency fully extant and self-governing in 1800 (1516–1830). span 1516–1830 confirmed (founded by Aruj/Hayreddin Barbarossa succeeding Kingdom of Tlemcen; ended by the 1830 French capture of Algiers). Capital Algiers, correct period name; coords 36.75N/3.06E land on the historic city (actual ~36.73–36.75N, 3.04–3.09E), within ±0.5° — kept. Description structural claims verified: dey from 1671, deys took pasha title and stopped accepting Sublime Porte representatives from 1710, corsair war, Odjak-driven turbulence, 1830 end — exactly 5 sentences, no geography/number clauses. yearDesc all verified and exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,": Mustafa Baba/Mustafa Pasha dey 1798–1805 (rose after death of dey Ali Hassan); de jure Ottoman / de facto independent; three beyliks Constantine, Oran (Western Beylik, capital Oran from 1791), and Titteri confirmed; famine 1803–1805 confirmed; plots failed in 1801 and 1804 confirmed; assassinated by the Odjak in August 1805 (deposed/killed 31 Aug 1805) confirmed. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for this polity; circulating ~3 million totals are unverifiable and rightly not asserted. Officer/officialName: "Regency of Algiers" is the standard formal historiographical name (Arabic دولة الجزائر, "State of Algiers"); no distinct fuller 1800 legal name in common use, so kept as-is.

name: Spanish Philippines
officialName: Captaincy General of the Philippines (Capitanía General de Filipinas)
description: Spanish colonization of the archipelago began with Miguel López de Legazpi's expedition of 1565, and the Captaincy General of the Philippines was organized as a territory of the Spanish Empire administered through the Viceroyalty of New Spain in Mexico City. Governed by a captain-general appointed on the Crown's behalf, the colony fused royal authority with the pervasive power of the Catholic religious orders, whose friars administered parishes and much of provincial life. Its economy long revolved around the Manila galleon trade linking Acapulco to Asia, while the encomienda system gradually gave way to provinces headed by alcaldes mayores. Late eighteenth-century Bourbon reforms, including the 1784 establishment of an Intendencia and later economic liberalization, sought to make the colony more directly productive for Spain. Spanish rule endured until 1898, when defeat in the Spanish–American War and the Treaty of Paris transferred sovereignty to the United States.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Captaincy General of the Philippines was governed by Rafael María de Aguilar y Ponce de León, who had held office since 1793 and would become the longest-serving Spanish governor of the colony. The territory remained administratively dependent on the Viceroyalty of New Spain, with Manila serving as its seat of government, commerce, and ecclesiastical authority. Aguilar's Enlightenment-influenced administration prioritized defense, reinforcing fortifications and, in that very year, establishing a Naval Command to monitor foreign vessels entering the archipelago. Spanish authority was concentrated in Luzon and the Visayas, while the Muslim sultanates of Mindanao and Sulu remained largely beyond effective colonial control. The Manila galleon trade with Acapulco still operated, though its long dominance was beginning to wane amid the wider disruptions of the Napoleonic era in Europe.
issues: No corrections needed — all claims independently corroborated. VERIFIED: (1) Existence — Captaincy General founded 1565 (Legazpi), fully operational in 1800, governed as a dependency of the Viceroyalty of New Spain until 1821 (Wikipedia: Captaincy General of the Philippines). (2) Governor Rafael María de Aguilar y Ponce de León served 1 Sep 1793 – 7 Aug 1806 and was the longest-serving Spanish governor-general (12 yr 11 mo). (3) Naval Command formed in 1800 to check foreign vessels — confirmed verbatim on the Aguilar Wikipedia article. (4) Capital Manila (Intramuros) at ~120.975E/14.591N rounds to 120.98/14.60, within tolerance; Manila was capital 1595–1898. (5) Span 1565–1898 — Spanish sovereignty ended via the Treaty of Paris signed 10 Dec 1898 (US Senate ratified Feb 1899; the Captaincy General article lists 1565–1899 for the handover, but 1898 is the standard end-of-Spanish-rule year and is accepted). (6) Population c. 1.5–1.9M corroborated: Friar Buzeta's 1799 estimate ~1,502,574 (~1.5M) and a ~1.89M figure cited for 1800. (7) Mindanao/Sulu sultanates beyond effective Spanish control — accurate for 1800. Area and GDP correctly dashed (no reliable period-specific figures). name: Netherlands Antilles
officialName: Colony of Curaçao and Dependencies (Batavian Republic); Windward islands administered separately as Sint Eustatius and Dependencies
yearDesc: In 1800, the Dutch Caribbean islands were possessions of the Batavian Republic rather than any unified "Netherlands Antilles," which would not be created until 1954. In 1800, they were split between the Curaçao colony to the south — Curaçao, Bonaire, and Aruba, centered on Willemstad — and the Windward group of Sint Eustatius, Saba, and Sint Maarten to the north, each administered separately after the West India Company's dissolution in 1792. In 1800, French forces from Guadeloupe attacked Curaçao in July, and after the siege the Dutch governor surrendered the island to the British frigate HMS Nereide on 13 September. In 1800, this began a British occupation of Curaçao that was formalized in 1801 and lasted until the Peace of Amiens returned the island to the Batavian Republic in 1803. In 1800, the islands' economy rested on trade, shipping, and salt, with Curaçao long serving as a major entrepôt for smuggling and the trans-shipment of goods and enslaved people across the Caribbean.
issues: No corrections needed; all load-bearing claims independently verified against Wikipedia and reputable sources. Verified: (1) "Netherlands Antilles" is anachronistic for 1800 — entity created 1954, dissolved 10 Oct 2010. existedIn1800 "partial" is the right call (physical Dutch possessions existed; the named entity did not). (2) In 1800 the islands were under the Batavian Republic (1795–1806), administered as two separate colonies — "Curaçao and Dependencies" (Curaçao, Aruba, Bonaire; capital Willemstad) and "Sint Eustatius and Dependencies" (Sint Eustatius, Sint Maarten, Saba); officialName matches sources. (3) Second Dutch West India Company: dissolution decided 1791, company defunct 1792 → Dutch state assumed direct administration; "1792" is defensible. (4) Curaçao invasion: French from Guadeloupe landed 22 July 1800; HMS Nereide arrived 10 Sept, Governor Lauffer's surrender accepted ~13 Sept (Wikipedia "three days later"), capitulation effective 25 Sept when French withdrew — record's "13 September" surrender date stands. British occupation 1800–1803, returned via Peace of Amiens. (5) Willemstad coords 12.11/-68.93 match actual 12.108/-68.934 (within tolerance). (6) yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences, all begin "In 1800,", all political/economic with no numeric pop/area/GDP; kept as-is. (7) pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable 1800 figures for this grouping. Notes (not corrections): span "1792 – 1954" is an acknowledged single-entity simplification of two separately-administered colonies; the yearDesc's "formalized in 1801" is an underdetermined phrasing nuance (sources give British control 1800–1803 with the Sept 1800 surrender), but the load-bearing facts are solid, so no value changed. description correctly "" since reuseExisting is true.

name: Regency of Tripoli
officialName: Regency of Tripoli (Karamanli Tripolitania, an autonomous province of the Ottoman Empire)
description: The Regency of Tripoli became a hereditary autonomous state in 1711 when the Janissary officer Ahmed Karamanli seized power by killing the Ottoman governor and won recognition as pasha, founding the Karamanli dynasty. While it continued to pay nominal tribute to the Ottoman sultan, it functioned in practice as an independent polity, financing itself largely through Mediterranean corsairing and tribute extracted from foreign powers. Power passed within the family until Yusuf Karamanli seized the throne in 1795 after recapturing Tripoli with Tunisian help and exiling his brother. Under Yusuf the regency fought the First Barbary War against the United States from 1801 to 1805 and later saw its corsair economy collapse as European powers suppressed piracy after 1815. Beset by debt and civil war following Yusuf's abdication in 1832, the dynasty ended in 1835 when Sultan Mahmud II deposed Ali II and restored direct Ottoman rule.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Regency of Tripoli was ruled by Yusuf Karamanli, who had held power since 1795 and governed as an autonomous pasha under nominal Ottoman suzerainty. Its economy still depended heavily on corsairing and the tribute European and American governments paid to protect their shipping from Tripolitan raiders. The regency had signed the Treaty of Tripoli with the United States in 1796, but relations were deteriorating as Yusuf pressed for larger payments. Within a year these tensions would erupt into the First Barbary War, which Yusuf effectively opened in May 1801 by having the flagpole at the American consulate cut down. The state was thus a small but assertive Barbary power, wealthy from maritime coercion yet increasingly exposed to the naval strength of rising Western nations.
issues: No changes — all claims independently verified and confirmed against Wikipedia (Karamanli dynasty, Yusuf Karamanli). existedIn1800: correct (regency ran 1711–1835, Yusuf ruling from 1795; no anachronism). officialName: accurate (autonomous Karamanli-ruled, nominally Ottoman regency). span 1711–1835: confirmed (founded by Ahmed Karamanli's 1711 coup; ended 1835 when Mahmud II deposed/exiled last ruler Ali II). Capital Tripoli, period-correct name; coords 32.887N/13.187E sit on the actual city (~32.885N/13.18E, within ±0.5°). name: Joseon
officialName: Great Joseon State (대조선국 / 大朝鮮國)
description: The Joseon dynasty was founded in 1392 by the general Yi Seonggye, who overthrew the Goryeo dynasty and took the throne as Taejo, establishing a state governed by Neo-Confucian principles that would endure for over five centuries. Its kings ruled through a centralized bureaucracy staffed by a scholar-official class recruited by civil examination, while a hereditary yangban aristocracy dominated politics and often coalesced into rival court factions. Diplomatically the dynasty operated within the sadae framework of ritual subordination as a tributary first of Ming and, after the seventeenth-century Manchu invasions, of Qing China, yet it retained broad internal autonomy. The dynasty weathered the devastating Japanese invasions of the 1590s and later cultivated an isolationist stance that earned it the epithet the "Hermit Kingdom." It endured until 1897, when King Gojong abandoned the Joseon name and proclaimed the Korean Empire in a bid to assert full sovereign independence.
yearDesc: In 1800, the reform-minded King Jeongjo died suddenly of an abscess, cutting short a reign noted for scholarship, the Kyujanggak royal library, and efforts to balance the court factions. He was succeeded by his ten-year-old son Sunjo, so effective power passed to the Grand Queen Dowager Jeongsun, who ruled as regent on the young king's behalf. Aligning with the conservative Noron faction, the regency moved swiftly against political rivals and Catholic converts, setting the stage for the Sinyu Catholic Persecution that erupted the following year in 1801. The kingdom remained a tributary of Qing China under the sadae framework while continuing its guarded, isolationist posture toward the wider world. This transition also marked the beginning of a long era of in-law clan domination, as powerful lineages such as the Andong Kim increasingly controlled the court around the boy-king.
issues: No changes; all claims independently corroborated. existedIn1800=yes verified (Jeongjo died 1800, succeeded by 10-year-old Sunjo under Grand Queen Dowager Jeongsun's regency — no anachronism). officialName "Great Joseon State (대조선국/大朝鮮國)" confirmed (Kingdom of Great Joseon). span 1392–1897 correct: founded 1392 by Yi Seonggye/Taejo, Joseon name ended 1897 when Gojong proclaimed the Korean Empire (the royal house continued to 1910 as the Korean Empire, but the Joseon polity ended 1897). Capital Hanseong is the correct 1800 name (漢城) for Seoul; coordinates 126.98E/37.57N sit on Seoul city center (within ±0.5°). description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause and no numeric figures. yearDesc begins "In 1800," and is exactly 5 sentences; Jeongjo's death from an abscess, the Noron/Byeokpa alignment, the 1801 Sinyu (Catholic) Persecution, continued Qing tributary status, and the Andong Kim in-law clan ascendancy all verified. pop kept as a range c.13.5–18.5M — corroborated (~13.8M peninsula per Statista low end; ~18.5M per scholarly Joseon-census-adjusted estimates high end) — Joseon censuses undercount, so the wide range is justified rather than a single point figure. area and gdp appropriately dashed: no reliable contemporaneous area figure and no dependable Maddison-style 1800 GDP for Korea (Maddison's Korea series effectively begins ~1820).

name: Zanzibar
officialName: Omani dominions of Zanzibar and the Swahili coast (dependency of the Al Bu Sa'id Sultanate of Oman)
description: Omani forces under Imam Saif bin Sultan of the Ya'rubid dynasty expelled the Portuguese from the Swahili coast and captured Zanzibar in 1698, bringing the island under the overseas dominion of Oman. After the Al Bu Sa'id (Busaidi) dynasty took power in Oman in 1749, its rulers installed governors on Unguja and made Zanzibar Town the seat of Omani authority along the coast, competing with the rival Mazrui family that controlled Mombasa. Through the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries the Busaidi consolidated their hold over Unguja, Pemba, Kilwa, and Lamu, drawing on Indian financiers and the Indian Ocean trade to build a commercial entrepot. Said bin Sultan, who ruled Oman from 1804, deepened this involvement, transferring his court to Stone Town by the 1830s and finally subduing the Mazrui at Mombasa in 1837. On his death in 1856 the realm was divided between his sons, and Zanzibar was formally separated from Oman as an independent sultanate in 1861.
yearDesc: In 1800, Zanzibar was a dependency of the Al Bu Sa'id Sultanate of Oman, then ruled from Muscat by Sultan bin Ahmad, and administered locally by an Omani governor from Zanzibar Town. The island served as the principal Busaidi base and entrepot on the East African coast, its commerce increasingly funneled through Indian merchants and financiers. Busaidi authority extended over Unguja, Pemba, Kilwa, and Lamu, but the rival Mazrui family still held Mombasa in defiance of the Omani sultan. The clove-plantation economy that would later define the island had not yet developed, and the coast's wealth rested chiefly on the trade in slaves, ivory, and goods passing between the interior and the wider Indian Ocean world. The sovereign Sultanate of Zanzibar did not yet exist, that separation from Oman lying more than half a century in the future.
issues: No changes needed; all claims independently corroborated. VERIFIED: (1) Omani capture of Zanzibar 1698 under Imam Saif bin Sultan of the Ya'rubid dynasty, expelling the Portuguese (Siege of Mombasa 1696–1698). (2) Al Bu Sa'id (Busaidi) came to power in Oman in 1749 — Ahmad bin Said elected Imam at Rustaq 9 June 1749 (dynasty is sometimes dated to its 1744 founding, but 1749 as stated is accurate). (3) Sultan bin Ahmad ruled Oman from Muscat 1792–1804, so he is correctly named as the 1800 ruler. (4) Mazrui family held Mombasa in rivalry with the Busaidi until Said bin Sultan subdued them in 1837 — confirmed. (5) Said moved his court to Stone Town: sources give 1832 or 1840, so 'by the 1830s' is an appropriate hedge. (6) Realm divided on Said's death in 1856; formally separated into independent Sultanate of Zanzibar vs. Muscat and Oman by the Canning Award on 6 April 1861 — confirmed. existedIn1800='partial' is correct: an Omani-ruled Zanzibar existed but not a sovereign Zanzibari sultanate (anachronism for 1800). CAPITAL: Stone Town/Zanzibar Town coordinates verified at actual -6.162, 39.192; supplied 39.19/-6.16 is within ±0.5° (kept). The supplied centroid (41.4, -3.4) was correctly rejected as sitting off the northern Kenyan/Somali coast. description = 5 sentences of political history, no geography clause, no numeric figures. yearDesc = 5 sentences beginning 'In 1800,'; clove economy correctly noted as undeveloped in 1800. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for this dependency.

name: Lunda Empire
officialName: Kingdom of Lunda (Empire of the Mwata Yamvo)
description: The Lunda Empire coalesced around 1665 when tradition holds that Ilunga Tshibinda, a hunter-prince from the Luba world, married the Lunda ruler Lueji, whose lineage produced the first paramount chiefs bearing the title Mwata Yamvo. Its first well-attested sovereign, Nawej, is dated to about 1695, and under him and his successors the state grew into a sprawling tributary confederation governed through a ruling council and a system of positional succession in which each new king assumed the name and identity of his predecessor. The empire expanded aggressively across the southern savanna, spinning off satellite states such as the eastern Lunda kingdom of the Mwata Kazembe, and grew wealthy by taxing and controlling long-distance trade in ivory and slaves that linked it to Portuguese Angola on the Atlantic and to Swahili-Arab merchants toward the Indian Ocean. It reached the height of its power in the first half of the nineteenth century before the militarized, gun-armed Chokwe, initially welcomed as elephant hunters, turned against their overlords and dismembered the state. Chokwe forces overran the royal capital and killed the reigning Mwata Yamvo in 1887, and the collapsing empire was soon partitioned between Portuguese Angola and the Belgian Congo Free State, ending its independence.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Lunda Empire was an independent and powerful African state governed by the Mwata Yamvo, with Nawej II a Ditend beginning his long reign around this time. In 1800, the court resided at the great royal capital of Musumba, a fortified center of paved courtyards, earthen walls, and thousands of retainers from which the king ruled a wide network of tributary chiefs. In 1800, the empire's prosperity rested on its monopolistic control of caravan trade, exporting ivory and captives westward to Portuguese and Luso-African traders and receiving cloth, guns, and other goods in return. In 1800, the Lunda system of provincial governors and tribute collectors held together a vast confederation, while satellite kingdoms like that of the Mwata Kazembe recognized the Lunda origin of their own ruling houses. In 1800, the Chokwe who would later shatter the empire were still largely subordinate hunters and clients, so Lunda authority over the region remained essentially intact and unchallenged by European powers.
issues: Corrected one claim. The description originally named the 1887 casualty as "the Mwata Yamvo Mulaji II"; this specific name is not supported by the scholarly rulers list. Wikipedia's List of rulers of the Lunda Empire (based on Hoover 1978) places "Mulaj a Namwan" at 1852–1857 and names "Mbal III a Kalong" as the ruler reigning in 1887, while the main Lunda Empire article names no ruler for the Chokwe overrun. The "Mulaji II in 1887" attribution appears only in a cluster of low-tier secondary blogs (theafricanhistory.com, searchinginhistory, Fatherland Gazette). Per the adversarial DASH-the-shaky-value rule, I removed the contested name and kept the well-corroborated fact: "Chokwe forces overran the royal capital and killed the reigning Mwata Yamvo in 1887." Everything else confirmed: existedIn1800=yes; officialName; span c.1665–1887 (Wikipedia infobox 17th c.–1887, founding c.1665); capital Musumba at lat -8.5555, lon 22.6762 (matches -8.56/22.68 within ±0.01°); first well-attested sovereign Nawej c.1695; founding tradition (Ilunga Tshibinda + Lueji → Mwata Yamvo); Nawej II a Ditend acceding ~1800 per Wikipedia rulers list (note: African History Extra alternatively gives r.1821–1853, but the yearDesc's soft "around this time" is defensible on the 1800 dating); Mwata Kazembe eastern offshoot; ivory/slave trade linking Portuguese Angola and Swahili-Arab merchants; partition between Portuguese Angola and Belgian Congo Free State. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no sourced 1800 figures (area estimates of ~150,000 km² by 1680 / ~300,000 km² at 19th-c. peak are not a sourced 1800 value).

name: Spanish Louisiana
officialName: Province of Louisiana (Provincia de la Luisiana), a province of the Spanish Empire under the Captaincy General of Cuba
description: Spain gained Louisiana from France under the secret Treaty of Fontainebleau of 1762, formalized when France ceded it in the 1763 Treaty of Paris ending the Seven Years' War. Spanish authority was resisted at first, sparking the Louisiana Rebellion of 1768 by French colonists, which Governor Alejandro O'Reilly crushed the following year before imposing Spanish institutions. Under governors such as Bernardo de Gálvez the colony aided the American Revolution and campaigned against Britain, seizing British West Florida between 1779 and 1781. Governed from New Orleans as a province tied to the Captaincy General of Cuba, it saw substantial immigration, plantation growth, and the rebuilding of its capital after devastating fires in 1788 and 1794. Its Spanish era ended when the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso of 1800 secretly returned it to France, leading to the formal 1803 handover and the immediate sale of the territory to the United States in the Louisiana Purchase.
yearDesc: In 1800, Spanish Louisiana remained under Spanish administration governed from New Orleans, with the Marquess of Casa Calvo serving as interim governor after Manuel Gayoso de Lemos died in 1799. In 1800, on October 1, Spain and Napoleonic France secretly signed the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso, agreeing to retrocede the colony to France in exchange for Italian territory. In 1800, this transfer stayed hidden and unexecuted, so Spanish officials, law, and garrisons continued to run the province day to day. In 1800, New Orleans thrived as a busy river and Gulf port, its trade increasingly bound up with the expanding United States upriver. In 1800, the colony faced growing pressure from American settlers pressing westward and from disputes over navigation rights and the deposit at New Orleans.
pop: ~44,000 (1800 Lower Louisiana census incl. West Florida: ~19,852 free, ~24,264 enslaved)
area: ~2,140,000 km² (~828,000 sq mi, per the later Louisiana Purchase extent)
