name: Rajput states
description: The Rajput states of Rajputana were never a single kingdom but a constellation of independent Rajput dynasties, chiefly the Kachhwaha rulers of Jaipur (formerly Amber), the Rathore rulers of Marwar (Jodhpur) and Bikaner, and the Sisodia rulers of Mewar (Udaipur), whose lineages traced back to medieval foundings such as Marwar in 1243 and Jodhpur city in 1459. After the decline of Mughal authority in the eighteenth century these states asserted autonomy but fell into chronic internecine rivalry and disputed successions that neighbouring powers exploited. From the 1750s onward the Maratha chiefs Sindhia of Gwalior and Holkar of Indore repeatedly invaded, extracted crushing tributes, and reduced most eastern Rajput courts to tributary status, a pressure compounded by devastating Pindari and Pathan raids. Ruinous conflicts such as the war over the Mewar princess Krishna Kumari in the 1800s left the states militarily exhausted and financially drained. Their independence effectively ended when, after the Third Anglo-Maratha War, the major states accepted British subsidiary alliances in 1817–1818, entering British paramountcy while retaining nominal internal self-rule.
yearDesc: In 1800, Rajputana was a patchwork of separate Rajput kingdoms rather than one state, each ruled by its own maharaja and locked in mutual rivalry. Jaipur was ruled by Sawai Pratap Singh, Marwar (Jodhpur) by Bhim Singh, and Mewar (Udaipur) by its own Bhim Singh of the Sisodia line. The Marathas under Daulat Rao Sindhia and Yashwant Rao Holkar dominated the region, exacting heavy tributes and periodically ravaging the countryside, while Pindari bands added to the devastation. The states had by this date appealed to the British East India Company for protection, but the Company's policy of non-interference meant those requests were refused. Their courts were beset by succession disputes and financial exhaustion, leaving them unable to resist Maratha extortion in the years around 1800.
issues: No corrections needed; every claim was independently re-verified and confirmed. RULERS (all reigning in 1800): Jaipur — Sawai Pratap Singh (r. 1778–1 Aug 1803); Marwar/Jodhpur — Bhim Singh (r. 17 Jul 1793–19 Oct 1803); Mewar/Udaipur — Bhim Singh of Mewar (Sisodia, r. 1778–1828) — all confirmed via Wikipedia. MARATHA CHIEFS: Daulat Rao Sindhia of Gwalior (r. 1794–1827) and Yashwant Rao Holkar of Indore both in power c.1800, extracting tribute and ravaging Rajputana (corroborated, incl. Warren Hastings' 1815 note that Jaipur/Jodhpur/Udaipur were "devastated" by Scindia, Holkar, Pindaris, Amir Khan). FOUNDING DATES: Marwar 1243 (Rao Siha) and Jodhpur city 1459 (Rao Jodha) confirmed (Wikipedia's stated 1243; some sources give 1226/1240 for Marwar). KRISHNA KUMARI war: the succession/marriage crisis peaked 1807–1810 (she was poisoned 1810) — the description's "in the 1800s" reads as the decade and is acceptable. BRITISH NON-INTERFERENCE (yearDesc sentence 4): confirmed — Wikipedia: "In the late eighteenth century, despite the request from two Rajput rulers for British support, the British East India Company initially refused to support the Rajput states," and "Some Rajput states, in 1800s, appealed... but their requests for assistance were denied," so appeals/refusals "by this date" are period-accurate. SUBSIDIARY ALLIANCES 1817–1818 confirmed after the Third Anglo-Maratha War (Jodhpur 6 Jan 1818, Udaipur 13 Jan 1818, Jaipur by Apr 1818, negotiated by Metcalfe). Description = exactly 5 sentences (political history, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP figure); yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". span/capital/lon/lat intentionally blank/null — correct for a collective label of many dynasties with no single seat or founding date. pop/area/gdp dashed — no reliable aggregate 1800 figure exists for this multi-state grouping; none fabricated.

name: Sultanate of Brunei
description: The Sultanate of Brunei took shape in the fourteenth century when its rulers adopted Islam, and it grew into a formidable maritime power that at its sixteenth-century zenith under Sultan Bolkiah claimed suzerainty over much of coastal Borneo and parts of the Philippines. It was governed as a Malay monarchy in which the sultan ruled with a council of great nobles, drawing wealth from trade, tribute, and control of regional sea lanes. From the late seventeenth century the state declined amid recurring succession disputes, a civil war, European commercial pressure, and endemic piracy, and its authority steadily contracted toward its Bornean heartland. In the nineteenth century this decline accelerated as the sultanate ceded Sarawak to James Brooke in 1841 and progressively lost Labuan and its northern Bornean territories. Weakened and shorn of most of its former domains, Brunei accepted British protectorate status under Sultan Hashim Jalilul Alam Aqamaddin in 1888, ending its era as an independent power.
yearDesc: In 1800, Brunei was an independent sultanate ruled by Sultan Muhammad Tajuddin, whose reign spanned the turn of the century. By this date its once-vast maritime empire had shrunk to control of northwestern Borneo, with outlying claims contested by the rival Sultanate of Sulu. The court maintained the traditional apparatus of a Malay monarchy centered on the sultan and his senior nobles, and Muhammad Tajuddin pursued diplomacy abroad, sending envoys to China to promote Brunei's trade and to the Spanish authorities in Manila. Its economy still rested on regional maritime commerce, tribute from dependencies, and the export of forest and jungle produce. European colonial encroachment and coastal piracy already pressed on the state, foreshadowing the sharper territorial losses that would come after 1841.
issues: No corrections. All claims independently verified against Wikipedia and reputable sources. existedIn1800=yes confirmed: fully sovereign in 1800; Sarawak cession (1841), Labuan (1846), and British protectorate (1888) all postdate 1800. Reigning sultan Muhammad Tajuddin confirmed — Wikipedia's List of sultans dates his first reign 1778–1804 (then a second reign 1804–1807), so he was unambiguously on the throne in 1800 and the "reign spanned the turn of the century" phrasing is accurate. Span c.1368–1888 confirmed (Islamic conversion of Awang Alak Betatar / Sultan Muhammad Shah ~1368; protectorate 1888). officialName correct. Capital confirmed: Brunei Town / Kampong Ayer on the Brunei River, present-day Bandar Seri Begawan; coordinates 114.94, 4.89 match BSB (114.940, 4.890) within tolerance — no fix needed. description = exactly 5 sentences, political history only, no geography clause, no numbers; Bolkiah zenith, decline factors, Sarawak 1841, Labuan, 1888 under Hashim Jalilul Alam Aqamaddin all corroborated. yearDesc = exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,"; shrinkage to NW Borneo, Sulu rivalry over northern Borneo, China envoy + Spanish/Manila diplomacy, forest-produce economy, and piracy/colonial pressure all corroborated. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable ~1800 figures exist for this state and Maddison has no Brunei series; not fabricated.

name: Kingdom of Kandy
description: The Kingdom of Kandy was founded around 1469 when Senasammata Vikramabahu asserted the central highlands' independence from the Kingdom of Kotte, and it was re-established as the sole surviving native polity by Vimaladharmasuriya I in 1592 after the Sitawaka interlude. For over two centuries its rulers waged a long resistance against the Portuguese and then the Dutch, exploiting the mountainous interior to preserve Sinhalese sovereignty and guardianship of the sacred Tooth Relic. After a succession crisis on the death of Vira Narendrasinha, the throne passed in 1739 to his brother-in-law Sri Vijaya Rajasinha, beginning the Tamil-speaking Nayak dynasty from South India, whose kings patronised Theravada Buddhism despite their own Hindu background. The last Nayak king, Sri Vikrama Rajasinha, alienated the powerful Kandyan chiefs through erratic and brutal rule, notably the execution of the minister Pilima Talawe and the killing of Ehelepola's family. In 1815 defecting nobles helped British forces under Governor Robert Brownrigg seize the capital and capture the king, and the Kandyan Convention formally ended the kingdom and the more than two-thousand-year Sinhalese monarchy.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Kingdom of Kandy stood as the last independent native state on the island, ruled by the young Nayak king Sri Vikrama Rajasinha, who had been crowned in 1798 at about eighteen. Its coastal lowlands were by then held by the British East India Company, which had displaced the Dutch in 1796 and administered the maritime provinces under Governor Frederick North. Real power at court was contested between the king and his chief minister, the Adigar Pilima Talawe, whose intrigues shaped Kandyan policy toward the British. Tensions with the Company over trade and detained subjects were mounting toward open conflict, which broke out in the First Kandyan War of 1803. The kingdom's rugged interior, its guardianship of the Tooth Relic, and the divided loyalties of its Kandyan aristocracy defined its precarious survival in these years.
issues: No changes made; all claims independently verified against Wikipedia (Kingdom of Kandy, Sri Vikrama Rajasinha, Nayaks of Kandy) and Sri Lankan history sources. Confirmed: existedIn1800=yes (fully independent interior kingdom until British conquest 1815); span 1469–1815 (founded by Senasammata Vikramabahu 1469, ended by Kandyan Convention 2 March 1815); capital Kandy/Senkadagalapura at 7.2906N 80.6356E (matches modern Kandy 7.2906N 80.6336E, within tolerance); Vimaladharmasuriya I re-established the kingdom 1592 after Sitawaka; Nayak dynasty began 1739 with Sri Vijaya Rajasinha, brother-in-law of Vira Narendrasinha; Sri Vikrama Rajasinha crowned 1798 at ~18 (born Kannasamy Nayak); he executed Pilima Talawe and killed Ehelepola's wife and children; British displaced Dutch 1796 and administered maritime provinces under Governor Frederick North; First Kandyan War 1803 (British entered Kandy 22 March 1803); 1815 conquest under Governor Robert Brownrigg. Noted nuance (not an error): the Nayaks were of Telugu (Balija) origin but Tamil-speaking and used Tamil/Sinhala as court languages; the description's "Tamil-speaking Nayak dynasty" is directly supported by Wikipedia's Kingdom of Kandy article, which calls them "a member of the Tamil-speaking Nayak house from southern India," so it was left as written. pop/area/gdp correctly dashed — no reliable 1800 figures exist for the Kandyan interior kingdom specifically (Maddison covers Ceylon as a whole, not Kandy), so any number would be fabricated. Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no geography clause or numeric figures; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,".

name: Lao kingdoms
officialName: The Lao kingdoms of Luang Prabang, Vientiane and Champasak (Siamese tributary states)
description: The unified Lao kingdom of Lan Xang fragmented in 1707 into three rival successor kingdoms—Luang Prabang in the north, Vientiane in the centre, and Champasak in the south. Weakened by their mutual rivalry, all three fell under foreign domination during the eighteenth century, alternately submitting to Burma and to Siam. Following the Lao–Siamese War of 1778–1779, King Taksin's Siam subjugated the three kingdoms, carrying off the revered Emerald Buddha and Phra Bang and reducing each to a tributary whose rulers were confirmed and whose succession was controlled from Bangkok. They persisted as semi-autonomous vassals for over a century, with Vientiane destroyed and dissolved in 1828 after King Anouvong's failed rebellion against Siamese overlordship. The remaining kingdoms endured under Siamese suzerainty until 1893, when the Franco-Siamese crisis forced Siam to cede the Lao lands east of the Mekong to French Indochina.
yearDesc: In 1800, the three Lao kingdoms all stood as tributary vassals of Siam under the newly established Chakri dynasty in Bangkok. Luang Prabang was ruled by King Anurutha, who had been held hostage in Bangkok and only permitted to return to reign in 1792 under Siamese overlordship. Vientiane was governed by King Inthavong, who had succeeded his deposed brother Nanthasen in 1795 and ruled as a loyal Siamese tributary. Champasak in the south was under King Fay Na, installed in 1791 and likewise subordinate to Bangkok, which controlled the succession and demanded tribute from all three courts. Each king retained internal authority over his own realm, but real sovereignty lay with Siam, and the future rebel Anouvong—then serving under Inthavong—had not yet risen to the Vientiane throne.
issues: No changes; all claims independently verified against Wikipedia/history sources. Political history confirmed: 1707 tripartite fragmentation of Lan Xang into Luang Prabang, Vientiane, and Champasak; 1778–1779 Lao–Siamese War under Taksin's Thonburi/Siam, with the Emerald Buddha (Phra Kaew) and Phra Bang carried off; tributary status 1779–1893; Vientiane obliterated and dissolved in 1828 after Anouvong's failed rebellion; 1893 Franco-Siamese crisis (Paknam Incident) forced cession of Lao lands east of the Mekong to French Indochina. All three 1800 rulers confirmed: Anurutha of Luang Prabang (r. 1792–1819, hostage in Bangkok, returned 1792), Inthavong of Vientiane (r. 1795–1804/05, succeeded deposed brother Nanthasen 1795), Fay Na of Champasak (r. 1791–1811, installed by Rama I). Anouvong did not accede at Vientiane until 1804/05, so "not yet risen to the throne" in 1800 is correct. Chakri dynasty founded 1782, correctly described as "newly established." Capital Vientiane coordinates 17.97N/102.6E verified (actual 17.967N, 102.6E, within tolerance). Description is exactly 5 sentences of political history with no numeric pop/area/GDP figures; yearDesc is exactly 5 sentences beginning "In 1800,". Population, area, and GDP left dashed—not reliably sourceable for these pre-modern polities. Minor note (not corrected, does not affect record): Champasak's formal secession is sometimes dated 1713 rather than 1707, but the 1707 fragmentation date for the collective successor-state process is standard and acceptable. existedIn1800 = yes is correct.

name: Sultanate of Morocco
description: The Alaouite dynasty, a Sharifian family claiming descent from the Prophet Muhammad, rose to power from the Tafilalt oasis where Mawlay al-Sharif was proclaimed leader in 1631, and it has ruled Morocco ever since. Moulay al-Rashid reunified the country and captured Fez in 1666, restoring centralized monarchy after the decline of the Saadi Sultanate, and his successor Moulay Ismail (r. 1672-1727) brought the state to its apogee through strong central rule backed by the Abid al-Bukhari slave army. After Ismail's death the sultanate endured decades of civil strife and contested successions until Sultan Mohammed III stabilized it, followed by the brief violent reign of al-Yazid. Moulay Slimane (r. 1792-1822) continued centralization, suppressed coastal piracy, and, influenced by reformist ideas, cracked down on Sufi brotherhoods, though he faced serious tribal rebellions late in his reign. The dynasty preserved Morocco's independence through the 19th century until European pressure culminated in the French and Spanish protectorates of 1912, after which it regained sovereignty in 1956 and continues to reign today.
yearDesc: In 1800, Morocco was an independent sovereign sultanate ruled by Moulay Slimane of the Alaouite dynasty, who had held the throne since 1792. In 1800, his authority extended southward as the inhabitants of the Tuat oases agreed to pay taxes to the sultan, strengthening the reach and revenue of the Makhzen. In 1800, Slimane pursued a policy of centralization, having recently recovered Oujda and the eastern provinces from the Turks in 1798 and fixed the border at the Wadi Kiss. In 1800, he had restricted trade with the European powers after reversing his father's overseas commerce policy, amid tensions with Spain and Portugal, while maintaining cordial relations with the young United States. In 1800, Fez served as his principal capital, from which the court and army governed a realm of largely autonomous tribes.
issues: CORRECTED two yearDesc sentences. (1) Sentence 3 originally claimed that "In 1800, Slimane...curbing the Sufi mystical orders and the veneration of saints in line with austere reformist currents" — this is ANACHRONISTIC for 1800: the documented anti-Sufi/anti-saint-veneration crackdown began around 1805 (removal of his father's mausoleum in Rabat) and intensified only after the 1811 letter from Saud ibn Abdulaziz brought Wahhabi influence, with the qubba over his father's grave removed in 1812. Replaced with a corroborable 1800 centralization fact (recovery of Oujda and the eastern provinces from the Ottomans in 1798, border set at the Wadi Kiss; Makhzen centralization active by 1800 per Wikipedia). (2) Sentence 5 originally claimed Fez's capital status was "shared in practice with Marrakesh" — UNSUPPORTED: sources designate Fez as Slimane's principal/sole reign seat (1792-1822); Marrakesh was a campaign site and his place of death, not a documented co-capital. Rewrote to keep Fez as principal capital. VERIFIED and kept: existedIn1800 yes (independent Alaouite Sultanate under Moulay Slimane, r. 1792-1822, corroborated by Wikipedia/Archnet); dynasty founding 1631 (Mawlay al-Sharif proclaimed in Tafilalt); span "1631 – Present" (continuous Alaouite rule to today); capital Fez with coords ~34.03N, 5.00W (correct); officialName "Sharifian Empire" (French l'Empire chérifien, the recognized formal designation; "Sharifian Sultanate" is an equally valid alternative); description exactly 5 sentences of political history, no geography/number clause; 1800 events Tuat taxation, restricted Europe trade (reversal began 1798), cordial US relations, piracy suppression all corroborated. pop/area/gdp DASHED — no reliable well-sourced ~1800 figures exist (population estimates range widely and Maddison lacks a robust separate Morocco 1800 series); not fabricated.

name: Madras Presidency
description: The Presidency of Fort St George grew from the English East India Company's coastal trading settlement at Madras, founded in 1639–40 and elevated to a full presidency in 1684. Governed by a Governor-in-Council answerable after 1774 to the Governor-General at Calcutta, it was transformed from a commercial factory into a territorial administration through the Company's wars against its Indian rivals. Its power in the south was consolidated by the Anglo-Mysore Wars against Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan, culminating in Tipu's defeat and death at Seringapatam in 1799, followed by cessions from the Nizam of Hyderabad in 1800 and the annexation of the Carnatic from the Nawab of Arcot in 1801. Ruled directly by the Company until the Crown assumed control after the 1857 rebellion, it then continued as a province of the British Raj. It endured as an administrative unit until the independence and partition of British India in 1947.
yearDesc: In 1800, the Presidency of Fort St George was governed by Edward Clive, who served as Governor of Madras from 1798 to 1803. It had just emerged victorious from the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War of 1799, in which the storming of Seringapatam ended the reign of Tipu Sultan and removed the Company's most formidable southern adversary. In this year the Nizam of Hyderabad ceded the districts of Rayalaseema, the so-called Ceded Districts, to the Company in return for the maintenance of British troops under the subsidiary alliance system. The Nawab of Arcot still nominally held the Carnatic, though his effective independence was on the verge of extinction and his territory would be annexed the following year in 1801. The presidency thus stood as the dominant British power across the Coromandel coast and much of the Deccan interior, administered from Fort St George at Madras.
issues: CORRECTED: The description's opening sentence claimed the presidency was "elevated to a full presidency in 1684 under its first President, William Gyfford." This is a factual error contradicted by cross-corroborating sources (Wikipedia's Elihu Yale, William Gyfford, and List of colonial governors of Madras articles all agree): when Madras was elevated to a presidency on 8 August 1684, ELIHU YALE was made its first President; William Gyfford was the Agent at the time of the elevation (Agent 1681–1684) and became the SECOND President in January 1685. (One source, "History of the Madras Presidency," loosely calls Gyfford "the first President," but this is a minority/imprecise reading and cannot be relied on.) Since the president attribution was contested, I removed the disputed "under its first President, William Gyfford" clause entirely rather than assert a contested name, keeping only the confirmed founding (1639–40) and elevation (1684) facts. Description now exactly 5 sentences, no geography clause, no numeric figures. VERIFIED AND UNCHANGED: existedIn1800=yes (fully functioning EIC presidency in 1800); officialName "Presidency of Fort St George" (confirmed formal name until 1937); span 1684–1947 (elevated 1684, ended at 1947 independence; formally became Madras State 1950); capital Madras at 80.28E/13.08N (Chennai coordinates, within tolerance). yearDesc fully confirmed and unchanged: Governor Edward Clive 1798–1803; Seringapatam/Tipu's death 4 May 1799; Nizam's cession of the Rayalaseema Ceded Districts in 1800 under the subsidiary alliance; Nawab of Arcot's Carnatic annexed 1801 (Carnatic Treaty, 31 July 1801) — begins "In 1800," and is exactly 5 sentences. DASHED (unchanged): pop, area, gdp — no reliable ~1800 figures for the Company province, correctly left as em dashes rather than fabricated.

name: Kingdom of Travancore
description: Travancore was founded in 1729 by Anizham Thirunal Marthanda Varma, who consolidated the older principality of Venad and expanded it by conquering neighbouring chiefdoms such as Quilon, Kayamkulam, and Kottayam. Marthanda Varma centralised royal authority by suppressing the Ettuveetil Pillamar nobility, dedicated the kingdom to the deity Padmanabha in the 1750 Trippadidanam, and repelled the Dutch East India Company at the Battle of Colachel in 1741. His successor Karthika Thirunal Rama Varma, known as Dharma Raja, presided over a cultural flowering, defended the realm against Tipu Sultan of Mysore, and entered into treaties with the British East India Company. Under Balarama Varma the state came firmly within the Company's subsidiary system, and the revolt of Velu Thampi Dalawa in 1808–1809 marked the final challenge to British paramountcy before the dynasty settled into the status of a loyal princely state. Travancore remained a princely state under the British Raj until it acceded to independent India, its rulers finally merging the state into the Indian Union in 1949.
yearDesc: In 1800, Travancore was ruled by the young Maharaja Balarama Varma, who had ascended the throne in February 1798 as a teenager. In 1800, effective administrative power lay largely with the Dalawa (chief minister), with Velu Thampi rising to prominence at court amid the intrigues of the young ruler's minority. In 1800, the kingdom was bound to the British East India Company by the subsidiary treaty concluded in 1795, obliging it to maintain a Company force and pay a subsidy. In 1800, the seat of government was Thiruvananthapuram, to which the capital had been transferred from Padmanabhapuram in 1795, though the older fortified palace remained an important residence. In 1800, mounting subsidy arrears and friction with the Company Resident were building the tensions that would culminate in the fuller Subsidiary Alliance of 1805 and Velu Thampi's revolt at the decade's end.
issues: Verdict "corrected": political history, founding (1729, Marthanda Varma from Venad), Battle of Colachel (1741), rulers (Dharma Raja, Balarama Varma who ascended 17/18 Feb 1798 aged ~16), span (1729–1949), capital (Thiruvananthapuram, moved from Padmanabhapuram in 1795), and coordinates (record 76.95E/8.52N vs actual 76.937E/8.524N, within ±0.5°) all CONFIRMED against Wikipedia. description = 5 sentences, no geography clause, no pop/area/GDP numbers — valid, kept unchanged. pop/area/gdp correctly DASHED (earliest census ~1816–1820; no individual princely-state GDP series) — kept. CORRECTIONS to yearDesc (sentences 2 and 5): the original asserted Velu Thampi was "appointed the previous year [1799]" as Dalawa, but sources disagree on his appointment date (Wikipedia/Grokipedia variously give 1799 and 1802 for his tenure as Dalawa), so I softened this to "rising to prominence at court" rather than asserting a contested 1799 appointment as fact. Also clarified the subsidiary-alliance framing: a subsidiary treaty of 1795 is well-attested (and used by Wikipedia's own infobox), but the firm/full Subsidiary Alliance placing Travancore under British control was the 1805 Treaty of Perpetual Friendship — sentence 5 now notes the 1805 culmination so the 1795 figure isn't over-claimed. officialName kept. No fabricated facts added.
